[removes the two washers from on top of the nuts] Build Your Own Seismograph With a few materials and some time, you can build your own seismograph. To do this activity... You need to understand how a seismograph works. A typical seismograph works in a very simple way: - A heavy weight is fastened to a horizontal rod as shown in the diagram. - This rod hangs from a pole and is free to swing from side to side when the ground shakes. - At the other end of the rod (away from the pole) is an ink pen, and directly underneath the pen is a piece of paper rolled around a - This cylinder rotates so that the pen continuously draws an ink line along the moving paper. If the ground does not move, the rod does not swing, and the pen stays in place, so the ink line is smooth and straight. If the ground shakes, however, the row swings and so the pen draws a zigzag line as the paper turns. The stronger the shaking, the sharper the zigzags. This zigzag picture made on the paper roll is called a seismogram. It is also helpful to read about seismology. You can go to this list of references from the University of California Seismological Laboratory (use "back" to return). You will also need some materials and a place to build your seismograph. Ever since people first became curious about earthquakes, they have tried to design some kind of seismograph. How would you make one? Project 1: Your Own Design a) Think of a creative but effective way to measure the seismic waves (shock waves) from an earthquake. Draw a clear diagram that shows and labels all parts. Then write a paragraph explaining how your design works. A good design would be... b) Gather the materials you need and build the seismograph you designed. Be prepared to show other students how your device works. - 1)... made of common inexpensive materials found in a local store; - 2)... able to determine the relative magnitude (size) of each vibration it - 3)... able to measure vibrations continuously for at least one minute; - 4)... able to measure even slight vibrations (such as a person jumping up and down next to your seismograph). Project 2: Try This Recipe Here is a simple seismograph you can build: This seismograph can be built will some simple materials. Dimensions are approximate -- you might be able to vary them or even modify the design. Build the seismograph according to the diagram. Here are some important steps, and/or modifications you may need to consider: - Wood base (10 in x 24 in x 0.5 inch) - Wood stand (2 in x 4 in x 12 in) - Two wood support blocks (2 in x 4 in x 8 in) - Wood beam (1 in x 1 in x 20 in) - Two wood dowels (diameter = 0.25 in, length = 10 in ) (1 dowel must slide through center of adding machine paper roll) - Brick or other compact heavy weight - Strong wire, or non-elastic thick rope or twine (7 feet) - Roll of adding machine paper (width >= 2 in) - Smooth-sided can with lid and base ( similar dimensions as paper roll) - Round-headed screw or bolt or nail (1 in long) - Various sizes of nails - Masking tape, strapping tape, or duct tape - If you have a drill, you can mount the two wood dowels in holes drilled through the two wood support blocks. If you do not have a drill, saw two slots into the upper edge of each wood support block and then drop the dowel into these - The pen must write easily when pulled down onto the paper roll by the brick. Cap it when not in use. You may try substituting a soft lead pencil, a charcoal pencil, a crayon, etc. - Fasten the wood stand securely to the wood base by nailing upward from underneath the wood base. The wire (or cord) attached to the back and side of the wood stand will help to keep it stable. - Fasten the screw securely into one end of the wood beam . This screw will rest against the wood stand so that the wood beam hangs level over the base. To help the screw stay in place and not slip off of the wood stand , drill or carve out of the stand a small hole 1/4-inch deep and slightly larger in diameter than the screw head. The head of the screw can sit in this hole and press against the wood stand. - Both the adding machine paper roll and the smooth can should be able to rotate. The smooth can rotates when you crank the nail at one end of its wood dowel; the paper roll can turn either with its wood dowel or separately from the dowel. Tape down the end of the paper roll onto the can so that when you crank the can, the paper should wind up around the can and cause the paper roll to rotate as it feeds paper to the can. - If you want to be creative, see if you can devise a way to make the paper roll feed onto the smooth can automatically so that you do not have to crank the can by hand. You might try buying a miniature battery-operated motor that can turn the wood dowel of the can. oh, oh wait you forgot look! First he washes his face and hands. Object-oriented programming languages place data and "functions" that change data into a single unit. This unit is called an "object". Objects can interact with each other and change another object's data. This is usually called encapsulation or information hiding. Most modern programming languages are object-oriented, or at least allow this style of programming. Examples of this are Java, Python, Ruby, C++, C# and other C languages. Today, we're going to learn about something called "histograms," which are super cool tools to help us understand our data better. Imagine you have collected information on your favorite subject - it could be anything like the number of pets in your class, the heights of plants in your garden, or even the ages of people in your family! With so many numbers, it becomes challenging to make sense of all this information. That's where histograms come into play! They help us visualize these numbers by organizing them into groups or "bins." Now, let me introduce you to the `HISTBIN` function that helps create those bins. It has three main ingredients: X, N, and k. Think of X as your collection of numbers, N as the number of equal-sized boxes you want to divide your numbers into, and k as the specific box you would like to know more about. You also get to choose between three types of values for each box: its lower limit, upper limit, or center. Let's dive deeper into understanding these parts with some real-life examples! Example 1: Weighing Apples Say you weigh 15 apples and record their weights in grams. Your X list looks something like this: {127, 146, 139, 128, 141, 133, 132, 145, 129, 130, 135, 148, 126, 138, 131}. To organize these weights using `HISTBIN`, first decide how many equally sized boxes (or bins) you need, say N=4. So now you have four weight ranges to categorize your apples. Next, pick any one box, let's take the second one (k=2); maybe it covers the range of 135-144 g. Now, apply the `HISTBIN` magic: * Lower Limit: This tells you the starting point of the selected box. In our case, max(apples)-(bin count)*bin size => 148-(4\*11)=122 g. This means any apple weighing 122 g or higher goes into the considered box. * Upper Limit: And guess what? This gives you the ending point of the chosen box. Using similar logic, you find out that everything up to 134 g falls into the same category. * Center Value: Finally, curious minds like yours may wonder about the average weight inside this special box. Well, just calculate the middle of the box limits, i.e., (122+134)/2 = 128 g. Ta-da! So, there you go! Play around with different numbers of boxes and discover new insights about your wonderful collections! Remember, practice makes perfect, so grab your friends, gather interesting facts and figures, and explore together! Happy analyzing :) yeah, let's see if we can untangle 'em. Title: How to Call Back an HP Battery Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of call backing an HP battery. This technique is often used when an HP laptop's battery isn't charging properly or showing incorrect information. By performing a call back, you can reset the battery firmware and potentially resolve any issues. Before proceeding with this tutorial, ensure that your HP laptop has an internal battery (not removable). Please note that this procedure may void your warranty; perform at your own risk. I. Preparation Step 1: Save Your Work Always save all open documents and close applications before starting. Restart your computer if necessary. Key tip: Make sure your laptop is connected to AC power during the entire process. II. Identify Your Battery Model Step 2: Open Device Manager Press `Win + X` keys together and select 'Device Manager'. Step 3: Locate Batteries Section Expand the 'Batteries' category by clicking on the arrow next to it. You should see two entries - Microsoft ACPI-Compliant Control Method Battery and one entry related to your specific battery model (e.g., HP Notebook PC Battery). III. Obtain Battery Information Step 4: Find Battery Details Right-click on the HP battery entry and choose 'Properties'. Go to the 'Details' tab. Step 5: Get Serial Number & Manufacturing Date From the Property dropdown list, select 'Battery Serial Number'. Note down the serial number. Next, find the manufacturing date using the same method but selecting 'Power Data' instead. The format might look like YYWW, where YY represents the year and WW stands for the week of manufacture. IV. Download HP Battery Recall Verification Tool Step 6: Visit HP Support Website Navigate to <https://support.hp.com/> in your web browser. Step 7: Search for Battery Recall Verification Tool Type "HP Battery Recall Verification" into the search bar and press Enter. Click on the first result from the sorted options. Step 8: Download Software Click on the 'Download Now' button under the appropriate operating system section. Once downloaded, install the software following the provided prompts. V. Run HP Battery Recall Verification Tool Step 9: Launch Application Open the installed HP Battery Recall Verification tool from its shortcut or via Start Menu. Step 10: Input Required Information Enter the previously noted serial number and manufacturing date. If prompted about external batteries, click 'No.' VI. Perform Callback Step 11: Review Results Check whether the tool detects any recalls or warnings regarding your battery. Follow the recommended actions accordingly. Otherwise, continue to Step 12. Step 12: Initiate Callback Process Select 'Run Battery Checkup'. A Command Prompt window will appear briefly while the callback operation takes place. Step 13: Close Applications Once completed, exit the application and restart your computer. VII. Verify Correct Functionality Step 14: Monitor Charging Behavior After rebooting, check if the battery charges correctly and displays accurate capacity levels. It may take several cycles for full recovery. Conclusion: In most cases, calling back an HP battery resolves charging problems without needing replacements. However, if issues persist after attempting these steps, consider contacting HP support or consulting a professional technician. Title: How to Delete a Playlist in Apple Music Apple Music is a popular music streaming service that allows users to create and manage their own playlists. However, there might come a time when you want to delete a playlist that you no longer need or want. This tutorial will provide a detailed guide on how to delete a playlist in Apple Music, with clear explanations for each step and additional tips to help you along the way. **Key Tips and Guidelines:** * Make sure you are signed in to your Apple Music account before attempting to delete a playlist. * Deleting a playlist is permanent and cannot be undone, so double-check that you really want to remove it before proceeding. * If you only wish to hide a playlist from view without deleting it, consider using the 'Hide' option instead (available on macOS). 1. **Open the Apple Music app.** The first step is to launch the Apple Music application on your device. You can find this app preinstalled on iOS devices, Mac computers running macOS, and Windows PCs with iTunes installed. 2. **Navigate to the Library tab.** Once the Apple Music app is open, locate and tap on the 'Library' tab at the bottom of the screen (iOS) or in the sidebar (macOS and Windows). This section contains all of your saved music, including playlists. 3. **Select Playlists.** Underneath the 'Library' heading, look for the 'Playlists' category and give it a tap. This action will display a list of all the playlists you have created within Apple Music. 4. **Locate the playlist you want to delete.** Scroll through your list of playlists until you find the one you wish to remove. Take note of its name and contents to ensure you have selected the correct playlist. 5. **Swipe left on the playlist name (iOS) or right-click on the playlist title (macOS and Windows).** Performing this gesture reveals additional options related to the selected playlist. On iOS devices, swiping left will bring up a red 'Delete' button; while on macOS and Windows systems, right-clicking will present a context menu containing various actions, one of which is 'Delete'. 6. **Tap Delete (iOS) or click Delete Playlist (macOS and Windows).** To confirm deletion, select either the 'Delete' button that appeared after swiping left on an iOS device or the 'Delete Playlist' option from the context menu on macOS and Windows systems. A confirmation window may appear asking if you are certain about removing the playlist - choose 'Delete Playlist' again to finalize the process. 7. **Verify that the playlist has been deleted.** After completing these steps, go back to the main 'Playlists' page to verify whether the unwanted playlist has indeed been removed. If it still appears in the list, try repeating the previous steps carefully, ensuring you have chosen the correct playlist for deletion. In conclusion, deleting a playlist in Apple Music involves navigating to the appropriate location, selecting the playlist, revealing the hidden delete option, and then confirming your decision. By following these simple yet detailed instructions, you should now be able to successfully delete any unwanted playlists within Apple Music, freeing up space and decluttering your library. Title: How to Construct a Research Paper Introduction: A research paper is an extended written presentation that provides evidence-based analysis or argument about a specific topic. Writing a research paper involves several steps, from selecting a topic and conducting research, to outlining, writing, revising, and proofreading your work. This wikiHow will guide you through the process with clear, detailed instructions and helpful tips along the way. I. Selecting a Topic 1. Choose a subject area that interests you: Consider what courses you are taking, areas where you excel, or subjects related to your major or career goals. By choosing something interesting, you'll be more motivated during the research and writing stages. 2. Narrow down your focus: A general topic can lead to overwhelming amounts of information, making it challenging to write concisely. Instead, select a particular aspect within the broader field. For example, instead of focusing on 'climate change,' narrow your scope by exploring its impact on agriculture in one country. 3. Consult with instructors or professionals: If unsure whether your chosen topic has enough substance, seek advice from professors, teachers, or industry experts who can provide valuable insights into your selected theme. II. Conduct Preliminary Research 1. Search online databases and libraries: Begin gathering background knowledge using academic search engines such as Google Scholar, JSTOR, or university library catalogs. Read widely but avoid getting lost in irrelevant sources. 2. Evaluate credibility: Not all resources found online are trustworthy. Verify websites' authority, accuracy, currency, relevance, and objectivity before citing them. Generally speaking, peer-reviewed articles published in reputable journals offer high-quality information. 3. Take notes meticulously: Record bibliographic details (author(s), title, publication date, etc.) and summarize essential points from every source consulted. Use note-taking software like Evernote or OneNote or traditional pen and paper methods. Keep track of quotes verbatim if intending to include direct citations. III. Develop Your Thesis Statement 1. Identify main arguments: Based on your preliminary research, identify central ideas or claims that have emerged regarding your chosen topic. These form the basis of your thesis statement. 2. Craft a strong claim: Formulate a single sentence encapsulating your primary stance or conclusion derived from analyzed data. Remember, this should represent a debatable point rather than an obvious fact. 3. Refine throughout the process: As you continue delving deeper into your study, refine your thesis accordingly. It may evolve over time based on new findings or perspectives uncovered during extensive reading and thinking. IV. Create an Outline 1. Organize sections logically: Divide your research paper into three primary parts - Introduction, Body, and Conclusion. Each part serves different purposes, requiring distinct types of content. * Introduction: Introduce the topic, engage the reader, establish context, present the significance of the issue being addressed, and end with a well-crafted thesis statement. * Body: Present multiple paragraphs, each dedicated to supporting aspects of your thesis with solid evidence drawn from reliable sources. Arrange these segments coherently so they flow naturally from one idea to another while maintaining topical unity. If the ice cream man trucks come today can I have something? The date for the launch of Firefox 3 was June 17, 2008, named "Download Day 2008." Firefox was aiming to set the record for most Firefox downloads in 24 hours. As of June 18, 2008, more than 6.88 million people have downloaded Firefox 3. From Jonathan Gardner's Korean Notebook Phonology refers to the sounds a language makes. Often times, it bears little resemblance to the writing system. Those of you who know English and who can pronounce it correctly know this to be true. I will introduce the Korean Phonology from my perspective, which is probably different than what you learned before. Basics of Phonology Koreans, like every language, distinguish between consonants and vowels. Consonants are sounds like 'k' and 'd', and vowels are the sounds in between, like 'a' and 'oo'. The Korean consonants are organized rather differently than English and many other languages. Once you understand the Korean consonant system, you will see that other languages use parts of it, even though they don't realize it. If you look at your mouth, you will notice that your tongue makes different shapes when it pronounces the consonants. Try right now making the following sounds, and note what your tongue and mouth do: - 'p', 'f', 'th','t', 'k', 'ng' You'll note that you started with your lips together ('p'), then you touched your lips to your teeth ('f'), and then you touched your tongue to your teeth ('th'), then your tongue to gums immediately behind the teeth ('t'), followed by the middle of your tongue to the top of your mouth ('k'), and then finally, the back of your tongue to the back of your throat. This is what I will refer to as "mouth position". Many letters in English share the same mouth position, so mouth position is not enough information about what consonant is being made. Voiced v. Unvoiced? In English, we distinguish 't' and 'd', which have the same mouth position, by listening whether one is voiced or not. Feel your throat, your vocal chords, with your fingers when you say "t" versus "d". Note that 'd' is voiced, meaning, the vocal chords are vibrating. The 't' sound, however, is unvoiced. I tell you this because in Korean, speakers and listeners do not distinguish between voiced and unvoiced sounds. It doesn't matter to them. The sound for ' ' is sometimes voiced, and sometimes unvoiced. They don't care, it's the same sound. If you're wondering, consonants in the beginning of a word tend to be unvoiced, and those in the middle tend to be unvoiced, although in reality they are somewhere in between, never quite reaching the extremes we have for 't' and 'd'. Koreans do, however, distinguish between aspirated and non-aspirated consonants. Make the shape of 't', and say it with an airy, raspy voice. You should almost be spitting. That's an aspirated 't', or ' '. Now, say the same sound, but try to say it so that a piece of paper hanging in front of your mouth only moves slightly. That's non-aspirated 't', or the ' ' sound. In English, the 't' sound is sometimes aspirated, and sometimes not, and we don't care which you use. Some words prefer aspiration, some do not, but who cares anyway? In Korean, aspiration is a critical discriminating factor. You'll have to learn to listen for it, and learn to say it. Stops / Double Consonants In English, we have lots of words with double consonants, and it doesn't matter to us. Take the word "hat" and put it next to "top", and say "hattop". In English, those two words just share the 't' sound to make something like "hatop". In Korean, when you double a consonant, the air and sound have to come to a complete stop. This is represented with the ' ', which is called ssang ( ), which means "twin" or "double". Words that start with ssang consonants must stop everything before making their sound. This produces a very hard, very interruptive sound, almost like you are a stutterer who is finally able to make his words come out. In the middle of a word, it is Some of the "softer" consonants also double. These are harder to pronounce, and sometimes Koreans have a harder time distinguishing them. Thankfully, it is rare indeed that these make a difference in the word. The way to pronounce these double-consonants is to hold the consonants with a little more force and a little longer than you otherwise would. Or, in other words, have the single-consonants form a slightly softer and quicker sound than you would in English. With this introduction, the consonants in Korean are ready to be introduced. I've provided the names for them, so you will know what to call them when someone tries to correct your pronunciation. |p/b|| ( )|| ( )|| ( )|| ( )| |t/d|| ( )|| ( )|| ( )|| ( )| |k/g|| ( )|| ( )|| ( )|| ( )| There are some miscellaneous consonants as well, that don't categorize in one of the above groups. |s|| ( ), ( )| |ch/j|| ( ), ( ), ( )| Velars (top of the mouth): , , The first group of words is the k/g sounds. Since it is aspirated, you should be moving a lot of air when you say it. The 'k' sound in English usually gets interpreted as a so be aware that that's what you're starting at as an English speaker. is a very gentle, almost subtle k/g sound. Ask a Korean to try the tongue-twister "She sells seashells at the seashore" and you'll hear "She sells shesells at the shesore." That is, unless they've trained themselves to pronounce things in English properly. Because the mouth shape is close to , the consonant sounds the same when it is final. Practice: , , , , , , , , . Of all the sounds in Korean, this is the one that drives English speakers crazy. It is the one consonant that will tell Korean speakers right away whether you have a thick foreign accent or not. This sound manifests itself in four distinct ways. Note that in each of these cases, the tongue is behind where it would be for 'd' in English, even a little bit more behind where it would be for . Case #1: Initially, it may sound like and 'l' or and 'r'. In "true" Korean, no words start with , so this is a moot point. If you force a Korean to say a word starting with , they will most likely make an sound as in case #3. But sometimes they will make the sound as in Case #2. In the many words they have borrowed from Chinese that start with this sound, they simply drop the initial consonant rather than force themselves to say it. That's why the "Lee" family is pronounced "Ee". Case #2: In the middle of a word, a single will make a very light flap like an 'r'. Think of the way people may trill the 'r', especially in Spanish. Now do that exactly once instead of 3-5 times like the Spanish do. You have . Another way to think of this is the lightest 'd' you have ever said in your life. Case #3: When you have 2 in the middle of a word, then you get an 'l' sound. This is lighter and quicker than the 'l' we have in English, but it is nonetheless entirely distinct from a single . Case #4: When you have an at the end of a word, it forms the shape of an 'l', and then holds that shape after all voicing has stopped. In NO CASE does the every make the "dirty" vowel sound you hear in American English, such as with "hard" or "cord". If you are doing this, you sound like a foreigner. Practice: , , , . When Koreans put two consonants together, they allow no room at all for a vowel to mitigate the difference. In order to pronounce the combination properly, end the first syllable with the shape of the final consonant, then pronounce the second consonant without allowing any space in between. Often this implies a change in pronunciation. Consult the following chart to see how this works in practice. - Be very careful not to make the (ng) sound here. Korean vowels are very precise. In fact, if you get the vowels of the word right, it almost doesn't matter what consonants you have used, Koreans will understand you. Korean vowels do not shift based on how the word is used. Any changes in vowels due to grammatical changes are well-defined and precise. If you want to sound like a Korean, first master your vowels. One note: A lot of English vowels are actually two vowels put together. Korean vowels are pure, meaning, you do not start with one vowel and end with another. For instance, in English, the long "o" is actually two vowels: "o" plus "oo". Note that Koreans think of some vowels ( , ) as "bright" and the rest ( , , , ) as "dark". You'll see why this is important as we get into grammar. The first vowel is a very pure, very clean, 'ah' sound. It is the sound your make when you open your mouth at the dentist. Open your throat, open your mouth, move your tongue down, and let your voice sing in a beautiful, pure way. is considered "bright". If you start with , move your tongue forward, and close your mouth slightly, you will produce the sound that seems to be unique to Korean. This is the vowel that most Americans mess up on, and it is the reason why Korean is considered hard. Master this vowel, and keep it distinct from , and you will be able to learn Korean quickly. is considered "dark". Open your mouth as for , but then close your lips into a tight "o" shape. Let your bass voice sing, and let your mouth resonate with all the rich goodness that this vowel possesses. Don't be afraid of making it to deep or too reverberant. The more, the better. is considered "bright". Make a circle with your lips, and stick your lips out all the way. This is the "oo" sound of . is considered "dark". This is a vowel that English speakers struggle with, although I cannot see why. We use it all the time. Think of the sound you make when you don't care what the vowel is. This is the sound you make for "a" and "the" when you are trying to say something. This is the sound. Close your mouth, move your tongue forward, keeping the back of the tongue down. Open your lips about 1/4 of the way, and relax. This is the vowel. Another way to think of it is to imagine you are paralyzed but trying to say "ee". Start with "ee", and relax, relax, relax, relax. is considered "dark". This is between "ee" and "i". Open your mouth a bit. Lift the back of your tongue, almost like you are going to make a "k" sound. American speakers tend to choose "ee" or "i" and stick with it, while Korean speakers are very consistent with their sound. I suggest thinking of it as one or the other, and then imagining that the Koreans simply pronounce that vowel slightly differently. In Korean, you can combine vowel sounds to make new sounds. Korean is somewhat unique among languages because they do not use accents, or even tones like Japanese and Chinese. They speak like a machine gun. However, there is some intonation in phrases and sentences. This is usually represented with slight variations in pitch. In between phrases, Koreans will take a small break. However, they do not vary the tempo of their speech. Intonation is also heavily influenced on where the speaker originates from. Speakers from the southwest area of South Korea, Pusan and Kyoungsan Province, speaking with exaggerated variations. To speakers in other regions, this sounds combative and emotional. Speakers in Seoul try to speak calmly and almost in a robot-like fashion. Speakers in other regions also have certain characteristics you will see. One area is know for speaking very, very slowly. The various regions are remarkably consistent in their pronunciation. You shouldn't have a hard time understanding what other regions are saying, especially if you have a moderate ability in Korean to begin with. Some regions vary the vowels by quite a bit. However, they are remarkably consistent, and understanding them is no different than understanding someone with a speech impediment. - Practice making the sounds above. Check with a native Korean speaker to see if you are doing it correctly. - Listen to a Korean speaker making the sounds. - Try to discriminate the sounds one from another. Focus on the problem areas for English speakers: - Aspirated vs. normal vs. stops. - The different vowels and combinations. - Try to mimic a Korean speaker. [as MEC is turned away from activity] Title: How to Accept Praise Gracefully Introduction: Learning to accept praise gracefully is an essential social skill that can help build self-confidence, strengthen relationships, and foster a positive attitude. It might seem like a simple task, but many people struggle with graciously accepting compliments or recognition due to various reasons such as humility, embarrassment, or fear of being perceived as arrogant. This tutorial will guide you through the process of accepting praise with confidence and poise. Step 1: Acknowledge the Praise When someone praises you, acknowledge their kind words by making eye contact, smiling, and saying thank you. Avoid downplaying your achievements or brushing off the compliment, as this may come across as disrespectful or unappreciative. Instead, simply say, "Thank you, I appreciate your kind words." Key Tips: * Make direct eye contact when acknowledging praise to show genuine appreciation. * Smiling demonstrates warmth and openness towards the person offering praise. * Saying 'thank you' shows gratitude and acceptance of the compliment. Guidelines: Practice acknowledging praise from friends, family members, or coworkers until it becomes second nature. Remember, there is no need to feel embarrassed or uncomfortable; instead, focus on the positivity surrounding the situation. Step 2: Show Gratitude Express sincere gratitude for the praise received. You can do so by elaborating briefly on what led to the achievement or success being praised. For example, if someone commends you on completing a project ahead of schedule, share some insights about your work ethic, time management skills, or team collaboration efforts. Key Tips: * Be specific when expressing gratitude - mentioning details reinforces the value of the praise and highlights your dedication. * Keep your response concise yet meaningful, avoiding lengthy stories or boasting about yourself. Guidelines: Showing gratitude allows both parties involved to connect over shared experiences while fostering mutual respect and admiration. Step 3: Return the Compliment (Optional) If appropriate, return the compliment by recognizing something positive about the individual who offered praise. This reciprocal act creates balance within the conversation and strengthens interpersonal bonds. Key Tips: * Authenticity is crucial when returning a compliment - ensure your words are heartfelt and relevant. * Refrain from using insincere flattery or exaggerated praise, which could damage trust and rapport between you two. Guidelines: Returning the compliment should be done sparingly and genuinely, focusing on qualities or actions that truly resonate with you. Step 4: Move On Once you have acknowledged, expressed gratitude, and possibly returned the compliment, move forward naturally without dwelling too much on the praise. Prolonged discussions about personal accomplishments may create unnecessary attention or give the impression of arrogance. Key Tips: * Maintain a balanced perspective regarding your abilities and achievements. * Focus on others around you, asking questions and engaging them in conversation. Guidelines: Shifting the spotlight away from yourself after receiving praise prevents any misconceptions of vanity or conceit while promoting inclusiveness and camaraderie. Conclusion: Mastering the art of accepting praise gracefully involves practice, patience, and mindfulness. By following these steps, you can develop strong communication skills, cultivate healthy relationships, and boost your overall sense of self-worth. So go ahead, embrace those well-deserved accolades with confidence and poise! I like this meatloaf but Sharon's meatloaf is so different. [as In places pitcher to her mouth] Title: How to Get a Guy and Stay with Him Introduction: This guide is designed to help women who are seeking a serious relationship with a guy. It outlines steps to attract a man, build a strong connection, and maintain a healthy, lasting partnership. This article assumes that the reader is respectful of others' feelings, boundaries, and personal values. Always remember, every individual is unique, so adapt these suggestions as needed. Section 1: Attracting a Guy Step 1: Know Yourself Before you start looking for a partner, understand your own needs, desires, strengths, weaknesses, interests, and goals. Self-awareness will enable you to find someone compatible with your lifestyle and personality. * Key tip: Journal or reflect regularly to develop self-understanding. Step 2: Be Authentic Present yourself honestly and genuinely. Pretending to be someone else may initially catch a guy's attention but being true to yourself builds trust and fosters genuine connections. * Guideline: Avoid exaggerating or lying about yourself; honesty leads to healthier relationships. Step 3: Improve Communication Skills Practice active listening and clear communication. Show interest when he talks by maintaining eye contact and asking questions. Clearly express your thoughts without dominating conversations. * Key tip: Practice empathy - try understanding his perspective before responding. Section 2: Building Connection Step 4: Find Common Interests Engage in activities where you can meet potential partners with similar hobbies and passions. Shared experiences strengthen bonds between individuals. * Guideline: Join clubs, attend social events, volunteer, or take classes related to your interests. Step 5: Establish Trust Gradually share more intimate details about yourself while encouraging him to do the same. Honesty creates an environment conducive to building mutual trust. * Key tip: Respect his privacy and expect the same level of discretion from him. Step 6: Spend Quality Time Together Regularly schedule one-on-one time to deepen your bond. Engaging in new experiences together allows both parties to learn and grow alongside each other. * Guideline: Balance group activities with solo dates to foster intimacy. Section 3: Maintaining Relationship Health Step 7: Express Gratitude Consistently thank him for his efforts and support. Recognizing his contributions reinforces positivity within the relationship. * Key tip: Small gestures like leaving notes or verbal appreciation go a long way. Step 8: Manage Conflict Constructively Disagreements are inevitable. Instead of avoiding arguments, practice effective conflict resolution skills such as remaining calm, actively listening, compromising, and forgiving. * Guideline: Never resort to name calling or insults - they damage trust and weaken relationships. Step 9: Nurture Individual Growth Continuously work towards becoming the best version of yourself. Personal growth enhances self-confidence which reflects positively on the relationship. * Key tip: Encourage each other's ambitions and celebrate achievements. Conclusion: Remember, no two relationships are alike. What works for some might not work for others. Patience, resilience, open-mindedness, and good humor are essential ingredients in any successful partnership. By following this guide, you increase chances of finding a great guy and cultivating a fulfilling, enduring love story. So you can move anyway you want. The Australian Aboriginal people arrived in Australia about 50,000 years ago or even earlier. Until the arrival of British settlers in 1788, the Aboriginal people lived by hunting and gathering food from the land. They lived in all sorts of climates and managed the land in different ways. An example of Aboriginal land management was the Cumberland Plain where Sydney is now. Every few years the Aboriginal people would burn the grass and small trees. This meant that a lot of grass grew back, but not many big trees. Kangaroos like to live on grassy plains, but not in forests. The kangaroos that lived on the plain were a good food supply for the Aboriginal people. Sometimes, Aborigines would name a person after an animal, and they could not eat that animal to help level out the food population. Lenses are curved glasses or transparent materials. The focus of convex lenses is where rays parallel to principal axis go. The focus of the concave lenses is where rays parallel to principal come from. A converging lens looks fat in the middle. A diverging lens looks thin in the middle. So let's talk about lenses, a lens is a curved piece of glass or some other transparent material that we're going to use to focus light and generate some images. So let's see how it goes, suppose that I've got an object out here in air and then I've got a piece of glass that's curved outward like this in a convex way. Alright we always with a lens have a principle axis if light comes in exactly along the principle axis it goes straight through the lens with no deflection whatsoever so that's the principle axis but most of the times if it's a lens it'll have 2 sides the principle axis needs to be perpendicular to both because light needs to go all the way through without being deflected. Alright let's see what some light rays coming from this object do. So we come in like that alright, now at the glass the light is going to refract in order to determine how it's going to refract we have to give ourselves a normal and then we say well this is a more crowded hallway than that so that means that the light is going to bend toward the normal. Alright so that's fine, what about one that comes let's see what about one that comes down like this, well down here our normal looks like this. Remember the normal has to be perpendicular to the lens. So the light is going to bend toward the normal notice what happens the light beams are coming together so this is going to give us light rays that come together and hit somewhere along the principal axis it seems like. So the focus is the place where rays that come in parallel to the principal axis go. So this focus will be right over here, where this hits the principal axis. Alright so this is called converging, when I have convex glass light ray converge after they go through. Alright what happens if I've got a piece of glass that's like this concave well in this case we're going to do the same thing so let's go ahead and draw some light rays. Here's one it's going to come down like this alright and then again we need our normal. Our normal is that way and again we bend toward the normal just like this. Alright now notice this light ray is not going towards the principal axis so something different is going on. Let's give ourselves a light ray that's parallel to the principal axis again I need a normal and I need to bend toward the normal like that so this guy is causing the light rays to diverge. So concave glass is called diverging alright because light rays come in and then they split apart. Alright now notice that this light ray appears to be coming from somewhere like there, that's called the focus of a diverging lens. So for convex the focus is the place where rays parallel to the principal go. For concave the focus is the place where rays parallel to the principal axis appear to be coming from. If I was looking on this side I would see the light coming like that and I would just continue that line straight back and that would be where the focus is. There's dolphins and whales and sharks and. [referring to S's voice on the tape] I can make them [magnet balls] move. 1) What lights a LED up? Voltage or current? 2) What does a resistor limit? Voltage or current? I mean, if I don't put a resistor in my circuit the LED bursts down: why? Too much volts or amperes? This should get you started as far as answering questions 1 and 2: http://www.sparkfun.com/tutorials/219 3) What remains constant in a circuit? Voltage or current? I mean, what is the same at the start and at the end of a circuit? Volts or amperes? It depends on your circuit. I asume that you are familiar with series and parallel circuits. In a series circuit, current is constant (assuming none of your resistors change values due to heat or other factors) and voltage will change from place to place. If you have a battery connected to two resistors (motors, resistors, anything really...) then you can use Ohm's Law to calculate everything about the circuit. Let's say we have a nine volt battery and two 50 resistors. Ohm's Law: Voltage = Current x Resistance We know Voltage (before the battery starts to die) is 9V. We also know the value of both of our resistors in the circuit. In series, resistors simply add their resistances to get a total. 50 + 50 = 100 , so now we have: 9V = I x 100 To find current, divide each side by R: 9V/100 = (I(100 ))/100 0.09 = I So the current is 0.09A, or 90mA. When working with many things currents will be listed in mA, so it's worth it to convert. To find the voltage across a certain resistor you already know that the current (constant in series circuits) is 90mA, and you know the resistance of the part, here 50 . So: V = 0.09A x 50 V = 4.5V The most important thing to get out of this is Ohm's Law. It is used everywhere and flipping it around, you can get almost whatever you want to know. As far as parallel circuits go, you still use Ohm's law, but there are some important differences. Look here and see if you can get them figured out.http://www.allaboutcircuits.com/vol_1/chpt_5/3.html Here is another good source: http://www.electronics-tutorials.ws/dccircuits/dcp_2.html 4) How should I put resistors when I have more than one LED? It will depend on your circuit. If all of the LEDs are coming on at once in series, you could get away with a single resistor calculated to supply the correct amount of current. (This is referred to as a current limiting resistor.) If the lights are independent of each other then you will need a resistor for each LED. 5) How should I put resistors when I have series or parallel circuits? Once you understand the various parts of series and parallel circuits, this will become easy for you to decide. Hope that helps! Can you put your hand in there? Dealing with an irritating school health aide can be challenging, but there are effective strategies you can employ to improve the situation. This guide will provide you with a comprehensive approach to handling this issue while maintaining respect and professionalism. ### Step 1: Identify the Issue Clearly define what makes the health aide irritating. Is it their communication style, lack of empathy, or perhaps unprofessional behavior? Understanding the root cause will help you address the problem more effectively. Key Tip: Be specific about the behaviors that bother you instead of generalizing. ### Step 2: Maintain Professional Composure Always maintain your composure and professionalism when interacting with the health aide. Avoid reacting emotionally or defensively to their actions. By staying calm, you demonstrate maturity and set the stage for constructive conversation. Guideline: Take deep breaths before responding to any provocation from the health aide. ### Step 3: Document Incidents Keep a record of all incidents involving the health aide's irritating behavior. Note down dates, times, witnesses, and details of each occurrence. Having concrete examples will strengthen your case if you need to escalate the matter further. Key Tip: Be objective and factual in your documentation; avoid personal opinions or interpretations. ### Step 4: Communicate Your Concerns Privately Request a private meeting with the health aide where you can openly discuss your concerns without distractions or interruptions. Explain how their behavior impacts you and request changes. Using "I" statements (e.g., "I feel uncomfortable when...") rather than accusatory language ("You always... ") fosters open dialogue. Guideline: Choose a time when both of you are unlikely to be rushed or stressed. ### Step 5: Listen Actively Allow the health aide to respond after sharing your concerns. They may have valid reasons for their actions or misunderstandings that led to the current state of affairs. Active listening involves paying full attention to the speaker, refraining from interrupting, and asking clarifying questions when necessary. Key Tip: Show empathy towards the health aide's perspective even if you disagree. ### Step 6: Seek Common Ground Work together to find solutions that satisfy both parties. Brainstorm alternatives and reach compromises where possible. When suggesting modifications, ensure they align with school policies and procedures. Guideline: Consider offering suggestions based on mutual interests, such as improving student wellbeing or creating a harmonious work environment. ### Step 7: Establish Boundaries We call her Haylie or Haylie bug. yeah, and you know those little um like little round things then you go around in a little plane and... Have you ever wondered how to organize and understand large sets of numbers? Well, today I'm going to teach you about a really cool math tool called a "stem and leaf diagram." It's like a fancy way of sorting numbers and seeing patterns. Imagine you have a bunch of test scores from your classmates. You want to know how everyone did and maybe even find the highest and lowest scores. A stem and leaf diagram can help you do just that! First, let's talk about what makes up a stem and leaf diagram. Basically, we take each number and break it into two parts - the stem and the leaf. The stem is the first part of the number, while the leaf is the last digit. For example, in the number 15, the stem would be 1 and the leaf would be 5. Easy peasy! Now, let's see how this looks with our test scores. We write down all the stems together, one after another, starting from the smallest number. Then, next to those stems, we list the leaves in order from smallest to largest. This way, we create groups of scores that make it easier to read and compare them. Let's say some of your classmate's scores were 82, 76, 90, 85, 78, and 92. To create a stem and leaf plot, follow these steps: 1. Write down the tens digits (the stems): 7 8 8 9 9 2. Next to each stem, put the ones digits (the leaves), still sorted smallest to largest: * For the 7 stem, add the leaves: 2 6 * For the 8 stem, add the leaves: 2 5 6 I think you height look toward the light. Title: How to Get Over a Scary Movie You Saw ============================================= Scary movies can be an exhilarating form of entertainment, but they can also leave you feeling frightened or anxious long after the credits roll. If you've recently watched a horror film that has left you feeling unsettled, this step-by-step guide will help you overcome your fears and get back to enjoying your everyday life. ### Step 1: Acknowledge Your Fear *Estimated time: 5 minutes* Recognize that it is normal to feel scared after watching a horror movie. Allow yourself to acknowledge these feelings without judgment. By acknowledging your fear, you take the first step towards addressing it and finding ways to move past it. #### Key Tips * Be honest with yourself about how the movie made you feel. * Understand that everyone reacts differently to scary movies. ### Step 2: Share Your Feelings With Someone Trustworthy *Estimated time: 10-30 minutes* Talking about your fears with someone close to you can help alleviate some of the anxiety associated with them. This person might provide reassurance, distraction, or even helpful suggestions for coping strategies. #### Guidelines * Choose someone who listens well and offers support. * Explain what aspects of the movie were particularly frightening for you. * Discuss any specific scenes or elements that continue to haunt you. ### Step 3: Engage in Calming Activities *Estimated time: Variable* Participate in activities that promote relaxation and reduce stress levels. These activities can serve as both distractions from your fears and soothing mechanisms to help calm your nerves. #### Suggestions * Listen to calming music or podcasts. * Practice deep breathing exercises or meditation. * Read a book or engage in another leisurely activity. ### Step 4: Limit Exposure to Reminders of the Film Mom need some bags get some toys. We expect them to perform and cut with ease, but we rarely pay any more attention to our knives than that. To understand your knife, it is important to learn about its construction and be able to identify the various knife parts. First, it is easy to divide the knife into two main parts, the handle and the blade. But each of those two parts can also be subdivided into its own parts. With the help of the photo at right and the descriptions below, you will better understand what components make up your knife. Point - The point is the part of the knife where the edge and spine come together. The point is often used for piercing. Tip - The tip is the forward part of the knife and includes the knife point. The tip is used detailed or delicate cutting. Edge - The edge is the cutting part of the blade. It extends from the point to the heel of the knife. Heel - The heel is the rear part of the edge, opposite the point. Spine - The spine is the top of the knife blade, opposite the knife edge. Bolster - The bolster is the band that joins the blade of the knife to its handle. The bolster provides balance for the knife and also helps to protect the hand from getting in the way of the knife edge. Tang - The tang is the part of the blade that extends into the handle of the knife. It is the surface to which the handle attaches to the blade. Scales - The scales are the part of the knife that creates the handle. Scales are often made of synthetic material or wood. Two scales are typically attached to the tang with rivets. Rivets - The rivets are metal pins used to join the scales to the tang to form the handle. Butt - The butt is the end of the handle of the knife. Title: How to Download Snapchat on Your iPhone Introduction: Snapchat is a popular multimedia messaging app that allows you to send messages, photos, and videos that disappear after they're viewed. This tutorial will guide you through downloading and installing Snapchat on your iPhone, so you can start snapping with friends and family. Step 1: Check Compatibility Before proceeding, make sure your device meets the minimum requirements: - An iPhone running iOS version 10 or later. - A stable internet connection (WiFi or cellular data). Key tip: Always keep your iPhone updated to ensure optimal performance and security. Updating also guarantees compatibility with new features and bug fixes from Snapchat developers. Step 2: Open App Store Tap on the App Store icon, which should be preinstalled on your home screen. The blue circle with a white 'A' inside is hard to miss! Step 3: Search Functionality Locate the search bar at the bottom of the screen; this is where you type in what application you are looking for. It has a magnifying glass as its symbol. Step 4: Type "Snapchat" In the search bar, enter "Snapchat." Make sure there are no typos since autocorrect may lead you astray. As you type, several suggestions will appear below - select "Snapchat" when it does. Step 5: Find Official Snapchat App From the list of apps presented, look for the official Snapchat app developed by Snap Inc., denoted by their yellow ghost logo. Be cautious about any other similar-looking apps that might attempt to mimic or deceive users into downloading fraudulent versions. Step 6: Installation Process Once you have found the correct app, tap the "Get" button next to it. If prompted, verify your Apple ID password or Touch ID / Face ID to authorize the installation process. Wait patiently while the app downloads and installs automatically onto your iPhone. Key Guideline: Keep your phone connected to WiFi or cellular network throughout the entirety of the download & installation process. Interrupting the flow could result in errors or slowdowns during the setup procedure. Step 7: Access Snapchat After successful completion, find the newly installed Snapchat app among your home screens. Typically, recently added apps appear at the end of the last page or within the App Library if enabled. Tap on the yellow square icon featuring a white ghost to launch the app. Congratulations! You have now successfully downloaded and installed Snapchat on your iPhone. Create your account, personalize your profile, add friends, and begin exploring all that Snapchat offers! Title: How to Add a Member to a GroupMe Chat on Your Computer (PC or Mac) GroupMe is a popular group messaging app that allows users to create and participate in conversations with multiple people at once. While the app is primarily designed for mobile devices, you can also access its features through a web browser on your computer. This tutorial will guide you through the process of adding a new member to an existing GroupMe chat using either a PC or a Mac. **Step 1: Open a Web Browser and Go to GroupMe's Website** First, launch your preferred web browser (Google Chrome, Mozilla Firefox, Safari, Microsoft Edge, etc.) on your PC or Mac. Next, navigate to the official GroupMe website by typing "[www.groupme.com](http://www.groupme.com)" into the address bar and pressing Enter. Accessing the site via a desktop browser enables easier navigation compared to using a smartphone touchscreen interface. **Step 2: Sign In to Your Account** Before proceeding further, ensure you have signed up for a GroupMe account if you haven't already done so. Once logged in, click on the "Sign in" button located near the top right corner of the page. Then enter your email address, phone number, or username along with your password associated with your GroupMe account. After filling out these details correctly, press the blue "Sign in" button. **Step 3: Navigate to Your Chats List** After signing in successfully, locate the "Chats" option situated at the left-hand side panel. Clicking this link displays all the groups and chats you are currently part of within the application. From here, select the specific group where you wish to add a new member. **Step 4: Open the Group Details Page** Within the chosen group conversation, look towards the upper section of the screen. You should notice three vertical dots arranged together - click them. A dropdown menu containing various options will appear; from this list, choose "Group settings." The subsequent page contains essential information about the selected group, including members' lists, permissions, and other customizable aspects. **Step 5: Locate the 'Members' Section** On the group settings page, scroll down until you find the "Members" heading. Underneath this header, there is usually a search box accompanied by a display area showing current participants in the group. If no such elements exist yet, they might be hidden under another expandable tab - check thoroughly. **Step 6: Invite New Members** To invite someone new, first make sure the focus is on the search box mentioned earlier. Input the name, email address, or phone number of the person you intend to invite. As you type, matching contacts may populate below the input field based on their relevance to what has been entered thus far. Selecting one of those suggested results simplifies things considerably. However, manual entry remains possible if necessary. Finally, hit the "+Invite" button next to the text box. Key Tips & Guidelines: * When entering contact info manually, double-check spelling and formatting accuracy to avoid sending invitations to incorrect addresses or numbers. * Remember that invited individuals must sign up for a GroupMe account before joining the chat if they don't already possess one. * Depending on the privacy level set for the group, only certain users may have permission to add others as members. Check whether you hold sufficient privileges prior to attempting invitation actions. * For optimal user experience while managing groups, consider downloading and utilizing the dedicated GroupMe app available for both iOS and Android platforms. A bar graph can help you quickly compare amounts or quantities by using rectangular bars that are proportional to the values they represent. This tutorial will guide you through the process of creating a basic bar graph in Excel XP, providing detailed explanations for each step along with helpful tips and guidelines. 1. **Prepare your data**: Before creating a bar graph, ensure that your data is organized in a table format with two columns - one for categories (or labels) and another for corresponding numeric values. For example: | Category | Value | |---|---| | Apples | 25 | | Bananas | 30 | | Cherries | 40 | | Durians | 15 | | Elderberries | 20 | *Key tip*: Make sure there are no blank cells within these two columns as this may affect the proper creation of your graph. 2. **Open Microsoft Excel XP**: Launch the program on your computer. If you have multiple versions installed, select 'Excel XP' from the list. 3. **Create a new workbook**: Click on 'File' > 'New' to start a fresh worksheet. You can also press `Ctrl + N` simultaneously. 4. **Input your data**: On the newly opened sheet, enter your categorized data exactly as shown in Step 1 above. Remember to avoid any empty cells within these two columns. 5. **Select the range of data**: Highlight all the cells containing your prepared data by clicking and dragging over them. It should look like the image below but without borders around individual cells: ``` +---+----------+-------+ | | A | B | +---+----------+-------+ | 1 | Category | Value | | 2 | Apples | 25 | | 3 | Bananas | 30 | | 4 | Cherries | 40 | | 5 | Durians | 15 | | 6 | Elder-...| 20 | +---+----------+-------+ ``` *Guideline*: The selected range must include headers ("Category" and "Value") since we want those displayed in our final graph too. 6. **Insert the bar graph**: With the correct cell range still highlighted, navigate to the toolbar and click on 'Insert'. From the dropdown menu, choose 'Column', then 'Clustered Column'. Alternatively, you can press `Alt + I`, followed by `C`, then `O`. ![Image showing location of Clustered Column under Insert tab](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/a/ad/Create-a-Bar-Graph-in-Microsoft-Excel-XP-Step-7.jpg/aid895614-v4- [reaching for the page in the book] O! the agony of those few minutes! The different sounds that the letter"d" takes at the end of a verb in the past tense An extract from the book: Word Power by the author of this article. The English language indicates that the action of the verb is in the past by having some form of the "d" or "t" sound end the word. We say some kind of "d" or "t" sound although the word is almost always written with a "d". Many people who learn English are so confused by the irregular forms of the verbs that they give up and invent their own ways of referring to the past. Some say: "Yesterday I walk to work" or other ways to avoid using the past tense that they have never learned. Sure, there are irregular words in English. The past of teach is taught; the past of buy is bought; the past of think is thought. But even these irregular words end in some kind of a "t" sound to indicate that the verb refers to the past. Luckily, there aren't too many of these irregular verbs. You just have to learn them. The good thing is that they behave more or less the same way. But let's look at the regular verbs. Most English verbs are regular. To indicate the past, they put some kind of a sound made with the tongue touching the back of the upper teeth. Almost always it is the sound of a "d" or of a "t". The ending of the verb "love" in the past: "I loved the movie" is very different from the ending of the verb "walk": "I walked to work." When it sounds like the letter "d", it is a voiced sound, that is the vocal cords vibrate. When it sounds like a "t", it is a voiceless or an unvoiced sound. But how do you know when it should end with a voiced "d" sound and when with a voiceless "t" sound? Although you may not believe it, there is a "rule" that will help you to form the past of most English verbs. You may still make some mistakes but little by little you will feel the mistakes and will correct them. The structure of your mouth will force you to make the right sound. The "rule" for the formation of the past is similar to the "rule" for the "s" at the end of plural nouns and verbs in the third person singlular of the present tense. The rule of the "d" in three parts: There is a one simple "rule" that covers the pronunciation of the "d" and "t" sounds. The sound that indicates the past of the verb is the voiceless "t" sound when the verb ends in a voiceless consonant. On the other hand, the indication of the past is the voiced "d" sound when the verb ends in a voiced consonant. The three parts of the rule are: 1. the voiceless "t" sound, 2. the voiced "d" sound, 3. the added syllable. 1. The voiceless (unvoiced) "t" The "rule" tells us when the last sound of a verb is is like that of the words talk, cap, mess, etc (that is, a voiceless sound), the past of the verb ends with a voiceless (or unvoiced) sound like that of the word walked. The past of these verbs is talked, capped, messed and the "d" is unvoiced. For example the letter "d" that represents the past in the written word is pronounced like the "t" of Tom (a voiceless sound) when the verb ends in a voiceless sound. So when the verb ends in voiceless sounds such as the letters k in the word looked, p in the word stopped, f in the word cuffed (or gh in the word laughed) the past is indicated by the voiceless "t" sound. This always happens so don't be fooled by the written letter "d". The past tense of the verb is also indicated by a voiceless sound when the verb ends in any "hissing" sound such as the words: face, wash, crunch. All these sounds are voiceless so the verbs that end with them will always have the "d" of their past form sounded voicelessly and therefore become the forms faced, washed, crunched. It is important to note that although the voiceless "d" is written "ed", you do NOT add a syllable to the original word. 2. The voiced "d" The "d" is voiced in two situations: a. when the word ends in a vowel sound such as, played, teed, owed, cued. The "strange" vowels are also followed by a voiced "d" such as in the words: furred, papered, pawed. The past of verbs ending in a diphthong sound also end in a voiced "d" sound, for example in the words: plowed, paid, toyed . b. when the word ends in a voiced consonant. Some examples of the second case are: b as in the word robbed, n in the word drowned, l in the word mailed, g in the word logged, v in the word heaved, m n the word farmed, n as in the word panned, the sound of the letters ng as in the word ring, r as in the word cars, v as in the word stoves, and thin the word bathed. Remember that that the voiced "d" sound forms the past of verbs that end in a voiced consonant, for example, burned is the past of the verb burn and lovedis the past of love. It is important to note that although the voiced "d" in these words is written with "ed", you do NOT add an extra syllable. 3. The added syllable In both cases, when the verb ends in either the sound of the voiced "d" or the sound of the voiceless "t", the English language adds a syllable to the verb. For example, the verbs in the present tense visit, vote, side, need, plant, adopt, add "ed" to make the past tense and become visited, voted, sided, needed, planted, adopted. The "ed" is pronounced with a special vowel followed by a voiced "d". The special vowel is the "short i" which has the IPA symbol of the small capital "i". We treat this sound in the book in the chapter on the short vowels. Remember a ship is not a sheep. You have to be able to hear the difference to be able to use this vowel in the added syllable. It is only in this special case that you pronounce the second syllable of the past of a verb. Not all verbs have two syllables in the past. It is important that you realize that most common English verbs have only one syllable. Do not think that you have to pronounce the "ed" of the words such as walked, talked, played, tuned, tooled. Do not read these words as they were written in your language. Although many verbs have "ed" in their past, it is just a strange note of English spelling. You often only pronounce one syllable with the past indicated by a voiced "d" or an unvoiced "t" according to which sound preceded the ending. You only pronounce the "ed" when the root form of the verb ends with your tongue touching the back of your teeth, either with a voiced "d" sound or with an unvoiced "t" sound. For example, "Today, I heat the coffee but yesterday I heated it" (2 syllables because the last consonant is a "t"). But, "Today I talk to my friend but yesterday I talked on the phone." (one syllable because the last consonant is not a "t" or a "d") The extra syllable: Listen to this as often as necessary for you to be able to distinguish the unvoiced "t" from the voiced "d". Review and practice all parts of the "RULE"! The first part of the "rule": the voiceless "t"; The second part of the "rule": the voiced "d" : The third part of the "rule": the added syllable An observatory is a place for making observations. The term is most used for astronomical observatories, which are places for looking at the sky. A modern astronomical observatory has a large telescope, or several. It is usually high up on mountains or hills, because higher places have cleaner, clearer, dryer air. Observatories also have to be away from places that have lots of light pollution, like cities. Too much light can make it so that the astronomers cannot see out of the telescope very well. In December 2005, Sharon was taken into hospital. Which targets can you reach? Title: How to Duplicate a Document in Mac OS X Lion Duplicating a document is a simple yet essential task when working with files on your Mac computer. This process creates an exact copy of the original file, which you can then modify without affecting the original document. By duplicating documents, you ensure that your original work remains intact while providing yourself with a new canvas to experiment or build upon. In this comprehensive guide, we will walk you through the steps required to duplicate a document in Mac OS X Lion (10.7). We'll also include helpful tips and guidelines along the way to enhance your understanding and facilitate the process. **Step 1: Locate the Original Document** First, navigate to the folder containing the document you wish to duplicate using Finder, the default file manager application in Mac OS X. You can do so by clicking on the blue smiley face icon found in your Dock or by opening Spotlight Search via Command + Space keys and typing the name of the file or its parent directory. Once located, proceed to the next step. **Step 2: Select the Document** To select the document, simply click once on its title. A single click highlights the file, indicating its selection. Make sure you have chosen the correct file since you don't want to create unnecessary copies unintentionally. Pay attention to details such as filename extensions if they are visible; these help distinguish between various types of files. **Key Tips:** - If multiple items need duplication, holding down the Shift or Command keys allows selecting more than one item at a time. - To avoid accidentally deselecting the file(s), refrain from clicking elsewhere until after completing Step 4. **Step 3: Initiate Duplication Process** After successfully choosing the document(s) to be copied, right-click (or hold Control and left-click) anywhere within the selected area. From the context menu that appears, move your cursor over the "Duplicate" option. As soon as you hover over it, the word "Duplicate" changes color slightly, giving visual feedback about the active choice. Left-click again to confirm your action. **Guidelines:** - The keyboard shortcut for duplicating a file is Command + D. However, note that pressing this combination works only when there is an active selection. Thus, make sure to complete Steps 1 and 2 before attempting this shortcut. - When right-clicking, avoid moving the mouse too quickly away from the clicked point, especially when dealing with numerous files, as doing so might interrupt the context menu display. **Step 4: Verify Successful Duplication & Examine New Copy** Once you've initiated the duplication process, Mac OS X Lion instantly generates a replica of the original document bearing the same name but appended with the phrase "copy." For example, if you were duplicating a text file named "Example.txt," the newly created copy would appear as "Example - Copy.txt" in the same location. Double-check whether the duplication has taken place correctly and examine the attributes of the new copy to ensure accuracy. **Key Points:** - Always verify successful completion of any automated tasks like this one to prevent potential data loss due to system glitches or user errors. - Checking the properties of both the original and copied files ensures consistency and serves as another layer of confirmation that everything went smoothly during the duplication process. Congratulations! You now know how to duplicate a document in Mac OS X Lion effectively. With practice, these actions will become second nature, enabling you to manage and manipulate your digital assets efficiently and securely. Happy computing! Do ya know what this reminds me of? Are you doin' silly things with your tongue? More Than Mosses Collectively, the mosses, liverworts and hornworts are called bryophytes. They are classified in the Plant Kingdom, they contain chlorophyll and they produce their own food. The bryophytes are called non-vascular plants because they lack internal conducting systems, called vascular systems, to carry nutrients through the plant. Bryophytes vary in size from a little over a millimetre tall to trailing species which grow in strands well over a metre long. Bryophytes come in many colours and these photographs show some of the variety. The trailing moss Papillaria flavolimbata can form extensive curtains of strands. The silvery-green moss Bryum argenteum is a widespread species which occurs from forest to town. A moss plant consists of leaves on a stem. While there are many cells in a moss leaf, in most cases the cells are in one plane so that the leaf is only one cell thick. This is markedly different to the leaves of vascular plants which are many cells thick. Mosses produce spores in capsules that often grow on short stalks. A hornwort in the genus Phaeoceros showing the thallose growth form and a few immature green spore capsules. Hornworts have neither leaves nor stems. They grow as flat, green lobes called a thallose growth form. A fertile hornwort produces its spores in a long, tapering horn-like capsule that grows up from the lobes. The whole horn-like structure is the capsule and there is no stalk. There are two types of liverworts leafy and thallose. The leafy liverworts have leaves on stems and look rather like mosses. Thallose liverworts grow as flat green lobes and some of these liverworts look like hornworts. The clock wasn't ringing] Title: How to Change the System/Boot Drive Letter in Windows XP Windows XP assigns drive letters to all partitions and drives connected to your computer during installation. However, there might be situations where you need to change the system or boot drive letter due to various reasons such as conflicting software that can't handle certain drive letters, organizing data by rearranging drive letters, or simply personal preference. This tutorial will guide you through changing the system/boot drive letter in Windows XP with clear instructions and helpful explanations along the way. **Disclaimer:** Modifying drive letters may lead to potential issues if not done correctly. Always back up important data before proceeding. The author is not responsible for any damage caused by following this tutorial. ### **Step 1: Open Disk Management Tool** Press `Win + R` to open Run dialog box, type `diskmgmt.msc`, then press Enter to launch Disk Management tool. It allows managing disks, volumes, and drive letters. As an alternative, right-click My Computer > Manage > Storage > Disk Management. ![Disk Management](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/a/ab/Change-the-System-%252FBoot-Drive-Letter-in-Windows-XP- Driving by but not like up close. He does like puzzles, so I or thought maybe he would play with that but [laughs]. Lily and Ben were playing with marbles on the floor. They had many marbles of different colors and sizes. They liked to roll them and make them hit each other. "Look, I have a big red marble. It is mighty. It can knock down all your marbles," Ben said. He rolled his red marble and hit Lily's blue marble. Lily's blue marble rolled away. "That is not nice, Ben. You should share your marbles and play fair," Lily said. She was sad and angry. "I don't want to share. I want to win. My red marble is the best," Ben said. He rolled his red marble again and hit Lily's green marble. Lily's green marble rolled away. Lily had only one marble left. It was a small yellow marble. She did not think it was mighty. She thought it was weak. She wanted to cry. But then she had an idea. She picked up her yellow marble and poked Ben's red marble with it. Ben's red marble did not roll. It cracked. "Hey, what did you do? You broke my red marble. That is not fair," Ben said. He was shocked and sad. "I poked it. You said it was mighty, but it was not. It was fragile. You should not be mean and boastful. You should be kind and humble," Lily said. She felt sorry for Ben, but also proud of her yellow marble. Ben looked at his broken red marble and then at Lily's yellow marble. He realized that he was wrong. He said sorry to Lily and asked her to be his friend. Lily smiled and said yes. You want to put that in the middle? The temperature of a gas is how hot or cold it is. In physics it is usually measured in kelvins although degrees Celsius are used more elsewhere. In a gas, the average velocity (how fast they move) of the molecules is related to the temperature. The faster the gas molecules are moving, the more they collide, or smash into each other. These collisions release energy, which in a gas comes in the form of heat. Conversely if the temperature around the gas becomes hotter then the gas particles will convert the thermal energy to kinetic energy, making them move faster and making the gas hotter. The White tower was a part of Thessaloniki's ancient walls that were built many times through the ages. Thessaloniki was the second-most well-fortified city in the Byzantine Empire, after Constantinople (Istanbul). The walls can still be seen above the city today and despite the many earthquakes, they are still standing. The city was home to a Roman imperial palace and mausoleum. The palace ruins can be seen today but the mausoleum together with a Roman arch can be still seen standing today. Very important sights of Thessaloniki are the medieval churches such as Agios Dimitrios, Agios Georgios and Agia Sofia. Agios Dimitrios it the largest and oldest Basilica in Greece. Thessaloniki's Christian monuments are part of the UNESCO World Heritage initiative. The city also has many Ottoman monuments, such as the Bey Hammam and the old district of the city near the walls, with its distinctly Turkish architecture. Many museums are in the city and some of them are: Archaeological Museum of Thessaloniki, Museum of the Macedonian Struggle, Jewish Museum, Ethnological Museum of Macedonia and others.
