Step 1: Open Adobe Photoshop To start with your "Step and Repeat" task in Photoshop, first open the software on your computer by clicking on its icon. If you have multiple versions installed, opt for the latest one for better results. Step 2: Create or Import Your Design Open a new document (File > New) if creating a design from scratch, or import an existing image (File > Open) that needs the 'Step and Repeat' treatment. Make sure the resolution is set high enough for print quality if needed. Step 3: Duplicate The Layer Once your artwork is ready, duplicate the layer by dragging it onto the "Create a new layer" button at the bottom of the Layers panel or using Ctrl+J (Windows) / Command+J (Mac). This ensures any changes made won't affect the original layer. Step 4: Transform & Position The Duplicated Object Select the duplicated layer, then go to Edit > Free Transform (Ctrl+T / Command+T), which allows you to resize, rotate, distort, perspective transform, or skew the object as required. After transformation, position this element next to the initial shape/image carefully so they align perfectly. Use the arrow keys along with the Shift key for precise movement. Step 5: Copy And Paste To Create Multiple Copies Now comes the actual 'step-and-repeat' part. With the second layer still selected, press Ctrl+C (Win) / Command+C (Mac) to copy it. Then hit Ctrl+V (Win) / Command+V (Mac) to paste. You should now see a third identical layer above the second one. Move this like before until all gaps are filled evenly. Step 6: Utilise Arrange Commands For Alignment For more complex designs where manual alignment might be challenging, consider employing Photoshop's built-in Arrange commands located under the Edit menu. These options include Distribute Horizontal Centers, Distribute Vertical Centers, Align Left Edges, etc., making the process easier and faster. Step 7: Merge Layers Once Done After successfully repeating and arranging the elements, merge them into one layer when satisfied with the result (right click over the topmost layer > Merge Layers or simply Ctrl+E / Command+E). Remember, merging layers permanently combines their content; hence, ensure no further edits are necessary on individual layers. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Always keep originals unaltered on separate layers or files until finalizing the project. - Maintain consistency while scaling and positioning repeated items. - Consider working with smart objects instead of regular layers for flexibility during editing stages. - Leverage automation tools such as Actions or Scripts for repetitive tasks like these. - Take advantage of Snap feature (View > Snap) to accurately place elements relative to others or guides. - Don't forget to save often! = = = children/CHIDES_NA/NewmanRatner/07/4664AM.cha = = = Sometimes like you can really get him laughing. Worship of the said deities is often done through the aid of pictures or icons ("murti") which are said not to be God themselves but conduits for the devotee's consciousness, markers for the human soul that signify the ineffable and illimitable nature of the love and grandeur of God. They are symbols of the greater principle, representing and are never presumed to "be" the concept or entity itself. Thus, Hindu image worship is a form of iconolatry, in which the symbols are venerated as putative sigils of divinity, as opposed to idolatry, a charge often levied (erroneously) at Hindus. For more details on this form of worship, see murti. It was getting late and Franklin was very tired and very hungry. In the light of the moon a little egg lay on the leaf. Title: How to Delete Messenger Contacts on iPhone or iPad Deleting contacts from your Messenger app can help you manage your list of friends and ensure that your personal information is up-to-date. This tutorial will guide you through the process of deleting Messenger contacts on both iPhone and iPad devices. Step 1: Open the Facebook Messenger App Locate the Messenger app on your home screen or in the app library and tap on it to open. The icon resembles a blue chat bubble with a lightning bolt inside. Step 2: Access Your List of Contacts Tap on the "People" tab located at the bottom right corner of the screen. This will take you to your list of Messenger contacts. Key Tip: If you cannot find the "People" tab, make sure that you have updated your Messenger app to the latest version. Outdated apps may not display all features correctly. Step 3: Find the Contact You Want to Delete Scroll through your list of contacts until you locate the one you wish to delete. Alternatively, you can use the search bar at the top of the screen to quickly find a specific contact by typing their name or phone number. Step 4: View the Contact's Profile Once you have found the contact you want to delete, tap on their name or profile picture to view their profile. This will provide more details about the contact and give you access to additional options. Step 5: Initiate the Deletion Process On the contact's profile page, look for the three horizontal dots (iOS) or the gear icon (iPad) located at the top right corner of the screen. Tap on this icon to reveal a dropdown menu. From the list of available options, select "Block" or "Ignore Messages." Key Guideline: Although there isn't a direct option to delete a contact within the Messenger app, blocking or ignoring messages from a contact effectively removes them from your active Messenger contacts list. However, keep in mind that blocking or ignoring a contact will prevent them from sending you messages or seeing when you are online. Step 6: Confirm the Block or Ignore Action A confirmation popup will appear, asking if you are sure you want to block or ignore messages from the selected contact. To proceed with the deletion, confirm the action by tapping "Block" or "Ignore" as appropriate. Step 7: Check Your Updated List of Contacts Return to the "People" tab to verify that the deleted contact no longer appears in your list. If they do still appear, try refreshing the page by pulling down slightly on the screen and releasing. By following these steps, you have successfully removed a contact from your Messenger app on either an iPhone or iPad device. Keep in mind that while this method does not permanently delete the contact from your Facebook account, it does remove them from your active Messenger contacts list. When you buy something "on credit," it means you're borrowing money from the seller to pay for it now, and then you promise to pay them back later. This is a common way for businesses to buy things too, like when Bumpas Enterprises buys $4,562,500 in goods each year from their supplier. Now, sometimes when you buy something on credit, the seller will give you a discount if you pay them back quickly. In this case, the supplier offered Bumpas Enterprises a discount if they paid within 15 days (this is called "2/15"). But if Bumpas doesn't pay within 15 days, they have to wait until day 50 to pay without losing the discount. The question asks us to find out what interest rate Bumpas is essentially paying by waiting to pay until day 50. To calculate this, we need to figure out what's called the Effective Annual Percentage Rate, or EAR. It tells us how much extra we'll end up paying compared to the original amount we borrowed, all calculated over a full year. We can think of it kind of like the total cost of renting money for a whole year. Here's the formula we can use to calculate the EAR: EAR = ((1 + small discount fraction / invoice period)*number of days in a year / number of days before late payment penalty) - 1 Let's break down the different parts of this formula: * The "(1 + small discount fraction / invoice period)" part represents the fact that there's a smaller amount you could pay if you took the discount, so your actual interest rate should include the difference between the two amounts. * The "*number of days in a year / number of days before late payment penalty" part adjusts our calculation based on the length of time between when you actually pay and when you were supposed to pay. * Finally, subtracting 1 at the end converts everything into a percent, just like normal interest rates. When we plug in the numbers for Bumpas Enterprises, we get: EAR = ((1 + 2 / 98) * 365 / 35) - 1 Solving this equation gives us an EAR of approximately 23.45%, which is the additional cost Bumpas pays every year by choosing not to take the discount and instead paying after 50 days. January 19, 2010 [ We studied methods of computing sines in a previous exercise. In today's exercise, guest author Jos Koot, a retired physicist, amateur pilot, and Scheme enthusiast, gives us an example of the use of sines. Feel free to contact me if you have an idea for an exercise, or if you wish to contribute your own exercise. Jos writes this disclaimer: Do not use the example program for real life flight planning. It may not be fool proof!!! Use more appropriate equipment and procedures after studying aeronautics more profoundly. I do not accept any claims. There is a lot more to flying than presented in this exercise. ] When preparing to fly, a pilot must know: - The direction and distance from the airfield of departure to the airfield of destination. This direction is called the ground track (GT). - The name (WN) and speed (WS) of the wind. The name is the direction from which the wind blows and is measured in degrees clockwise from north. The direction proper (WD) is opposite to that of the name. - The air speed of the plane (AS) This is the speed of the airplane relative to the moving air. Directions are measured in degrees clockwise from geographic north, 360 degrees on the compass-card. You may measure distances and times in any unit you like. Speeds should be in unit of distance per unit of time, of course. We take the air speed as a given datum and we ignore time needed for climbing and descending. Because the plane may be drifted by the wind, the pilot must calculate which direction to head for in order to compensate the drift. For this purpose the pilot may draw a triangle whose sides are speeds: - Ground track. This side is to be drawn in the direction from takeoff to destination but with unknown speed. This still unknown speed is called the ground speed (GS). - Drift caused by the wind. Both direction and speed are known thanks to meteorologists. The drift points in the direction the wind blows to and is opposite to that of the name of the wind. For example, wind from east causes drift to west. - True heading (HT). This is the direction the pilot must choose in order to compensate for the drift. The direction is unknown, but the speed is: it is the air speed, also called the cruise speed. It is instructive first to draw some navigation triangles by means of a pair of compasses and a ruler. The air speed is significantly greater than the wind speed, of course. First draw a line in the direction of the ground track. On this line choose a point B. Draw the drift ending in point B. Now we have point C. Draw a circle with point C for its centre and the air speed for its radius. The circle has two points of intersection with the ground track. One of the intersection points is A. Point A and the other point of intersection correspond to the two roots of the quadratic equation. Because the ground speed is directed from point A to point B it is not difficult to see which point of intersection is the right one. Now the vector equation AB = AC + CB is satisfied. The known data are: ground track GT, distance (D) from departure to destination, wind name WN, wind velocity WS and the air speed AS. The air speed is significantly greater than the velocity of the wind, of course. From these data we want to compute: true heading TH, ground speed GS and time of the flight (FT=D/GS). One solution uses the law of sines, which states that the ratio of the length of a side to the sine of the opposite angle is the same for all three sides of any triangle. We have , hence . The ground speed can be calculated as . Another solution uses the cosine rule c = a + b - 2ab cos( ) to compute the third side of a triangle when two sides and the included angle are known, a quadratic equation is obtained. Solving this equation for the unknown ground speed, we have . Because we want a positive ground speed, take the root with the plus sign. Finally use the cosine rule again in order to compute the angle of correction (angle A), which is the angle between the ground track and the required true heading: , so the true heading is . This angle must be added or subtracted from the direction of the ground track depending on which side the wind drifts the plane. Your task is to write two functions, as above, that take a distance, ground track, wind speed and direction, and air speed and computes the true heading, ground speed, correction and duration of flight. When you are finished, you are welcome to read or run a suggested solution, or to post your own solution or discuss the exercise in the comments below. [naming Seth's older brother; 15 sec silence] Title: How to Draw Anime Eyes (Easy) Introduction: Anime eyes are known for their expressiveness and unique styles that can convey various emotions. This tutorial will guide you through drawing basic anime eyes with simple steps, making it easy for beginners to understand and practice. By following these instructions, you'll be able to create captivating characters with engaging gazes. Step 1: Understanding Basic Shapes Start by sketching two overlapping circles or ovals - one slightly smaller than the other - to form the base shape of an eye. The larger circle represents the iris while the smaller one is the pupil. These shapes help establish proportion and positioning. Overlap them more if you want your character to appear squinting; overlap less for wide-eyed expressions. Key tip: Keep proportions consistent between both eyes for symmetry and balance. Step 2: Adding Highlights and Reflections To give life to your anime eye, add small highlights within the pupil using tiny ellipses or dots. Place one highlight at the top part of the pupil, which simulates light reflection off a round surface. For added realism, include another subtle reflection near the bottom edge of the pupil. Guideline: Multiple reflections indicate brighter lighting conditions, whereas fewer reflections suggest dimmer environments. Step 3: Defining Iris Details Divide the space between the pupil and outer edge of the large circle into four sections using curved lines. Each section should resemble a pie piece. Fill in alternating segments with solid colors or patterns like concentric circles, spokes, or radial lines. Avoid overly intricate designs as they may distract from overall expression. Note: Darker shades create deeper-set eyes, while lighter tones result in softer looks. Experiment with different hues to find what suits your art style best! Step 4: Outlining the Eye Structure Now, trace around the circumference of the initial circles using smooth, continuous lines. Connect the upper portions of these outlines to form eyebrows, then extend the lower edges downwards to signify eyelashes. Vary thickness and curvature based on gender and age; thicker brows typically represent masculinity, whereas thinner ones imply femininity. Similarly, longer lashes suit female characters, whilst shorter lashes work well for males. Step 5: Creating Depth & Dimension Shade along the inner corner of the eye, beneath the lower lash line, and under the eyebrow. Lightly blend this shadow towards surrounding areas to produce a sense of depth. Additionally, darken the outer corners of the eye and any creases formed when connecting the eyebrow to the eye socket. Pro tip: Use a soft graphite pencil for blending shadows smoothly without harsh transitions. Step 6: Expressive Touches Depending on the emotion you wish to portray, consider adding specific details such as teardrops, sparkles, or furrowed brows. Teardrops usually consist of a few wavy curves converging at a point below the eye; sparkles involve several small flecks scattered across the eye area. To depict anger or concentration, draw vertical wrinkles between the eyebrows. Conclusion: Drawing anime eyes requires patience and practice, but mastering this foundational skill allows artists to bring their characters to life. With time, experimentation, and dedication, even complex techniques become manageable. Remember, every artist has their own unique approach, so feel free to adapt these methods according to personal preference and artistic vision. Happy drawing! Description/Size: The Blue Whale is the largest animal to have lived. They can grow to over 100 feet in length (3 school buses) and weigh well over 120 tons ( 240,000 pounds). One female was reported as weighing 174 tons! Blue Whales have bluish-gray or gray bodies with light white spots throughout. Their underbellies are white or light gray. Flippers can be eight feet long, and the tail (flukes) may reach a length of over 25 feet. The Blue Whale is so large that it heart needs to be the size of a car to pump blood throughout its massive body. The Blue Whale has two blowholes on the top of its body. Blow holes function like human nostrils but can shoot water 30 feet into the air! Their large layer of blubber helps them stay warm when swimming through very cold water. Diet/Feeding: Despite its size, the Blue Whale does not eat large animals. In fact, its diet is formed by microscopic shrimp-like organisms called Krill. Krill are one of the most important organisms in the ocean ecosystems, as many large whales depend on prodigious amounts to survive. Blue Whales gulp huge amounts of ocean water filled with millions of Krill and Plankton. The water is then filtered through baleen plates in the whale's jaw. As the water escapes through the baleen plates, the Krill and Plankton are retained. Baleen plates work much like a water filter you may have on your kitchen sink. Such plates allow the Blue Whale to consume about 2,000-9,000 pounds of krill per day, or 40,000,000 individual krill. You would have to eat 8,000-36,000 cheeseburgers per day to eat the same amount. The Blue Whale has about 320 baleen plates on each side of its jaw. They are each more than three feet in length. In addition, the whale's tongue may weigh 4 tons and can hold 50 people. Reproduction/Young: Female Blue Whales give birth to a single calf that comes out tail-first. The newborn calf instinctively rises to the surface with its mother's help to take its first breath of oxygen. The calf weighs 6-8 tons, or about 1,800 times the size of a human baby. The young Blue Whale grows very fast and will drink about 200 pounds of its mother's milk per day. Calves stay with their mothers for about a year. Communication: Blue Whales are the world's loudest animal. Their whistles are louder than a jet engine and can be heard for miles underwater. Whistles are used to communicate with other whales and to locate large bodies of Krill. Range/Habitat: Blue Whales inhabit virtually all parts of the Earth's oceans except for extreme polar seas. They generally stay near the surface and give birth in warmer waters. Blue Whales are fast swimmers despite their size, and can reach speeds of 30 miles per hour in short bursts. Status: Blue Whale populations have experienced alarming declines. Today, there are wide estimates concerning the number of Blue Whales alive. Estimates range from as few as 1,300 to as many as 14,000. The giant but gentle Blue Whale was hunted mercilessly by whale hunters and nations that sponsored whale hunting. Their meat, oil and body parts were harvested by hunters on ships equipped with harpoons, long daggers that could be shot out like arrows. It is now illegal to hunt Blue whales and populations may be rising. After fourteen months in office, Paul Martin got the nickname "Mr. Dithers". He was called so by the magazine "The Economist". They say this is because he changed his opinion too often. [asks Jo Ann to play the piano later] Well she picked up the peanut butter. Many people who do not speak the language read a romanized version to know roughly how the words will sound, even if that is not the normal way to write the language. Some languages, like Chinese and Japanese, use the Latin alphabet in their languages so that they can be typed on a computer more easily. In mainland China, pinyin is the official romanization for Mandarin Chinese, and it is used to type Chinese characters on the computer by typing them phonetically. Even though many Japanese computers have kana keyboards to type Japanese on the computer, Japanese can also be typed using the Latin alphabet. PROBLEM. To describe an equilateral triangle on a given finite straight line. Let AB be the given straight line: it is required to describe an equilateral triangle on AB. From the centre A at the distance AB describe the circle BCD. [Postulate 3. From the centre B, at the distance BA, describe the circle ACE. [Postulate 3. From the point C, at which the circles cut one another, draw the straight lines CA and CB to the points A and B. [Post. 1. ABC shall be an equilateral triangle. Because the point A is the centre of the circle BCD, AC is equal to AB. [Definition 15. And because the point B is the centre of the circle ACE, BC is equal to BA. [Definition 15. But it has been shewn that CA is equal to AB; therefore CA and CB are each of them equal to AB. But things which are equal to the same thing are equal to one another. [Axiom 1. Therefore CA is equal to CB. Therefore CA, AB, BC are equal to one another. Wherefore the triangle ABC is equilateral, [Def. 24. and it is described on the given straight line AB. q.e.f. Formatting a memory card on a Nokia N73 is a straightforward process that can help you free up space, improve your device's performance, and ensure optimal storage functionality. This tutorial will guide you through the necessary steps with clear explanations and helpful tips along the way. **Key Tips and Guidelines:** * Before formatting, make sure you have backed up any important data from the memory card as this process erases all content permanently. * Only format the memory card using your Nokia N73 or appropriate software; other methods may result in damage to the card or loss of compatibility. * You should format a new memory card before first use to set it up correctly and optimize its performance on your Nokia N73. **Step 1: Accessing the Memory Card Settings** From the home screen, navigate to **Menu > Tools > Settings > Connection > Mass Storage.** Make sure 'Connect via mass storage when connected to PC' option is selected if you wish to connect the phone to a computer later. Afterward, go back one level by pressing the left softkey (labeled as '< Back') until you reach **Settings**. Now select **Memory**, then choose **Mass memory**. Here you will see details about both internal and external memories. Select **Format memory card**. If prompted again, confirm the action by selecting **Yes**. **Step 2: Choosing Format Type** You will be asked to pick between two different formats - FAT16 and FAT32. The choice depends mainly on the size of your memory card: * Choose **FAT16** for cards smaller than 2GB. * For cards larger than 2GB, opt for **FAT32**. Select either FAT16 or F Fat32 based on the size of your memory card. Once chosen, press the center button (or tap OK) to proceed. **Step 3: Initiating the Format Process** A warning message will appear, reminding you that all data on the memory card will be deleted. Confirm that you understand this by choosing **Yes**. The formatting process will begin. It might take several minutes depending upon the capacity and condition of your memory card. Please do NOT interrupt the power supply during this time. **Step 4: Completing the Format Process** Once completed, an information box will pop-up stating 'Card formatted successfully'. Press the center button (or tap Done) to close it. Your memory card has now been reformatted and prepared for usage. **Step 5: Transferring Data (Optional)** If you had previously saved valuable files elsewhere, reinsert them onto the freshly formatted memory card. Connect your Nokia N73 to your computer via USB cable, enabling file transfer mode, and copy over your essential documents, photos, music, etc., into their respective folders within the memory card directory. Congratulations! By following these steps, you've successfully formatted your memory card on your Nokia N73 while learning some handy background info too. Remember always to backup your crucial data prior to future format attempts. Did you ever see a real one? And I'd like you and your mom to write a letter to Eric Care about his book. Spherical Geometry and Triangles Date: 02/09/2004 at 23:33:15 From: Nil Subject: (no subject) Is it possible to have a triangle with two 90 degree angles, and the other two legs from the connected 90 degree angles meet? Where would you find such a triangle? I thought maybe if the triangle is on a sphere, but then the lines aren't straight. Date: 02/10/2004 at 00:28:04 From: Doctor Jeremiah Subject: Re: (no subject) Hi Nil, You are correct that in the study of "spherical geometry" it is possible to have a triangle with two 90 degree angles. The idea of "straight" is relative to where you live. If you live on a sphere then something that looks straight to you will look curved from a 3D perspective. Let's look at an example of such a triangle using a sphere we are familiar with--the Earth. Say you are exactly on the North Pole. Walk straight south 345.8767 km. Then walk straight east 345.8767 km. Then walk straight north 345.8767 km. You are back at the North Pole and all three of your corners are 90 degree angles. Here is how I found that particular value: Starting on the North Pole, first you walk south X distance in a straight line and then you walk east X distance in a straight line. At that point you must be 1/4 of the way around the globe so that when you walk back north X distance in a straight line you will have a 90 degree angle with the straight line you walked south away from the North Pole. The radius of the Earth is 6378.137 km, so the circumference is 40075.02 km. If you walk X km south from the North Pole you have gone X/40075.02 % of the way around the Earth which is 360*X/40075.02 degrees. The radius of the line of latitude at that point is R = 6378.137*sin(360*X/40075.02) km. That means the circumference of that line of latitude is 2*Pi*R. When you walk east around that line of latitude you want X km to be 1/4 of the way around, so X = 2*Pi*R/4. Now we have two equations: R = 6378.137*sin(360*X/40075.02) X = 2*Pi*R/4 And when we solve them (numerically) we get X=345.8767 km That may be more math than you were interested in. The short answer to your question is that if we work with triangles drawn on a sphere rather than on a plane, it is in fact possible for a triangle to have two or even three 90 degree angles. - Doctor Jeremiah, The Math Forum http://mathforum.org/dr.math/ Search the Dr. Math Library: Ask Dr. MathTM 1994-2013 The Math Forum [the diapers are stored in a closet and Ross is using nonverbal means in place of language] Ye'h nothin' there to capture her too much whereas her sister would have loved it she's interested in telling all the time even though she has no concept of it. A chord can be described in a number of ways, and I will describe them in more and more detailed and mathematical ways as you progress through UkeSchool. Here in the beginner's lessons, we can think of a chord as 4 notes, one note for each string, all played at the same time. The first chord you are going to learn is called the "C" chord, also known as the "C Major" chord. A chord diagram is a simple drawing of the instrument, with markers for the finger postions required to make the chord. At UkeSchool, you can click on any chord chart with your mouse, and it will play the chord for you! Try it! We also have colour in our chord diagrams - in this case, the 'gold' colour means the 'ring finger' - that shouldn't be too hard to remember. You'll learn about more fingers as you learn more chords. To play the "C" chord as shown in this chord diagram, you simply press the end of your ring finger down on the "A" string between the second and third "fret", those metal bars that go across the neck. You have to have your finger far enough back from the third fret that the string can vibrate freely, and you have to press down hard enough that the string stays down on the fret. Now, drag the fingernail on your right index finger down across all four strings - you've played your first chord! Does it sound the same as when you click on the chord diagram? If it doesn't, you probably need to tune again. Don't feel bad if you do - it'll take a little while to get everything settled. Take a deep breath, tune up each of the strings with the uke tuner, and try the chord again - once you get it sounding nice, you'll be ready for your first jam session! She can't jam her fingers in it. Calculating the Percentage of Two Numbers: A Comprehensive Guide Step 1: Understand What a Percentage Is A percentage is a number that represents a fraction of 100. The word "percent" comes from the Latin words per centum, meaning "by hundred." It's often used to express quantities as parts of a whole or ratios between different values. For example, if you have 50 candies out of a possible 100, you would say you have 50% (fifty percent) of the candies. Key Tip: Remember that percentages are always relative to a base value of 100. Thus, understanding the relationship between two numbers involves converting them into their equivalent fractions of this common denominator. Step 2: Gather Your Numbers Before calculating the percentage, make sure you have identified clearly both the numerator (the number representing the part being considered) and the denominator (the total amount). These could be any two related numerical values. For instance, let's consider a scenario where John scored 85 marks out of 100 in an exam. Here, '85' is our numerator and '100' serves as the denominator. Step 3: Convert the Numerator Into Its Equivalent Fraction of the Denominator To do this, divide the numerator by the denominator. This will give you a decimal representation of what proportion the numerator forms of the denominator. In our example, we would calculate 85 / 100 = 0.85 Guideline: If your answer is greater than 1 or less than zero, double-check your original numbers because there might be a mistake in calculation or interpretation. Step 4: Multiply the Decimal Value Obtained By 100 Since percentages are based on hundreds, multiplying the decimal value obtained earlier by 100 converts it into an equivalent percentage form. So, continuing with our example, when we multiply 0.85 by 100, we get 85%. This means that John has achieved 85% of the total score. Step 5: Interpret the Result Now that you've calculated the percentage, interpret its significance depending on the context. Using our previous example, knowing that John got 85% tells us about his performance level in the examination. Remember, interpreting results correctly can help inform decision making, whether in academic settings, business operations, financial management, scientific research, etc. By following these steps meticulously, anyone should be able to accurately determine the percentage of one number compared to another. Not only does this skill prove useful across various aspects of life, but mastering it also enhances quantitative reasoning abilities - a critical component of mathematical literacy. _Heerein is declared, how dangerous the occasion is, ensuing by anger and despight, in such as entirely love, especially, being injuried and offended by them that they love. _ When the King perceived, that Madame _Pampinea_ had ended her discourse; he sat sadly a prety while, without uttering one word, but afterward spake thus. Like tell me if you agree or disagree with anything I put. But what is electricityy? First you have to know what an atom is. An atom is the smallest part of something. It is microscopic and every thing is made up of atoms. |Atoms are made up of three parts. There are :| Protons These parts of an atom have a positive charge. They are in the middle of the atom, called the nucleus and they do not move. Neutrons These parts of an atom have no charge. They are neutral and part of the nucleus of an atom with the protons. Electrons These parts of the atom are very small and weigh a lot less then the protons and neurtons. Electrons are not part of the nucleus of the atom, instead they move around in orbits outside the nucleus. Electrons are the only part of an atom that moves. So what does this have to do with electricity? Electricity is the flow of moving electrons. When the electrons flow it is called an electrical current. |To understand why electrons flow you need to understand that atoms can lose electrons by rubbing against another material. Think about when you rub your head against a balloon. Believe it or not, your hair is actually getting some electrons from the balloon. Because your hair has more electrons then protons, we say it is negitively charged. | But what about the balloon? Well the balloon has more protons than electrons so it is positively charged. Now that we know objects can have positive or negitive charges let's talk about how objects that are charged will behave. There are 3 main rules about electrical charges: like charges repel |So if both you and your friend rubbed balloons on your head and then tried to stick the balloons together they would repel or push away from each other because they would both have the same charge.| unlike charged objects attract Since your hair has a positive charge and the balloon has a negitive charge, they are attracted to each other. Kind of like to magnets trying to stick together. a charged object will attract an uncharged object So the balloon is charged and the wall has no charge. This means the wall is attracted to the balloon. Knowing these three rules you can think of the lunch line full of boys. (Pretend the boys have a negitive charge.)A girl comes to the front of the line. (The girl has a positive charge.) The boys are attracted or trying to get as close as possible to the girl. The boys all start pushing the boy in front of him closer to the girl and to move away from the other boys. This is how the electrons flow. The negitive charges (electrons) move because they are repelled by other electrons and because they are attracted to the positive charges (protons). The flowing electrons make electricity, but how can we use it? |You need three things to let you use this electrical current: a circuit or a path for the electrons to move through, a power source, or something that is going to make the electrons move, like a battery or a generator, and something to use the electricity, like a light bulb or a t.v..| This is a lot of information electricity. Title: How to Avoid Suspicion and Becoming Paranoid Introduction: In our daily lives, we often find ourselves in situations where avoiding suspicion is crucial. This could be at work, school, or even within our social circles. While being cautious can help us avoid trouble, becoming overly paranoid can lead to unnecessary stress and anxiety. The goal is to strike a balance between vigilance and trust. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to do just that. Step 1: Understand the Difference Between Caution and Paranoia Caution involves assessing potential risks and taking measured steps to mitigate them. On the other hand, paranoia refers to an irrational fear or mistrust that negatively impacts your life. It's important to distinguish these two states because while caution is healthy, paranoia is not. Key Tip: Regular self-reflection can help you identify if your actions stem from rational concern or irrational fear. Step 2: Analyze Your Surroundings Objectively When entering a new environment or situation, take time to observe without jumping to conclusions. Look for objective evidence of danger rather than relying on assumptions or preconceived notions. Guideline: Use your senses fully - sight, sound, smell, touch, and hearing - to gather information about your surroundings. Step 3: Evaluate Information Logically Once you have gathered enough data, evaluate it logically. Ask yourself questions like: What are the facts? Is there any concrete evidence suggesting I should feel suspicious? Remember, logic trumps emotion when making decisions based on observation. Key Tip: If possible, discuss your observations with someone else who has no stake in the outcome. A fresh perspective might reveal aspects you overlooked due to bias or lack of knowledge. Step 4: Consider Alternative Explanations There may be multiple interpretations of the same event. Try to think of alternative reasons why something happened before deciding it was directed against you personally. Guideline: Always remember Occam's Razor principle which suggests that the simplest explanation tends to be the correct one. I'll bring you some Lego blocks to play with. An example of this is smallpox. This virus used to kill people, cause blindness, and bad scars. Now there has not been a person who had smallpox in over thirty years. Grooming your ring-necked parakeet is an essential part of maintaining its health and happiness. Regular grooming can help prevent feather damage, maintain healthy skin, and strengthen the bond between you and your pet bird. Here's a comprehensive guide on how to groom your ring-necked parakeet: **Things You'll Need:** 1. Birdbath or shallow dish 2. Soft-bristled toothbrush or small brush designed for birds 3. Nail clippers specifically made for birds 4. Styptic powder (in case of cutting the quick) 5. Cotton swabs 6. A towel or blanket to wrap your bird in 7. Optional: Feather dusters or mineral blocks for beak trimming **Step 1: Acclimate Your Bird to Being Handled** Before attempting to groom your bird, ensure that they are comfortable being handled. Spend time with them daily, offering treats from your hand, and gently touching their body. This process may take several days or even weeks depending on your bird's personality. **Step 2: Prepare a Safe Space** Choose a quiet, well-lit area where your bird feels safe. Cover the table or surface with a towel to create traction and reduce stress. Have all necessary tools within reach. **Step 3: Bathe Your Bird** Ring-necked parakeets enjoy bathing. Fill a birdbath or shallow dish with warm water. Place your bird into the water. They might splash around and play. Afterward, dry them thoroughly using a clean towel. Bathing should be done once or twice a week. **Step 4: Brushing Feathers** Use a soft-bristled toothbrush or avian brush to gently comb through your bird's feathers. Start at the head and move towards the tail. Be careful near sensitive areas like the eyes and ears. Brushing removes dirt, old feathers, and promotes new growth. **Key Tip**: Never force brushing if your bird resists. Gradually increase tolerance by starting with short sessions. **Step 5: Trimming Nails** Hold your bird securely but gently. If possible, have someone else hold your bird while you trim. Identify the pink section inside the nail (the 'quick'). Only cut the white tip beyond this point. Use sharp, curved nail clippers designed for birds. If you accidentally cut the quick causing bleeding, apply styptic powder immediately to stop the bleeding. **Guideline**: Always err on the side of caution; leaving nails slightly longer is better than risking injury. **Step 6: Cleaning Cere (Nasal Area)** The cere is the fleshy area above your bird's beak where it breathes. Use a damp cotton swab to carefully wipe away any visible debris. **Step 7: Checking and Possibly Trimming the Beak** A healthy beak naturally wears down as the bird eats and plays. However, if the upper and lower parts do not align properly, consult a vet about potential beak deformities. To maintain beak length, provide toys with abrasive surfaces, mineral blocks, or purchase special feeders that require more effort to extract food. Severe overgrowth requires professional attention. Remember, patience is crucial when grooming your bird. It could take multiple attempts before your parakeet gets used to the process. Always treat your bird kindly and positively reinforce good behavior during grooming sessions. The Medici family were important patrons of the arts. In 1489, Lorenzo de' Medici, the head of the family, asked Ghirlandaio to send his two best pupils to the academy. Michelangelo was one of the students chosen and he attended the academy from 1490 to 1492. He heard the teaching and discussion of Marsilio Ficino, Pico della Mirandola and Angelo Poliziano. The philosophy that they taught was called Humanism. It was based on the philosophy of the ancient Greek Plato. Michelangelo's ideas and his art were influenced by these teachings. [MOT leans back and lets Chi finish alone] It might not look like it, but the planet Venus is standing farthest from the Sun for its current "morning-star" appearance. Although it's quite bright, it's low in the east at first light, so any trees or buildings along the horizon will block it from view. Venus and the other planets all lie near the ecliptic -- the Sun's path across the sky. Right now, the ecliptic tilts low across the south during the day. So as Venus rises, it follows a low angle -- it scoots along the horizon instead of popping straight up into the sky. That means that even though there's a wide angle between Venus and the Sun, the planet doesn't climb very high in the sky before daybreak. But the light of day doesn't fully hide Venus from view. It's so bright that it's still visible even after sunrise. The trick is to know when and where to look for it. Once you find it, it stands out. Today, for example, Venus is due south around 10 or 10:30. It's roughly two-thirds of the way up to the zenith -- the point directly overhead. And around 3:30 or 4, it's due west, a little more than the width of a hand at arm's length above the horizon. The details vary depending on your location, but that gives you a general bearing on the planet. One thing you can't see through the daylight is Mars. But it's in good view at first light, a little to the left of Venus. It's not as bright as Venus, but its closeness to the morning star will help you find it. Title: How to Fan a Single Action Revolver Single action revolvers are popular firearms among collectors, enthusiasts, and shooters due to their unique design and historical significance. One distinctive technique often associated with single action revolvers is "fanning" - rapidly firing multiple rounds by using the cylinder's rotation to cock the hammer between shots instead of manually doing so. This wiki-style guide will provide you with an in-depth explanation of this technique and its applications. Please remember that fanning should only be practiced under controlled conditions and following all safety protocols. **Step 1: Understanding Your Revolver** Before attempting any advanced techniques like fanning, ensure you have a thorough understanding of your single action revolver's operation. Key components include the cylinder, ejector rod, grip, trigger, and hammer. Spend time familiarizing yourself with these parts before moving onto more complex operations such as fanning. **Step 2: Safety First!** Always prioritize safety when handling firearms. Make sure the gun is unloaded before beginning any practice or training exercises. Point the muzzle in a safe direction at all times, keep fingers off the trigger until ready to shoot, and wear appropriate protective gear during live firing sessions. **Step 3: Assessing Suitability** Not all single action revolvers are suitable for fanning. Ideally, choose one designed specifically for rapid firing or competition purposes. These models typically feature heavy-duty springs and robust construction capable of withstanding repeated high-speed cycling without damage. Avoid antique or collector's items which may suffer irreparable harm from improper usage. **Step 4: Grip Technique** A firm yet comfortable grip is crucial for successful fanning. Grasp the handle firmly but don't squeeze too tightly. Position your index finger straight along the outside edge of the frame above the trigger guard, keeping it clear of the trigger itself while maintaining control over the weapon. **Step 5: Initial Cocking** Cock the hammer fully back to the first click (half-cock position) using your thumb. Some modern replicas have safeties; make sure they're disengaged if present. At half-cock, there's less risk of accidental discharge compared to full cock. **Step 6: Starting The Fan** With the hammer cocked, place your middle finger on the rear portion of the hammer spur. Apply steady pressure backwards towards the initial cocking motion. As the cylinder rotates, allow gravity or gentle forward momentum to bring the hammer forward again. **Step 7: Maintaining Control** As the hammer falls naturally, maintain contact with your middle finger to prevent it from slamming down abruptly. Simultaneously, continue applying slight backward pressure to cock the next round. Rapidly repeat steps six and seven for consecutive shots. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Practice slowly at first to develop muscle memory and proper form. Gradually increase speed once confident in your ability to safely fan the revolver. * Keep your wrist stiff and arm stationary to minimize movement and improve accuracy. * Be aware that fanning produces significant noise, recoil, and muzzle flash, potentially affecting sight picture and accuracy. * Regular maintenance checks are essential after practicing fanning since this technique exerts additional stress on mechanical components. Remember, mastery requires patience and consistent practice. Don't rush the process and always respect firearm safety rules. Happy shooting! Clicking with anybody you meet is an essential social skill that can greatly enhance your personal and professional relationships. This guide will provide you with detailed steps and useful tips to help you connect with people more effectively. **Step 1: Be Genuinely Interested** One of the most important aspects of clicking with somebody is showing genuine interest in them. People are generally more open and receptive when they feel valued and heard. To demonstrate this interest, ask open-ended questions about their experiences, opinions, and feelings. Avoid asking yes or no questions as they limit the conversation. Key tip: Listen attentively while maintaining eye contact during conversations. Show empathy by acknowledging their emotions and thoughts. **Step 2: Find Common Ground** Discover shared interests or experiences between you two. It could be hobbies, favorite books or movies, travel destinations, or even common acquaintances. Finding these connections creates rapport and makes it easier for both parties to engage in meaningful discussions. Guideline: Don't force similarities; instead let them emerge naturally through conversation. Authenticity goes a long way in building trust. **Step 3: Practice Active Listening** Active listening involves fully focusing on what the other person is saying without interrupting or judging them. Repeat back key points to confirm understanding and ask relevant follow-up questions. This approach shows respect for their ideas and encourages further dialogue. Key Tip: Put away distractions like phones or laptops during face-to-face interactions. Your undivided attention speaks volumes. **Step 4: Use Positive Body Language** Nonverbal cues such as facial expressions, gestures, and posture significantly influence how others perceive us. Adopt an open body language - uncrossed arms, maintained eye contact, and friendly smiles. These signals convey approval, engagement, and warmth towards the speaker. Guideline: Mirror the other person's body language subtly to create a sense of comfort and familiarity. However, avoid mimicking excessive mannerisms which might come off as mockery. **Step 5: Respect Differences** Understand that everyone has unique perspectives based on their upbringing, culture, beliefs, etc. Instead of dismissing differences, embrace them as opportunities for learning. By doing so, you foster inclusivity and promote mutual respect. Key Tip: If disagreements arise, focus on finding common ground rather than winning arguments. Remember, clickings aren't about agreement but rather comfortable companionship despite diversity. **Step 6: Offer Sincere Compliments** Complimenting someone genuinely can make them feel appreciated and respected. Focus on qualities that require effort, such as skills, knowledge, or character traits. But ensure your compliments are heartfelt and appropriate. Insincere flattery may damage trust and credibility. Guideline: Space out your compliments throughout the interaction instead of showering too many at once. Overdoing it can seem insincere. **Step 7: Share About Yourself Appropriately** While focusing on the other person is crucial, sharing about yourself also plays a role in creating connections. Disclose enough information to establish a balanced conversation flow. Keep it light and positive initially before diving deeper into personal topics. Key Tip: Always tailor self-disclosures according to the situation and relationship level. What feels suitable for a close friend might be inappropriate for casual encounters. By consistently practicing these steps, you'll find it becomes second nature to click with anyone you meet. Just remember, patience and practice are key! [holds it up again to show MOT] Harvesting milk thistle can be a rewarding experience, as this plant has many uses, including medicinal purposes. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to harvest milk thistle: **Step 1: Identify Milk Thistle** Before you start harvesting, make sure you have correctly identified milk thistle. It is a tall plant with prickly leaves and a spiky purple flower on top. The stem is usually green and can grow up to 2 meters (6.5 feet) high. Its most distinctive feature is its white veins on the leaves, which look like they have been splashed with milk - hence the name. Key Tip: Familiarize yourself with other similar looking plants in your area to avoid confusion. **Step 2: Choose the Right Time** The best time to harvest milk thistle is in late spring or early summer when the flowers are just beginning to open. This is when the leaves contain the highest concentration of nutrients. However, if you want to collect the seeds, wait until late summer or fall when the seed heads have turned brown and dried out. Guideline: Regularly check your milk thistle patch so you don't miss the optimal harvesting period. **Step 3: Gather Necessary Tools** You will need gloves, scissors or pruning shears, a basket or bucket, and possibly a tarp or newspaper to collect the seeds. Make sure all your tools are clean to prevent spreading diseases between plants. Key Tip: Leather gloves are recommended due to the sharp spines on the leaves. **Step 4: Protect Yourself** Put on your gloves before handling the plant to protect against the spiny leaves. Long sleeves and pants can also provide extra protection. Be careful not to touch your face while working to avoid accidentally transferring any sap. **Step 5: Harvest the Leaves** Using your scissors or pruning shears, cut off the outermost leaves first. These are typically tougher but still packed with beneficial compounds. As you move inward, the leaves become more tender. Remember, only take what you need; leaving some foliage behind allows the plant to continue growing. **Step 6: Prepare the Harvested Leaves** Rinse the collected leaves thoroughly under running water to remove dirt and insects. Pat them dry with a towel or let them air dry completely before storing them in an airtight container in the refrigerator. They should last for about a week. **Step 7: Harvest the Seeds** If you're after the seeds, wait till the flower head turns brown and starts drying out. Cut off the entire flower head and place it upside down inside a paper bag. Hang the bag in a warm, dry place away from direct sunlight. After two weeks, shake the bag gently to release the fully ripe seeds. Key Tip: Make sure the seeds are completely dry before storage to prevent mold growth. **Step 8: Store the Seeds** Pour the separated seeds into a small jar or envelope. Label it clearly with the contents and date of collection. Store in a cool, dark place. Properly stored seeds can remain viable for several years. Remember, always respect nature and never overharvest. Leave enough for the plant population to regenerate and maintain biodiversity. Enjoy your homegrown milk thistle! She was chased by a puppy this morning. Once we know the rotation centre, we can easily determine the angle in which the shapes have rotated. In F 28, the values of angles AOA' and BOB' are equal; they are both 140 and it is the angle rotated from segment AB to A'B'. ROTATION CENTRE AND ANGLE OF TWO COUNTERPART TRIANGLES. The process is the same as we have studied so far. In F 29, we have two equivalent (counterpart) triangles. We will determine the rotation centre and the value of the rotation angle. In F 30, we have the bisectors of the lines AA', BB' and CC', which define the circumcentre. Using this circumcentre, we trace arches in green, red, and blue as described by vertices A, B and C to A', B' and C'. If we take a closer look at F 31, we can see that the angles that form AOA' (in green), BOB' (in blue) and COC' (in red) represent the rotation angle, which logically, have to be the same. In this case, we find that the rotation angle is 157 . SYMMETRY IN THE PLANE. When the position of a shape in the plane, its dimensions and form, correspond to one and another side of a point, an axis or a plane, we can say they are symmetrical shapes. Such shapes must be found at an equal distance of the point, axis or plane of symmetry: In F.1, point O, or the centre of the circumference (circumcentre), is the middle point for every line. Point O is the centre of symmetry. If we take any segment, for example, OD and we fold it by the middle (point O), we will find it coincides with OD'. Point O is found at an equal distance of points A and A', B and B', C and C', and D and D'. Points AOA', BOB', COC' and DOD' are aligned. Instead of using points, as in the previous case, let's analyse the segments that form the pentagons in F.2. In this case, both pentagons are symmetrical is relation to a central point of symmetry; O: In F. 3, we see that the segments that join the counterpart points AA', BB', CC', DD' and EE' go through point O, which is the symmetry point: If we fold a sheet of paper with the contents of F.3 through point O con with its corresponding inclination, both pentagons would coincide. The problem with the mean is that it does not tell anything about how the values are distributed. Values that are very large or very small change the mean a lot. In statistics, these extreme values might be errors of measurement, but sometimes the population really does contain these values. For example, if there are 10 people in a room who make $10 per day and 1 who makes $1,000,000 per day. The mean of the data is $90,918 per day. Even though it is the average amount, the mean in this case is not the amount any single person makes, and thus is not very useful for some purposes. Defending yourself against a trespasser who sues for injuries can be a daunting experience, but with careful planning, research, and potentially legal representation, you can navigate this situation effectively. This guide will walk you through the necessary steps to protect your interests and respond appropriately. ### Step 1: Understand the Legal Context Before taking any action, familiarize yourself with basic legal concepts related to trespassing and personal injury lawsuits. Generally, property owners have a duty of care towards individuals on their premises, which varies depending on whether the person is an invitee, licensee, or trespasser. A trespasser is someone who enters another's property without permission, and the owner typically owes them minimal or no duty of care. However, there are exceptions when children are involved (the attractive nuisance doctrine) or if the landowner exhibits wanton disregard for human life. Key tip: Consult local laws and regulations regarding trespassing as they may vary by jurisdiction. ### Step 2: Gather Relevant Information Collect all available details about the incident, including: - Date and time of entry - Method of entry (locked door, broken window, etc.) - Specific location on your property where the injury occurred - Type of injury sustained - Any witnesses present during the event This information will help establish important facts surrounding the case and could prove useful in building your defense strategy. Key guideline: Be thorough and accurate in documenting these details to strengthen your position. ### Step 3: Assess Property Security Measures Review your existing security measures to determine if they were adequate at the time of the incident. Consider factors such as fencing, lighting, signage, alarms, and surveillance systems. If improvements could have been made, address those areas moving forward to prevent similar occurrences. Key tip: Implementing enhanced security measures now can demonstrate good faith efforts to maintain a safe environment. ### Step 4: Consult With An Attorney Given the complexities of legal proceedings, consulting with a qualified attorney experienced in premises liability cases is highly recommended. They can provide valuable insights into applicable laws, assess the strength of the plaintiff's claim, and recommend appropriate next steps. Key guideline: Seek professional advice early in the process to ensure you understand your rights and obligations. ### Step 5: Prepare a Response And I'll tell you how to spell the words. Title: How to Enable Scripting in Internet Explorer Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of enabling scripting in Internet Explorer. Enabling scripting allows dynamic content to be displayed in web pages, enhancing your browsing experience. However, it's important to note that enabling scripts can pose security risks if you visit untrusted websites. Always ensure you only enable scripts when necessary and from trusted sources. Step 1: Open Internet Explorer Start by launching Internet Explorer on your computer. This is typically found in your start menu or desktop shortcut. Step 2: Access Internet Options Click on the gear icon located at the top right corner of the browser window. A dropdown menu will appear; select 'Internet options'. This opens a new dialog box containing various settings related to your browser. Step 3: Navigate to Security Tab In the opened dialog box, locate and click on the 'Security' tab. Here, you can adjust security levels for different internet zones (Internet, Local intranet, Trusted sites, Restricted sites). Step 4: Select Zone & Custom Level Choose the zone for which you want to modify scripting settings, usually either 'Internet' or 'Trusted Sites'. After selecting the desired zone, click on the 'Custom level...' button below the 'Security level for this zone' slider. Key Tip: If you are unsure about what zone a website falls under, check its URL. Websites with familiar domain names like google.com fall under the 'Internet' category while those within your network may belong to 'Local intranet'. Step 5: Locate Active Scripting Option A new dialog box labeled 'Security Settings - <Zone Name>' appears. Scroll down until you find the 'Active Scripting' option under the 'Scripting' section. Step 6: Set Active Scripting to 'Enabled' By default, active scripting should be set to 'Disable', meaning scripts won't run. To change this, simply click on the radio button next to 'Enable'. Guideline: Remember, enabling active scripting increases risk as malicious scripts could potentially harm your system. Only enable it when absolutely needed and after confirming the site's trustworthiness. Step 7: Confirm Changes & Close Dialog Boxes Once you've enabled active scripting, click 'OK' at the bottom of both open windows to save changes and close them. The updated setting takes effect immediately. Conclusion: You have now successfully enabled scripting in Internet Explorer. Keep in mind that this action might expose you to potential threats if not managed carefully. Therefore, always exercise caution when visiting unfamiliar websites and consider using additional safety measures such as antivirus software. oops, does he have booboos in his hair? Hey diddle the cat and the fiddle the cow jumped over the moon. Let's say "hooray" for the letter A as it joins the Alphabet Parade. The seven letter A worksheets cover the short and long sounds made by the letter A, how to spot the letter A in printed words, and how to write the uppercase and lowercase versions of the letter A. Each worksheet also includes extra tips and activity suggestions you can try at home to help your child quickly learn all about the letter A and the sounds it makes. The letter A, like most vowels, has two sounds: a short sound and a long sound. The short letter A sound is the sound that begins the word apple. The long sound is simply the name of the letter, so you hear the long letter A sound in the word cake. I suggest introducing the short vowel sound before introducing the long vowel sound. In this way, your child can begin sounding out three-letter words that have the consonant-vowel-consonant formation (as in the word mat) whereas words with the long vowel sound typically include a "silent e" at the end which can by challenging for young children to understand (as in the word bake). The short letter A sound is made with the mouth slightly open and the tongue pushed forward in the mouth so that it just barely extends over the front, bottom teeth. Encourage your child to say this sound alone at first. Then, ask him to say the short letter A sound at the start of words such as apple, after, ask, and ant. Lastly, ask him to practice saying the short letter A sound when it is between two consonants, such as the words bat, can, mat, and nap. The long letter A sound is made with a similar mouth formation but the tongue does not extend as far forward in the mouth. Typically the long letter A sound is also a deeper (more baritone) sound than the short letter A sound. As when practicing the short letter A sound, ask your child to first practice making the long letter A sound by saying words where the sound appears in the starting position in a word such as acorn, ape and aim. Then ask your child to say words with the long letter A sound in the middle between two consonant sounds, such as cape, mate, and sail. For some fun practice with the long letter A sound, take all the face cards and aces from a deck of playing cards. Show your child the cards one at a time and have him call out what he sees, practicing the long letter A sound each time you show him an ace. When teaching your child how to write the uppercase letter A, it can be helpful to explain how the letter looks like a tent. Some children also call it an upside-down V with a line across the middle. While it is tempting to start at the bottom of the writing line and draw a tent, this letter takes three separate strokes. Ask your child to start at the top of the writing line and draw a diagonal line down to the bottom writing line. He must then lift his pencil and put it back on the starting point to draw the second diagonal line. Now he lifts his pencil for the third stroke which goes across the middle of the letter. Unlike the uppercase A, the lowercase a is written in one continuous stroke without lifting the pencil. First have your child draw a round loop. Then, without lifting his pencil, direct him to pause briefly to change direction and draw the straight line down. Coming Next Week: The Letter O. Drawing cartoon portraits can be a fun and rewarding activity that allows you to express your creativity and improve your drawing skills. This tutorial will provide you with a comprehensive guide on how to draw a basic cartoon portrait, including in-depth explanations, key tips, and guidelines to help you along the way. **Things You'll Need:** 1. Paper or sketchbook 2. Pencil (preferably HB or 2B) 3. Eraser 4. Colored pencils, markers, or watercolor (optional) 5. Ruler or straight edge (optional) 6. Graph paper or grid paper (optional) 7. Reference image(s) **Step 1: Choose Your Subject** Decide who you want to draw as a cartoon character. It could be a friend, family member, famous person, or even an original character. Having a reference image will make this process easier. If possible, choose an image where the subject is facing forward and their facial features are clearly visible. **Step 2: Outline Basic Shapes** Lightly sketch basic shapes such as circles, ovals, rectangles, and triangles to create a simplified version of the face and head structure. These guides will help ensure accurate proportions and alignment throughout the drawing process. * The top circle represents the forehead and hairline. * Below the first circle, add two smaller overlapping circles for eyes. Make sure there's enough space between them for the nose and mouth. * A horizontal line connecting the bottom edges of both eye circles indicates the location of the eyebrows. * Add another slightly curved line below the eyebrow line for the bridge of the nose. * Sketch a vertical guideline down the center of the face to assist with symmetry. ![Basic Face Structure](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/a/ad/Draw-a-Cartoon-Face-Step-2-Version-3.jpg/aid1089688-v4- [tells MOT he knows the middle one.] Blank makes it possible for you to move. In some math problems like the equation for a line, there can be more than one variable ("x" and "y" in this case). To find points on the line, one variable is changed. The variable that is changed is called the "independent" variable. Then the math is done to make a number. The number that is made is called the "dependent" variable. Most of the time the independent variable is written as "x" and the dependent variable is written as "y", for example, in y = 3x + 1. This is often put on a graph, using an "x" axis (going left and right) and a "y" axis (going up and down). It can also be written in function form: f(x) = 3x + 1. So in this example, we could put in 5 for "x" and get y = 16. Put in 2 for "x" would get y=7. And 0 for "x" would get y=1. So there would be a line going thru the points (5,16), (2,7), and (0,1) as seen in the graph to the right. Title: How to Find the Moon on Google Earth Difficulty: Easy Step 1: Open Google Earth To begin your quest to find the moon on Google Earth, you'll first need to open the application on your device. Google Earth is available for download on various platforms such as Windows, macOS, Linux, Chrome OS, iOS, and Android. Once installed, launch the program to continue. Key Tips & Guidelines: Make sure your internet connection is stable since Google Earth relies heavily on online data to provide high-quality satellite imagery. Step 2: Familiarize Yourself with the Interface Spend some time familiarizing yourself with the user interface of Google Earth. Key components include the search bar at the top left corner, navigation controls (compass rose and tilt slider) at the top right corner, and layers panel at the bottom right corner. The primary map view occupies most of the screen space. Understanding the layout will help you navigate efficiently through the application. Step 3: Navigate to the Layers Panel Locate the layers panel at the bottom right corner of the window. This feature allows you to add or remove different overlays that can enhance your experience. Step 4: Enable the Sky Layer In the layers panel, expand the 'More' section by clicking on the arrow next to it. Scroll down until you see 'Sky' under the 'Featured Content' heading. Check the box beside 'Sky' to enable this layer. Enabling the sky layer incorporates celestial bodies like the sun, moon, stars, and planets into your Google Earth view. However, please note that due to technical limitations, these objects may not always appear accurately positioned based on real-world coordinates. Step 5: Use the Search Bar to Locate the Moon Click on the search bar located at the top left corner of the window. Type 'Moon' without quotes and press enter. Wait for Google Earth to perform the search. Searching for the moon directs Google Earth to center its view towards our natural satellite whenever possible. However, keep in mind that the exact positioning might be off due to the reasons mentioned earlier. Step 6: Adjust View Settings if Necessary If the moon isn't immediately visible after performing the search, try adjusting the view settings using the navigation controls. You can rotate the view around by clicking and dragging the compass rose. Zoom in or out using the scroll wheel on your mouse or touchpad gestures if available. Additionally, you can utilize the tilt slider to change the angle of viewing. While these actions won't affect the moon's virtual location, they could potentially make it more discernible amidst other graphical elements. By following these steps, you should now have successfully found the moon within Google Earth! Although locating the moon virtually doesn't serve any scientific purpose, exploring the universe from the comfort of your home certainly provides an intriguing perspective. The period after World War 2 saw more observatories where large and accurate telescopes are built and operated at good observing sites, normally by governments. For example, Bernard Lovell began radio astronomy at Jodrell Bank using leftover military radar equipment. By 1957, the site had the largest steerable radio telescope in the world. Similarly, the end of the 1960s saw the start of the building of dedicated observatories at Mauna Kea in Hawaii, a good site for visible and infra-red telescopes thanks to its high altitude and clear skies. The time between pulses (often called "pulse rate time", or PRT) sets the limit on how far away an object can be detected. That distance is called the "range" of a radar transmitter and receiver. Radar transmitters and receivers use long PRT's to find the distance to objects that are far away. Long PRT's makes it possible to accurately determine the distance to the moon, for example. Fast PRT's are used to detect objects that are much closer, like ships at sea, high flying aircraft, or to determine the speed of fast moving automobiles on highways. Imagine you have a garden hose with water flowing out of it. Now, let's say we want to change the shape of the nozzle attached to the hose, but keep the same amount of water flowing out. To do this, we need to understand how the speed of the water changes when we alter the shape of the nozzle. Let's introduce two imaginary planes, called the z-plane and the -plane, where each point on these planes represents a different shape of the nozzle. When we transform the nozzle shape from the z-plane to the -plane, something interesting happens to the water's speed: its magnitude gets multiplied or divided by a certain factor based on how much smaller or larger the new nozzle shape is compared to the original one! This means that even though the nozzle has changed shapes, the overall "force" of the water coming out remains the same - isn't nature fascinating? Now, imagine drawing a small loop around the nozzle tip in both the z-plane and the -plane; the total "energy" of the water swirling inside those loops will remain constant regardless of the nozzle's shape! It simply tells us that changing the shape doesn't affect the total energy stored in the moving water near the nozzle area. To make things more tangible, think of this concept like shrinking or expanding a balloon full of air without adding or removing any air from it. You would still feel the same force pushing against your hand, whether the balloon is big or small because the pressure (air molecules bouncing back) stays the same despite size differences. Similarly, our magical water nozzle conserves its power during shape transitions thanks to this amazing mathematical rule! seventy-one seventy-two seventy-three seventy-eight... Title: How to Feel Drunk Without Drinking Disclaimer: This article is intended for educational purposes only and should not be used as a guide to engage in dangerous or harmful behavior. Always prioritize your health and safety. The closest evolutionary relatives of humans, chimpanzees and bonobos, have either one or no copies of the gene for producing salivary amylase. A duplication event of the AMY1 gene led to the production of amylase in the saliva. The same event occurred independently in rodents. This shows the importance of salivary amylase in organisms that eat relatively large amounts of starch. You need ta be more careful how you swing that head around. How many you got in here already? In the 1860s, James Clerk Maxwell and William Thomson (later known as Lord Kelvin) suggested a system with three base units - length, mass, and time. Other units would be derived from those three base units. Later, this suggestion would be used to create the centimetre-gram-second system of units (CGS), which used the centimetre as the base unit for length, the gram as the base unit for mass, and the second as the base unit for time. It also added the dyne as the base unit for force and the erg as the base unit for energy. 1. Help others who are being bullied. Be a friend, even if this person is not yet your friend. Go over to them. Let them know how you think they are feeling. Walk with them. Help them to talk to an adult about what just happened. (Just think for a moment about how great this would be if someone did this for you when you were being picked on or hurt!) 2. Stop untrue or harmful messages from spreading. If someone tells you a rumor that you know is untrue or sends you a message that is hurtful to someone else, stand up and let the person know this is wrong. Think about how you would feel if someone spread an untrue rumor about you. Don't laugh, send the message on to friends, or add to the story. Make it clear that you do not think that kind of behavior is cool or funny. 3. Get friends involved. Share this site www.schoolclimate.org/bullybust and other related sites with friends. Let people know that you are an upstander and encourage them to be one too. Sign the Stand Up Pledge, and make it an everyday commitment for you and your friends. 4. Make friends outside of your circle. Eat lunch with someone who is alone. Show support for a person who is upset at school, by asking them what is wrong or bringing them to an adult who can help. 5. Be aware of the bullying policy at your school and keep it in mind when you witness bullying. If there isn't a policy, get involved or ask teachers or front office staff to speak about how you can reduce bullying. 6. If someone is new at your school, make an effort to introduce them around and make them comfortable. Imagine how you would feel leaving your friends and coming to a new school. 7. Refuse to be a "bystander". If you see friends or classmates laughing along with the bully, tell them that they are contributing to the problem. Let them know that by laughing they are also bullying the victim. 8. Respect others' differences and help others to respect differences. It's cool for people to be different - that's what makes all of us unique. Join a diversity club at school to help promote tolerance in your school. 9. Ask your teacher or principal to develop a bullying program or project that will help reduce bullying in school. Ask them to bring together a team of students, parents and teachers to meet as "Stand Up Ambassadors" to talk about bullying on a regular basis and share stories and support. Talk about the "hot spots" where bullying most likely occurs (ex. the bus, bathroom, an unmonitored hallway) and what can be done on a school level to make sure students and teachers are on the same page about bullying. 10. Learn more about bullying. For example: Why do kids bully? Where does bullying take place most often in your school? What are the effects of bullying? Why are people afraid to get involved? Understanding this information will help you if you are bullied and will help you to stand up to bullies if a friend or classmate is being bullied. [continues to look at the picture in the book] You put it back on the table. - slide 1 of 3 Materials and Preparation A topographic map shows the various elevations of a landscape by using contour lines. These contour lines connect the areas that have the same elevation. The following materials will be needed to make the topographic map: - block of modeling clay - piece of wax paper - rubber band - metric ruler - brown and blue colored pencils - butter knife Make a small mountain out of the modeling clay. It needs to be a little over 10 cm high and should include gullies or valleys where streams would run. Place the mountain on the wax paper. Use the rubber band to attach the pencil 2 cm from the end of the ruler as shown in this picture. - slide 2 of 3 - Hold the ruler upright, with the pencil point touching the clay mountain. - Mark a line completely around the clay mountain by moving the ruler/pencil around the clay mountain. - Move the pencil up to the 4 cm mark on the ruler and repeat step 2. - Using the pencil, mark the contour line moving the pencil up 2 cm at a time until you reach the top of the clay mountain. - Remove the wax paper and place the clay on a sheet of blank paper. Trace around the outside edge with the brown pencil. Take the mountain off the paper and place it back on the wax paper. - Using the butter knife, carefully cut the mountain at the 2 cm elevation line, by moving the knife all around the mountain. Make the cut as flat as possible, trying not to change the shape of the mountain. - Remove the wax paper and the bottom slice that you cut off of the mountain. Set the slice aside and place the remainder of the mountain in the center of the first contour line that you traced on the paper. Trace the edge as you did before, and the put the mountain back on the wax paper. - Repeat steps 6-8 for the remainder of the contour lines to complete your topographic map of your mountain. - Label the first contour you drew 0 cm, the next 2 cm, and continue to the top of the mountain. Use the blue pencil to draw streams in the gullies or valleys. - slide 3 of 3 Have the students discuss the following: - Is your topographic map an accurate representation of the mountain? - What is the elevation of your mountain? - Draw a straight line down one side from the top of your mountain to the bottom. Calculate the average slope along this line. As a challenge, ask students to trade topographic maps with a classmate. Have each student get more modeling clay and try to recreate their partner's mountain using their topographic map as a guide. Students can place together the sections cut from their original mountain and compare it with their partner's recreation. Discuss how close they came to the original.
