The Moringa tree is native to northern India, but today it is common throughout the tropical and sub-tropical regions of Asia, Africa, and Latin America. Moringa trees grow easily from seeds or cuttings. They grow quickly even in poor soil and bloom 8 months after planting. To grow from a cutting: After the trees have stopped producing fruits each year, branches need to be cut off so that fresh growth may take place. These branches are excellent for growing - Make a cutting at least 1" (2.5cm) in diameter and at least six feet (1.8m) long. - Dig a hole 3 ft. (1m) x 3 ft. (1m) and 3 ft. (1m) deep. - Place cutting in this hole and fill with a mixture of soil, sand and composted manure. Pack firmly around base of the cutting. Form a slight dome or cone shape, sloping down away from the cutting. It is desirable that water not touch the stem of the new tree. - Water generously, but do not drown the cutting in water. In India, the custom is to put some cow dung on top of the open end of the cutting. This is an excellent way to protect the cutting from pests. To grow from seed: Moringa seeds have no dormancy periods and can be planted as soon as they In the ground: It is best to plant the seeds directly where the tree is intended to grow and not transplant the seedling. The young seedlings are fragile and often cannot survive transplanting. To plant seeds directly in the ground: - Choose an area with light and sandy soil, not heavy with clay or water-logged. - Dig holes 1 ft (30 cm) square and 1 ft deep. Back-fill the holes with loose soil. Compost or manure will help the tree grow better, even though Moringa trees can grow in poor soils. - Plant 3 to 5 seeds in each hole, 2 in. (5 cm) apart. Plant the seeds no deeper than three times the width of the seed (approximately in. or 1.5 cm -- the size of one's thumbnail). - Keep the soil moist enough so that the top soil will not dry and choke the emerging saplings, but it should not be too wet or else the seeds can drown and rot. - When the saplings are four to six inches tall, keep the healthiest sapling in the ground and remove the rest. Termites and nematodes can kill a young sapling. Take measures to protect saplings from these two dangers. Note: If the soil is heavy, dig a larger hole of up to 3 ft (90 cm) in diameter and 3 ft deep, and backfill with 1 part sand and 2 parts original soil. Added compost or manure will help. When it is not possible to plant directly in the ground, use the following method: 4. Remove extra seedlings, leaving one in each bag. Seedlings can be transplanted after four to six months when they are 2-3 ft (60-90 cm) high. The ground where the trees are to be planted should be light and sandy, not heavy with clay or water-logged. Dig a hole 1 ft (30 cm) square and 1 ft deep. Backfill with loose soil. Adding compost or manure will help the trees grow better. - Water the planting holes one day before transplanting the seedlings. - Plant seedlings in the late afternoon to avoid the hot sun the first day. - Make a hole in the pit to accept all soil in the bag. Carefully cut open the sack and place the seedling in the planting hole. Be careful to keep the soil around the seedling's roots intact. - Pack soil around the seedling base. - Water only lightly for the first few days. If the seedlings fall over, tie them to a stick for support. Protect young saplings from termites and nematodes. back to Moringa Tree back to What Can I Do back to How do I grow the Moringa tree? Sounding out accuracy |Activity Type: Build Accuracy| |Activity Form: Standard| |Group Size: Small Group, Whole Class| |Length: 5 minutes| |Materials: None for Whole Class. For Small Groups, download 3x5 index cards| |Goal: Given a written regular word, sound out and then say the word ( abc -> "aaabbbcc" -> "abc" )| |Items: The words specified in the lesson titles, here| What to do - Write the items on the board. (For small groups, you can also use the index card version of the words.) - Let's sound out some words. When I touch each letter, you say its sound and keep saying it until I touch the next letter. Don't stop between sounds. After you sound out the word correctly, say it fast. - My turn first. Start saying the first sound as you touch just beneath the first letter. Hold each sound for about one second. For example, aaammm. Now I say it fast. What's the word? Am. - Your turn. Ready? Make sure everyone is looking at you, then touch the first letter and let the students sound out without you. Students: aaammm. What's the word? Students: am. - Continue with the other items on the board. - For words beginning with stop sounds, pause very briefly on the stop sound and slightly longer than usual on the next sound: caaaannn. Avoid cuhaaannn and c-(pause)aaannn. - Call on each student to sound out the words individually. Point to the words in a random order to avoid students memorizing a sequence. (If you are going through flash cards in a deck, shuffle the deck for each student.) In an Activity Log, make a note of students who continue to have trouble. If some students can say the word slowly but not fast, you may need to go over oral blending with them. View this video to see how to improve sounding out accuracy. Find Antler Sheds: A Comprehensive Guide ========================================== Antler sheds are the naturally shed antlers from male deer, elk, moose, and caribou. Finding these antlers can be an exciting and rewarding hobby for nature enthusiasts, hunters, or anyone who enjoys being outdoors. This extensive guide will take you through the process of finding antler sheds, providing detailed explanations, key tips, and guidelines along the way. ### 1. Understand Antler Shedding Patterns *Explanation*: Deer, elk, moose, and caribou shed their antlers annually as part of their natural life cycle. Learning about when and why they shed their antlers is crucial to successfully finding them. *Key Tips*: + Antlers begin growing in early spring (late March-April) and reach full growth by late summer (August). + Testosterone levels start dropping after the breeding season, causing pedicles (antler bases) to shrink and detach from the skull. + Most species shed their antlers during winter (December-February), but this varies depending on location, age, and overall health of the animal. ### 2. Choose the Right Time and Location *Explanation*: Timing your search based on local weather patterns and knowing where to look greatly increase your chances of success. *Key Guidelines*: + Look for areas with high concentrations of deer, elk, moose, or caribou populations. + Focus on searching after heavy snowfall or following significant temperature changes that weaken tree branches, causing them to break under the weight of the antlers. + Consider public lands, wildlife management areas, state parks, or national forests as potential locations. Always obtain necessary permits before entering restricted areas. ### 3. Observe Animal Trails and Bedding Areas *Explanation*: Animals tend to travel along well-worn paths, making trails and bedding sites excellent places to find sheds. *Key Tips*: + Identify heavily used trails using tracks, scat, rubbings, or feeding signs such as stripped bark or browsed vegetation. + Locate bedding areas characterized by flattened grasses or leaves, indicating frequent use by animals resting or ruminating. When and why are they used? If you have trouble remembering the difference between semicolons and colons, you're not alone. These punctuation marks are just tools that you use to help the reader understand your sentence structure. Each has a specific function. Once you understand what each is designed to do, you won't have trouble recognizing or using it. This tutorial will teach you all you need to know about semicolons and colons. To begin, make sure you know the difference between the two: A semicolon looks like a period on top of a comma. A semicolon is used to divide up the sentence--to signal the end of one part and the beginning of another part. It's more than a comma, but less than a period, so it combines the two into a single punctuation mark. A colon looks like one period on top of another. A colon says that you have introduced what will follow. Think of a colon as a pair of eyes, looking ahead to what's coming next. Now let's look at how these punctuation marks are used, starting with the semicolon. Supposing there was only one thing nothing more what should it be? Drawing the letter "S" can be both fun and challenging, but with practice and patience, anyone can master this classic cursive character. This tutorial will guide you through drawing an elegant, cool-looking "S," emphasizing proper technique, form, and flow. **Step 1: Familiarize yourself with basic shapes** Before diving into drawing the actual letter, take some time to understand its structure. An 'S' is essentially two overlapping circles connected by straight lines. Memorizing these components will make your final product look more uniform and polished. **Step 2: Choose your writing instrument** Select a pen or pencil that suits your preference. A fine tip creates cleaner lines while a thicker one might produce bolder strokes. For beginners, graphite pencils are recommended due to their ease of erasure. **Step 3: Start with a light guideline** Using a ruler, draw two horizontal parallel lines about half an inch apart as a base for consistency. These lines serve as guides for maintaining equal height throughout your 'S'. They also help prevent slanting which often results in unbalanced letters. **Step 4: Begin at the top** Place the tip of your pen just above the middle point of the left line (guideline). Now gently slope downwards towards the right until you reach the bottom part of the same guideline. Keep practicing this motion till you get comfortable with creating smooth curves without lifting the pen off the paper. **Step 5: Cross over to the next curve** Without picking up your pen, move directly across to start the second curl. Position the pen slightly below the midpoint of the right guideline, then arc upward meeting the first curve around the center. Remember, no lift! Maintain continuous contact between the page and the pen. **Step 6: Connect the loops** Still keeping your pen on the paper, extend a thin line from where the two curves meet, continuing beneath them. Curve this line back up to connect with the end of the initial stroke made in Step 4. Congratulations! You've drawn your first cool 'S'. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** - Always maintain even pressure when drawing. Too much force could tear the paper; too little may result in faint lines. - Try starting slow and gradually increase speed once you become confident. - Practice regularly to improve muscle memory and control over the pen/pencil. - If using ink, remember there's no room for errors. Either practice with pencil first or embrace imperfections as part of the learning process. - Don't rush. Good handwriting takes time to develop. Patience and persistence are key. By following these steps meticulously, you'll soon find yourself comfortably sketching out sleek, stylish 'S'es. Happy drawing! But it looks a little bit like a watermelon you're right. It is an agency of the United Nations (UN). - slide 1 of 6 These adjective games are a great way for students to learn more about this part of speech. Try these adjective activities at home or in the classroom to keep learning fun! - slide 2 of 6 Your students are sure to have had experience playing "I Spy." Turning the challenge into adjective games puts a new twist on an old classic! Have a student come up to the front of the class and say, "I spy with my little eye something that is " and name the color of the object. Then have the students in one row take turns asking questions in the form "Is it [adjective]?" Make sure that students are using only adjectives to ask the questions. After everyone in the row has a chance to ask a question, have students write down the object that they think is being referred to. Then have the student in the front of the room reveal the object. Any student who guessed correctly scores one point. Repeat these adjective activities with another student and the second row in the classroom. - slide 3 of 6 Have students make Mad Libs for each other. To do this, have them write short stories (or take some that they've already written). Encourage them to highlight all of the adjectives in the stories. Then have the class come up with a list of adjectives - silly ones and serious ones - and write them on the board. Have each student plug the adjectives into their story. Encourage students to share the finished stories with the class. - slide 4 of 6 Have each student write the name of an object on the top of a piece of paper. Arrange the students' desks in a circle around the perimeter of the room, with one paper on each desk. Then have students walk from desk to desk, adding an adjective to each paper as they pass. The adjectives they choose should describe each object. Then have students retrieve their papers and use the adjectives to write a poem about the object. - slide 5 of 6 The Longest List Divide the class into four or five groups. Give each group a small object (fruit, candy, school supplies, or pieces of nature work well), and have them make a list of as many adjectives as they can to describe the object. Give students fifteen seconds to create the list, and then have them pass the object to the next group and repeat the process. After each group has gotten each adjective, tally up their lists to see which group has the most adjectives. Have the winning group share their adjectives with the class. - slide 6 of 6 As an extension activity, and to test comprehension, challenge your students to come up with the own adjective games. You can then pair with another class, or even students in younger grades, and swap adjective activities. Learning has never been so much fun! See this article for some fun adjective lesson plans. Grammar Activities: Fun With Adjectives This series will include several parts of speech activities and games for each of the following parts of speech: nouns, verbs, pronouns, adjectives, and adverbs. Use these activities to inject some fun into your everyday grammar lessons! Two dollars ain't going to buy no vitamins around here. So he doesn't have any of the same things that you guys have. Kids' Questions About a Kid's BMI Every time you go for a check-up, does the nurse ask you to step on a scale to check your weight? Does she check your height to see how tall you are? She's measuring to see how much you've grown. She uses your weight and height to figure out your BMI, which is short for body mass index. The nurse at your school might check your BMI, too. What a Kid's BMI Shows BMI is a way to measure how much body fat you have. Doctors check BMI because weighing too much for your height can cause health problems like a broken hip, knee pain, heart disease, or diabetes. To find out your BMI, you need to know: - Your age - Your gender (whether you are a boy or girl) - Your height and weight With this information, a nurse or doctor can look at a chart to find your BMI. There are also BMI calculators on web sites that anyone can use. Kids need a kid's BMI calculator that is different than the kind adults use. Comparing Yourself to Other Kids Kids' BMIs are a bit different from adult BMIs because most kids are still growing. Kids get a BMI percentile. A percentile tells you how your weight compares to kids like you -- the same age and gender. For example, a boy with a 75th BMI percentile is heavier than 75 out of 100 boys his age. A high BMI (85th percentile or higher) means you weigh more than expected for your height. A low BMI means you weigh less than expected for your height. That's OK. You may be one of the lighter or one of the heavier kids in your age group. If your doctor thinks your weight could be a problem, he will talk with you and your parents about ways to make sure you are healthy. What to Do About BMI Remember that your body is growing and changing. That means the BMI you have now won't be the BMI that you'll have your whole life. You'll go through growth spurts, and your height and weight will change. As your body changes, your BMI will change, too. Don't go on a diet or stop eating food if you're worried that your BMI is too high. Your doctor will talk to you and your parents if you need to make any changes because of your BMI. Never go on a diet by yourself! It is important to always eat well and stay active so your body is happy and healthy. Remember to: - Eat lots of fruits and vegetables. Try to have five servings every day. - Try not to drink sugary drinks and sodas. Instead, drink water or reduced-fat milk. - Get away from that TV and move! Too much screen time means less time moving. - Move for at least 60 minutes a day. It doesn't have to be all at once. Walk, run, dance, or bike whenever you can. Instead of me asking you questions you ask me questions. It's like it's like the puzzles you have at home. She carries them into the house and puts them on the table. Has she seen any in real life? The accusative case is used for a direct object, the dative case for an indirect object. The instrumental case is used for the tool or instrument with which something is done. The prepositional case is used after certain prepositions, such as "in" and "on", but other prepositions may require the use of other cases. Each case has other uses than those listed. A daily convocation of people, without refreshments or any extraneous object but the simple pleasure of seeing and talking with each other, is a thing that can scarcely be understood in English or American society. Social entertainment presupposes in the Anglo-Saxon mind something to eat, and not only something, but a great deal. Enormous dinners or great suppers constitute the entertainment. Nobody seems to have formed the idea that the talking--the simple exchange of social feelings--is, of itself, the entertainment, and that being together is the pleasure. Title: How to Donate to Goodwill Goodwill is a nonprofit organization that provides job training, employment placement services, and other community-based programs for people who have barriers preventing them from obtaining jobs. By donating items you no longer need or use, you can help support these efforts while also decluttering your home. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to donate to Goodwill. ### Step 1: Gather Items for Donation Key Tip: Before gathering items, familiarize yourself with what Goodwill accepts as donations. Generally, they accept clothing, household goods, electronics, furniture, books, and more. However, there are some restrictions such as hazardous materials, weapons, and recalled items. Check their website or contact your local Goodwill store for specific details about acceptable donations. Guideline: Only donate clean, usable items. If an item isn't suitable for donation due to its condition, consider repairing it if possible, recycling it, or disposing of it properly. ### Step 2: Sort and Organize Your Donations Key Tip: Group similar items together (e.g., all shirts in one bag, kitchenware in another). This will make sorting easier at the donation center and may even speed up the checkout process. Guideline: Make sure all pairs are complete. For example, don't give away just one sock; pair it with its match first. Also, ensure that multi-piece sets like dishes or toys are intact and ready for reuse. ### Step 3: Determine Where to Donate Key Tip: Look up nearby Goodwill locations online. Some cities have multiple stores or drop-off points. Choose the most convenient option based on location and hours of operation. Guideline: Many Goodwills offer pickup services for large furniture donations. Schedule this in advance if needed. ### Step 4: Prepare Your Donation Receipt Key Tip: When dropping off donations, request a receipt from the attendant. Goodwill provides a standard form detailing the type and quantity of items donated. Keep this receipt safe because it could be tax deductible! Guideline: Note down estimated values of major items according to current market prices. IRS guidelines suggest using thrift store value estimates found in resources like TurboTax ItsDeductible or Salvation Army Valuation Guide. ### Step 5: Deliver Your Donations Key Tip: Upon arrival at the chosen Goodwill location, look for signs directing you to the donation area. You might find bins, carts, or attendants ready to assist you. Guideline: Remember to lift safely when carrying heavy boxes or bags. Bend your knees, keep your back straight, and use your leg muscles instead of your back to lift. ### Step 6: Claim Tax Deductions (Optional) Key Tip: At the end of the year, compile all your donation receipts and report them on your taxes under charitable contributions. Consult a tax professional if necessary. Guideline: To qualify for a tax deduction, donations must exceed $250 in total value. Additionally, individual items valued over $500 require appraisals. Always consult a tax advisor regarding your unique situation. By following these steps, you'll successfully contribute to Goodwill's mission while clearing out unnecessary belongings. Not only does this practice promote sustainability by reducing waste, but it also supports individuals seeking employment opportunities within your community. Baby Bear found a rocket in the closet under the stairs. The Thinker () (1902) is a bronze statue. It was made by the sculptor Auguste Rodin. It depicts a man sitting and thinking with his arms bent on his knee and his chin on his hand. Rodin first called the statue "The Poet". It was part of a commission by the Museum of Decorative Arts in Paris to create a huge gate based on the epic poem "The Divine Comedy" of Dante. Each of the statues in the piece represented one of the main characters in the poem. "The Thinker" was originally meant to depict Dante himself in front of the Gates of Hell considering his great poem. Over twenty casts of the sculpture exist. They are now in museums around the world. Some of those copies are enlarged versions of the original work, while others have somewhat different proportions. The substantial happiness, which enlarges and civilizes the mind, may be compared to the pleasure experienced in roving through nature at large, inhaling the sweet gale natural to the clime; while the reveries of a feverish imagination continually sport themselves in gardens full of aromatic shrubs, which cloy while they delight, and weaken the sense of pleasure they gratify. Rocks change over time depending on how much pressure, weathering, and they get. Pressure is the pushing down of top layers of rock on lower layers of rock. We use football as an example of this. Let s say that the football falls on the ground and a player leaps on top of it. Think about the player. He is just lying there, like a top layer. He is feeling pretty good because he is the top layer. Other players see this and jump on top of him, hoping to get the ball away. First one player leaps on top, and the player with the ball feels pressed down a little. Little by little, as more players pile on top, he is pressed down so much that he feels pushed into the ground and flattened. This is what we mean by pressure. Weathering is how the wind, rain, snow, etc. affect the rock. Weathering isn t how one rain storm affects a rock. It s what lots of rain storms do to it over a long, long time. Most of the time, weathering causes bits and pieces of rock to separate from larger chunks and washes them into streams and rivers. Erosion moves the pieces of rock from place to place using gravity, running water, or weathering. Erosion wears away rock over lots of time. Rocks go through something called the rock cycle. This is where rocks change over a lot of time. Time, to a rock, is thousands and millions of years. A rock can begin as one kind of rock and then change many times before it is mined. Underground heat and the pressure we were talking about, plus weathering can change rocks over millions of There are three kinds of rocks: and gemstones are mined in many different ways depending on how close to the surface they are found. For example, if you found a certain kind of rock at the bottom of streams or rivers, it would be mined using surface mining like placer mining. If it was about six feet underground, a different kind of surface open pit mining would be used. In open pit mining, layers of dirt are taken off the top until they get down to the rock they want to mine. If it is deep in the Earth, different underground mining are used. Mining companies use the kind of mining that will get them to the rocks, gemstones the cheapest. Mining companies will not mine for anything that won t make them money. Each of the rocks that we included on our site will include the following information: What it is and where it s formed, how and where it is mined, and what its uses are. Mutagenic chemicals increase errors in DNA replication, often by interfering with the structure of base-pairing, while UV radiation induces mutations by causing damage to the DNA structure. Chemical damage to DNA occurs naturally as well, and cells use DNA repair mechanisms to repair mismatches and breaks in DNA-nevertheless, the repair sometimes fails to return the DNA to its original sequence. Title: How to Become Famous on Fantage Fantage is an online virtual world for kids that allows them to play games, chat with friends, customize their avatar, and even become famous within the community. Here's our comprehensive guide on becoming famous on Fantage! Step 1: Create Your Avatar Create a unique and attractive avatar to make a good first impression. Choose from various hairstyles, clothes, accessories, and more to stand out among other users. A distinctive appearance can help you gain attention and followers. However, remember not to violate any rules or regulations while creating your look. Step 2: Interact With Others Interaction plays a significant role in gaining popularity. Visit different rooms, participate in mini-games, join conversations, and showcase kindness towards others. Being friendly and helpful will encourage people to add you as a friend, increasing your visibility. Also, try attending events such as parties, concerts, or fashion shows where many players gather. Key Tip: Avoid negative behavior like spamming chats or being rude to others; this could lead to warnings or bans which would harm your chances of fame. Step 3: Earn Stars and Fame Stars are the currency of Fantage, used to buy items from the Star Catalog. The higher your star count, the more respect you earn from fellow members. To get stars, engage in activities like completing quests, winning at minigames, selling unused items, or trading with others. Additionally, work on earning 'fame points'. These are awarded based on your participation in certain activities around Fantage. High amounts of fame allow you to unlock special features and privileges unavailable to regular members. Step 4: Build Your Home Having a well-decorated home is another way to attract visitors. Use furniture, wallpaper, flooring, etc., available in the Home Store to design a creative space reflecting your personality. Invite friends over and host small gatherings. Remember, every visitor contributes to boosting your reputation. Step 5: Participate in Contests Regularly check announcements for upcoming competitions related to art, writing, designing, etc. Winning these contests gives you recognition along with rewards. Even if you don't win, participating consistently demonstrates dedication and passion, making you noticeable. Step 6: Start a Fan Club Once you have gained enough popularity, consider starting your own fan club. This provides a platform for fans to interact directly with you, enhancing your influence within the Fantage community. Managing a fanclub requires responsibility, so ensure you can handle tasks like posting updates regularly, organizing meetings, and maintaining positivity. Step 7: Stay Active Consistency matters when trying to maintain fame. Regular visits keep you engaged with the community, ensuring constant growth in your network. Moreover, active users tend to be remembered more than those who log in occasionally. Guideline: Balance your time between offline life and Fantage to avoid burnout or neglecting real-life responsibilities. Title: "Comparing Fixed Points: A Fun Grade School Experiment" Have you ever played with magnets before? Imagine having two magnets that attract each other. When you move them closer together, they pull stronger, and when you move them apart, the pull gets weaker. Now let's think of these two magnets as two special machines (functions) that take numbers as input and give new numbers as output. These machines have something interesting known as "fixed points." A fixed point is a number that doesn't change after going through the machine; whatever value you put into the machine comes out the same! Let's call our first machine "Friendly Freddy," which has a fixed point at 0.5 because no matter what number you feed it near 0.5, it returns 0.5 itself. Our second machine, "Magnetic Mary," also has a fixed point but slightly off from Friendly Freddy's-at 0.6. This means feeding Magnetic Mary values around 0.6 gives her back the very same 0.6. You may wonder, "How far apart are their fixed points?" In our magnet example, since they're just one tenth apart, we say there's a small difference. But how do mathematicians figure this out accurately? And what happens if these machines were more complicated than friendly magnets? Well, imagine now that instead of moving physical magnets closer or farther, someone tells us how strongly connected these machines are by measuring their maximum difference when comparing outputs. They say that even though the machines act differently, their max difference is always less than or equal to 0.1. It turns out that whenever the distances between corresponding parts inside both machines are smaller than this limit, so too are the differences between their fixed points! To illustrate this using our magnetic friends, picture yourself holding a rope connecting them while standing on top of a hill. The length of your rope represents the max difference allowed between outputs. Now walk towards either friend without letting go of the rope until you find their magical spots where they don't want to budge anymore. Notice how close those fixed points are compared to the length of your rope! So remember, whether playing with real-life magnets or exploring mathematical functions, understanding relationships between key features helps uncover hidden connections. By studying limits of function differences, mathematicians unlock secrets about how closely related things like fixed points can be! Testers and How They Work Basically, they consist of a neon light bulb that is attached to two leads used for checking if a circuit is "hot" or "dead". When you press these two leads into an outlet, the bulb will light if the circuit is "hot" or on. If it doesn't light, then the circuit is "dead" or off. Always check to see if the tester is working properly by checking a circuit that you know is working properly before moving on. To double-check that an outlet is actually off, remove the outlet cover and test the screws on the sides of the outlet. You can also plug a lamp or vacuum into the outlet just to put your mind at ease. 2. Outlets and Testing A typical outlet has three holes built into it. The shorter straight slot is the "hot" lead. The longer straight slot is the "neutral" lead. The slot that looks like a small circle hole is the ground.To test the ground, test between the "hot" and "ground" slots. If the circuit is working and you have a good "ground" connection, the tester will light. The tester will also light if you test between the "hot" and "neutral" slots. Plug-in circuit testers are available that will test your circuit for you via three neon lights. They test for an open neutral, lack of a ground, wires on the wrong terminals, and no power. 3. Switches and Testing To test a switch, remove the cover plate and check from one of the screws on the side of the switch to the bare copper wire (ground) or the metal box. Keep in mind that the box may not be grounded, especially if it's a plastic box. 4. Testing Light Fixtures When checking light fixture wiring, take down the light and using a "tick-tracer", test the circuit to see if it working. This tester lights when you place it close to a wire that has current flowing through it. To double-check the circuit, first turn off the power to that circuit. Now, remove the wire nuts from both the black "hot" wires and the white "neutral" wires. Separate these sets of wires so that they are not touching one another. Turn the circuit back on and check between the black and white wires with the voltmeter or neon tester. Be careful not to touch the exposed wires. The voltmeter should show a reading of around 120 volts. Likewise, the neon tester should light if the circuit is working properly. Looked at all the nice stuff XXX. She had a box of colours, but her brother Timmy insisted on using the green pastel. Lucy was selfish and argued with him, thinking that she should be the one to use it. But she agreed with Timmy, not wanting Timmy to be disappointed. A week went by and Lucy tried her luck. She mixed many colours together to create a beautiful mural, showing a bright sunshine and a lake with swans and pink flowers. She suddenly ran out of colours to use and realised that the only colour she had left was the green pastel. Lucy was surprised and thankful to her brother, who had insisted on using the green pastel instead. That day, Lucy learned a lesson. She realised that it was not always necessary to be selfish, instead, it was far more beneficial to put others' ideas first. Had she not listened to her brother, she would not have completed her vibrant mural. The moral of this story is to never be too selfish and always take the time to listen to the ideas of others. No I don't know how to make a monster. Why is the shadow on the Moon the shape it is? Why is it that during some phases of the moon (1st quarter and 3rd quarter) show a crescent shadow and during mid phase the shadow is a straight line? This is kind of a neat question and it's to do with illuminating spheres and the angles that you look at them with respect to the light source. You could try to an experiment with some help from your friends if you have a ball and a flashlight - it is probably the best way to understand it. One of you has to be the sun (the one with the flashlight) the other the moon (the one with the ball) and the third person is the Earth( you don't get to hold anything!). If the line between the "Sun" and the "Moon" is at a right angle to the line between the "Moon" and the "Earth" (ie. if the 3 of you stand at 3 corners of a square), half of the "Moon" will be illuminated and the shadow will look like a straight line. When the Sun moves forward or backwards from this postion more or less of the bit of the Moon that can be seen from the Earth will be lit up and the shadow will now be curved. I hope that explains it OK. Try it out of you can - try to use quite a large ball and do it in a darkened room. I think that it should work OK and it should be fun anyway! It's difficult to work in 3D so playing with models can help! It's a tool many scientists use to understand what's going on. Get More 'Curious?' with Our New PODCAST: - Podcast? Subscribe? Title: How to Cope with Being Thought of as Irritating Online Introduction: Being active online can sometimes result in others perceiving you as irritating or bothersome. This may be due to miscommunication, differences in opinions, or misunderstandings. Coping with this situation requires emotional intelligence, patience, and effective communication skills. Here's an extensive guide to help you navigate through such situations while maintaining your dignity and relationships online. Step 1: Self-Reflection Determine if there is any truth to their perception. Consider whether your actions could have been interpreted differently than intended. Reflect on your tone, language, frequency of posting, and engagement with other users. Remember, self-awareness is crucial for personal growth and improvement. Key Tip: Be honest with yourself during this process. Recognizing potential areas for improvement will allow you to make positive changes moving forward. Step 2: Understanding Perspectives People come from diverse backgrounds and hold various beliefs. What one person finds annoying might seem normal to another. Try putting yourself in the shoes of those who find you irritating. By understanding where they are coming from, you can better respond to their concerns. Guideline: Avoid jumping to conclusions about why someone thinks you're irritating. Instead, ask open-ended questions to gain insight into their perspective. Step 3: Responding Gracefully When confronted about being perceived as irritating, remain calm and composed. Acknowledge their feelings without admitting fault immediately. Thank them for expressing their thoughts and assure them that you value their opinion. Key Tip: Never resort to insults or defensive behavior. Maintaining respect and decorum is essential for preserving relationships and resolving conflicts peacefully. Step 4: Clarify Your Intentions Explain your intentions behind your words and actions. Show genuine remorse if you unintentionally upset someone. Demonstrate empathy by acknowledging how your behavior affected them negatively. Guideline: Use "I" statements rather than blaming others. For example, say "I realize now that my frequent posts might have seemed overwhelming," instead of "You're too sensitive." Step 5: Making Amends If necessary, apologize sincerely for any harm caused. Apologizing does not mean admitting guilt but shows maturity and willingness to rectify the situation. Depending on the context, consider changing certain behaviors going forward. Key Tip: An authentic apology includes recognizing wrongdoing, expressing regret, accepting responsibility, and promising not to repeat the offense. Step 6: Seek Feedback Regularly Proactively request feedback from trusted friends or peers regarding your online presence. Constructive criticism can help identify patterns or habits that may contribute to others finding you irritating. Guideline: Choose individuals whose judgment you trust and who understand the nuances of online interactions. Step 7: Learn From Mistakes Use every negative experience as an opportunity for learning and growth. Implement new strategies and techniques based on what you've learned to improve your overall online conduct. Key Tip: Keep refining your approach over time. Personal development is an ongoing journey, not a destination. Conclusion: Navigating the complexities of online interaction can be challenging, especially when faced with the realization that some people perceive you as irritating. However, by following these steps diligently, you can foster healthier relationships, enhance your communication skills, and cultivate a more positive online persona. Watch LE' 's put water in it. Once there was a little fish who lived in the sea. He was having a very nice day and was swimming around looking for food, when suddenly he saw a hook! He was so scared, he quickly hid behind a rock. The fish was very uncomfortable. He wanted to stay away from the hook, but he was also very curious. He wanted to know what it was. So he decided to move a little closer to take a look. He moved slowly and carefully towards the hook, and he could see that it was there to catch fish. He had to find a way to stay away and stay safe. So the fish had an idea. He saw that there were some weeds near the hook. He swam over and hid underneath them. Now the fish felt much more comfortable. He was hidden underneath the weeds, and he could stay away from the hook. He was safe and happy now. Basic Math Fractions A response to the question: I would like suggestions about teaching fractions and division using manipulations to an academically challenging 7th grade self-contained class. As an introduction to the instruction, you may want to remind your students about what we are trying to find out when we divide. For example, if we were dividing 138 by four, we would be trying to find out how many groups of 4 there are in 138, or how many are in each group, for 4 equal groups, depending on the context of the problem we were doing the arithmetic for. (You have 138 pairs of sock to put in boxes, and four pairs will fit into each box. How many boxes will you need, vs. You have 138 pairs of socks to store in 4 boxes. How many should you put into each box, to evenly arrange them.) When we divide with fractions we are looking for the same sort of information. 1/2 divided by 1/4 is really asking, "if you have half a pie, how many fourths of a pie do you have." Since 2 fourths is equivalent to one half, the answer is two. It forces us to think about the fractions in relation to the whole. How about one-fourths divided by one half. That is asking how many halves there are in one fourth. If you draw a whole, and shade a fourth of it, you can see that half of a half is in a fourth. So the answer is one half. Here is where a pitfall enters. Some teachers have taught their students that when you divide you get a smaller answer. That is true for whole numbers, but not for fractions and decimals necessarily. When students divide one half by one fourth, and get the answer "two" it does not seem reasonable to them. Having them actually cut a half into fourths (fourths of the original whole, that is, not cutting a half into four equal pieces) will help them see what is happening when fractions are divided.------------- Thank you for your fraction tips. Now, I also am working with a 7th grade boy who is just this year memorizing most of his multiplication problems. He is working on simple one digit division problems and has alot of difficulty remembering the correct number for the quotient. He gets very frustrated. What do you suggest as the best way to proceed with him. Would it be better to have him work with a calculator or a multiplication chart? Any other suggestions?------------- Perhaps he really doesn't "see" what he is doing. Have you tried letting him use counters to work out these problems? For example, if the problem is 29 divided by 3, have him take 29 beans, or other materials, and divide them into three groups. Ask him how many are in each group. Then have him divide them into groups of three, and ask him how many groups he made. If you ask him to solve very simple word problems (that could be solved with division) while he uses these manipulations, you will also be helping him figure out a context for this "skill" to be used in. Once he makes the connection that dividing is making groups from a whole set, he may have less trouble figuring out a reasonable estimate, and remembering the correct answer. On the other hand, if he is having trouble remembering the quotient because he still hasn't mastered the multiplication facts, your strategy of using the chart is a good one. You might want to have him look for patterns in the chart, and then relate what he sees to models using beans or some other manipulations. He might begin with 24, and look for all the ways he can make equal groups. Then he could find these groups on the multiplication chart. Hope this gives you some ideas to start with. -Gail, for the T2T service Join a discussion of this topic in T2T. [Howard just arriving calling in from outside "hello"?] Title: How to Check if a Copy of Microsoft Windows XP is Genuine Introduction: Microsoft Windows XP was one of the most popular operating systems developed by Microsoft. However, with the release of newer versions like Windows 7, 8, and 10, many users have moved on. Still, there are countless machines running this decade-old software. A significant issue that arises with using older software is the question of its legitimacy. This tutorial will guide you through checking whether your copy of Windows XP is legal or pirated. Step 1: Understanding Legitimate vs Pirated Software A legitimate copy of Windows XP means it's legally obtained from an authorized reseller or directly from Microsoft. On the other hand, pirated software refers to unauthorized duplication, distribution, or usage of copyrighted material - which includes operating systems like Windows XP. Using pirated software can lead to various security risks such as malware infection, data loss, and even legal consequences. Key Tip: Always aim to use genuine software to protect yourself from potential threats and support the developers who create these products. Step 2: Identify Your Version of Windows XP To begin verifying your Windows XP installation, first identify what version you're running. Click on the "Start" button at the bottom left corner of your screen, then select "Settings," followed by "Control Panel." Double click on "System" to open the System Properties window. Here, under 'General' tab, you should see the system information including the type of OS installed (e.g., Windows XP Home Edition or Professional). Guideline: The process might slightly vary depending on whether you're using the Classic or Category view in Control Panel. But generally, you can find 'System' under Performance and Maintenance category. Step 3: Look for Product Key The product key serves as proof of ownership for your copy of Windows XP. You can usually find it stickered on the back or underneath your computer, or inside the DVD case if you bought it new. If you upgraded from an earlier version of Windows, the product key would be different than the original one. Key Tips: Keep your product keys safe because they'll be needed during reinstallation or activation processes. Also, never share them online since doing so could indicate illegal activity. Step 4: Activate Windows XP Activating Windows XP confirms that your installed copy matches the licensed product key. To activate, go to Start > All Programs > Accessories > System Tools > Activate Windows. Follow the prompts to complete the activation process. Guideline: Make sure you have a stable internet connection while activating; otherwise, you may need to call Microsoft's automated phone service. Step 5: Use Windows Genuine Advantage Tool Windows Genuine Advantage (WGA) is a tool provided by Microsoft to help determine whether the copy of Windows being used is genuine. Visit the official Microsoft website and download the latest WGA Notifications tool suitable for Windows XP. Install and run the program. It will automatically check your system and inform you about the status of your Windows license. Key Guideline: Be cautious when downloading files from the internet. Only download the WGA tool from the official Microsoft site to avoid potential threats. Conclusion: By following these steps, you now know how to verify if your copy of Microsoft Windows XP is legitimate. Regularly checking your system ensures peace of mind and reduces chances of falling victim to cybercrimes associated with pirated software. Remember, supporting developers through purchasing legal copies benefits everyone involved in creating quality software. You know how it's liquid and then it freezes hard? Each is a separate evolutionary origin of proteolysis. A venogram is an X-ray test that takes pictures of blood flow through the veins in a certain area of the body. During a venogram, a special dye (contrast material) is put into your veins so they can be seen clearly on an X-ray picture. A venogram looks at the condition of your veins and the valves in your veins. A venogram can show the veins in your legs, pelvis, or arm; the veins leading to the heart; or the veins leaving your kidneys. See pictures of normal blood flow and blood flow blocked by a blood clot (thrombus) . Why It Is Done Venography might be done to: - Check the blood flow in veins. - Find the right placement in blood vessels for medical devices such as How To Prepare Do not eat for 4 hours before a venogram. You may drink only clear fluids for 4 hours before the test. Before a venogram, tell your doctor if you: You will be asked to sign a consent form for this test. Talk to your doctor about any concerns you have regarding the need for the test, its risks, how it will be done, or what the results may mean. To help you understand the importance of this test, fill out the medical test information form(What is a PDF document?). How It Is Done A venogram usually is done in a hospital X-ray department by a radiologist and an X-ray technologist. A nurse may also be present. Take off all jewelry and metal objects before the test. You will need to take off all or most of your clothes. You will be given a gown to use during the test. You may be asked to urinate just before the test You will lie on an X-ray table. A tilting X-ray table is usually used when studying the legs. Safety straps will help you lie still if the table is tilted. a leg venogram, you will be asked to relax the leg and keep it still during the X-rays. An elastic band will be put around your leg or ankle to make the veins of the foot fill with blood. The dye will be put in a vein (IV) on the top of If the veins in your pelvis are studied, the dye may be placed in a vein in your groin. For an arm venogram, the dye will be put into a vein on the top of your hand or in your arm. Once there was a little girl. Her name was Bella. She was three years old. Did you know.......as the comet comes closer to the sun , the pressure of the sunlight pushes the dust , making a glowing tail? The tail of a comet always points away from the sun and when a comet travels away from the sun, it's tail is in front? Comet West's Type II dust tail measured about fifty million miles in length? At least fifteen hundred comets have been seen and recorded in the past two thousand years? Some comets come close to the sun then travel far out into space, beyond the orbit of Pluto? Here is the description of comets. A comet is only a few miles wide, too small to be seen from Earth. A comet's tail is so thin that it is the closest thing to nothing. Scientist believe that some comets may have been broken into smaller comets by passing too close to a planet or to the sun. A comet has an icy core, or nucleus, covered by a layer of black dust. The orbit of a comet is different depending on the comet. Comets that return to the sun at least once every 200 years are often called "short-period comets." About 40 comets have orbits that carry them only as far as the orbit of Jupiter. As a comet orbits closer to the sun, the ice begins to change from solid into a gas. Many comets have probably slammed into Earth during our planet's history. The Oort cloud, when comets roam, is halfway to the nearest star, 3,720 trillion miles from the sun. Comet Halley is a very famous comet. Edmund Halley was the first person to suggest that comets are members of our solar system. Halley's Comet was named in his honor. Comet Halley was recorded in 1531, 1607, and 1682. It last approached the sun in 1986, and will return again in 2061.Comet Halley comes no closer than 89 million kilometers from the sun. Halley the comet is about 10 miles (16km) long and 5 miles (8km) wide. It is coated with organic molecules that make Halley's surface darker than charcoal. [a little earlier, Mark was screaming downstairs and said] Are you gonna drink out of it? This came up in another lesson, so I'm repeating it here. Yes, we all need it, even though it seems elementary and simple. Nominative Case: The category of nouns used as the "grammatical subject" of a sentence. Think of the word "nom," which means "name." So nominative means the "name of something"... whether its a pronoun, actual name, or noun. Subjective just means related to being Nominative. Don't get hung up on terms... but focus on the correct usage. The pronouns in these sentences are used nominatively. I went to town. She went to town. He went to town. They went to town. And so on.... Objective case: nouns that are the "objects" of verbs. Objects "receive" the action. These pronouns are used objectively. Carol gave it to me. Carol gave it to her. Carol gave it to them. Carol gave it to us. And that prompts the question about the "you" and "me" rule. Or when to use the nominative or objective case of other pronouns. Mary and I went to town. Carol went with Mary and me to town. Carol went with her and Mary to town. The test to know which case to use? Remove the "other person" from the sentence. Which sounds proper? I went to town... OR... ME went to town. Carol went with I to town... OR... Carol went with ME to town. Carol went with HER to town... OR... Carol went with SHE to town. Get the idea? Any questions? Title: How to Do a Crisscross Ponytail A crisscross ponytail is a stylish and unique variation of the classic ponytail that adds an element of interest by crossing sections of hair over one another before gathering all the hair into a single ponytail. This hairstyle works well for both casual and formal occasions and can be done on medium-length to long hair. Here's a step-by-step guide on creating a beautiful crisscross ponytail at home. **Things You'll Need:** * Hairbrush or comb * Hair elastics (preferably clear or matching your hair color) * Bobby pins (optional) ### **Step 1: Brush Your Hair** Before starting any hairstyle, begin by brushing your hair thoroughly to remove tangles and knots. This will make the process smoother and help you create a neater, more polished look. Using a hairbrush or comb, gently brush through your hair from roots to ends. ### **Step 2: Section Your Hair** Divide your hair into two equal sections - top and bottom - by drawing a line horizontally across the back of your head with your fingers or the tail end of a comb. Secure the bottom section with a hair elastic to keep it out of the way while working on the top section. ![Sectioning your hair](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/a/aa/Do-a-Crisscross-Ponytail-Step-2.jpg/aid3645870-v4- So you said she does have favorites? Gene expression is the process by which the heritable information in a gene, the sequence of DNA base pairs, is made into a functional gene product, such as protein or RNA. The basic idea is that DNA is transcribed into RNA, which is then translated into proteins. Proteins make many of the structures and all the enzymes in a cell or organism. Most elements in nature consist of atoms with different numbers of neutrons. An isotope is a form of an element with a certain number of neutrons. For example, carbon has two stable, naturally occurring isotopes: carbon-12 (6 neutrons) and carbon-13 (7 neutrons). Carbon-14 (8 neutrons) is a naturally occurring radioactive isotope of carbon. At least two isotopes of each element are known (except for Oganesson, of which only a few atoms have been made). Plain carbon steels are made only of iron, carbon, and undesired elements. They fall into three general groups. Plain carbon steel with 0.05 to 0.2% carbon does not harden when cooled quickly. Welding it is simple, so it is used for shipbuilding, boilers, pipes, fence wire and other purposes where low cost is important. Plain steels are used for springs, gears, and engine parts. Plain carbon steel with 0.45 to 0.8% carbon is used for very hard items such as shears and machine tools. Title: How to Create the Perfect Conditions for Playing Rugby Rugby is an exciting and physically demanding sport that requires careful preparation and attention to detail when it comes to creating suitable playing conditions. By following this comprehensive guide, you will learn how to prepare the perfect rugby field, understand essential rules regarding weather considerations, and ensure player safety and enjoyment throughout the game. **Step 1: Prepare the Field Surface** *Explanation*: A well-maintained natural or artificial grass surface ensures optimal playability while minimizing injuries. *Key Tips & Guidelines:* - Natural Grass: Mow the grass to a uniform height between 25mm - 75mm (ideally around 50mm). Regularly aerate the soil to maintain proper drainage and prevent waterlogging. Fertilize as needed to encourage healthy growth. Repair any bare patches or divots before matches. This is identical to the electron, but carries electrical and other charges of the opposite sign. When an electron collides with a positron, they may scatter off each other or be totally annihilated, producing a pair (or more) of gamma ray photons. Title: How to Check Compatibility with a Potential Life Partner Introduction: Compatibility is an essential factor in any relationship, especially when considering a life partner. It's crucial to find someone who shares similar values, interests, and goals. This guide will walk you through various steps to check compatibility with your potential life partner. Remember that no two individuals are alike; therefore, finding common ground while respecting differences is vital. Step 1: Self-Reflection Before evaluating another person's compatibility, introspect about yourself. Understand your personality traits, beliefs, desires, ambitions, strengths, weaknesses, and lifestyle choices. Being aware of these aspects can help you identify what you seek in a partner and set realistic expectations. Key Tip: Use psychological tests or questionnaires available online to gain insights into your character. However, remember they provide general guidance rather than definitive answers. Step 2: Identify Shared Values Values significantly influence our decisions and actions. Discuss topics like honesty, loyalty, family, career, religion, morals, etc., to understand if your value systems align. While minor discrepancies are normal, major disagreements might lead to conflicts later. Guideline: Open-ended questions encourage deeper discussions. For instance, ask, "What does family mean to you?" instead of "Do you believe in strong familial bonds?" Step 3: Evaluate Communication Styles Effective communication forms the backbone of every successful relationship. Observe how well you both express thoughts, listen actively, handle conflicts, and maintain transparency. Differences in communication styles could result in misunderstandings and resentment. Tip: Pay attention to non-verbal cues too. They often reveal more than words do! Step 4: Assess Emotional Intelligence Emotionally intelligent people understand their feelings and those around them. They empathize, show compassion, manage relationships effectively, and adapt to different social situations. These skills contribute greatly to building harmonious partnerships. Step 5: Compare Interests and Hobbies While having identical hobbies isn't necessary, being interested in some mutual activities fosters bonding and provides opportunities for shared experiences. Moreover, understanding your partner's passions enriches your connection and promotes personal growth. Step 6: Analyze Career Goals and Work Ethics Career aspirations and work ethics should be considered as they impact financial stability, time management, stress levels, and overall satisfaction. If one partner prioritizes career growth over family life, tension may arise unless negotiated carefully. Step 7: Consider Social Circles and Lifestyle Preferences Diverse social circles expand horizons and bring fresh perspectives. Yet, drastically contrasting friend groups or lifestyles (like party animal vs homebody) might create friction. Thus, ensure there's room for compromise and adjustment. Step 8: Discuss Future Plans Long-term plans regarding marriage, children, relocation, retirement, etc., must be discussed early on. Although flexibility is important given changing circumstances, clear visions reduce future disappointments. Conclusion: Remember, checking compatibility doesn't guarantee a perfect match but increases the likelihood of a fulfilling relationship. Acceptance of flaws, willingness to evolve together, and genuine love form the cornerstone of any enduring union. Lastly, trust your instincts - sometimes, what feels right might defy logic! A full-grown moose has few natural enemies. Siberian tigers prey on adult moose. Wolves also pose a threat, especially to females with calves. Brown bears are known to prey on moose, although bears are more likely to take over a wolf kill or to take young moose than to hunt adult moose on their own. American black bears and cougars can take moose calves and can sometimes kill adult cows. Wolverine are most likely to eat moose as carrion but have killed moose, including adults, when the moose are weakened by harsh winter conditions. Killer whales are the moose's only known marine predator. They have been known to prey on moose swimming between islands off North America's northwest coast. And he forgot that it was time for school. Four paper-towel or toilet-paper tubes Four plastic cups Thick piece of cardboard Sheets or towels (optional) Fill each cup halfway with water. Place them on a table, near the edge, in the shape of a square. (You might want to cover the area below the table with a sheet or towels, in case of mess.) Put the piece of cardboard on top of the cups. Stand the tubes up on the cardboard. Each tube should be directly above a cup - check your setup from all sides to make sure the tubes and cups line up. Put one egg on the top of each tube. Think about how you can get the eggs to drop into the cups. What different ways might work? The broom is a tool to help the eggs drop. Here's how: Stand close to the edge of the table near the piece of cardboard holding the tubes and eggs. Use your foot to press the broom's bristles to the floor. Pull the broom handle away from the table about 45 degrees and let go, aiming for the piece of cardboard. What happens when the broom handle hits the cardboard? (Hint: Let the cardboard hang off the edge of the table just a bit, so the broom handle hits the cardboard and not the table.) The egg drop illustrates the transfer of potential energy to kinetic energy. The potential energy from the eggs transfers into kinetic energy after an outside force (gravity) acts upon the egg. This activity also demonstrates Newton's first law of motion (inertia), which says that an object at rest tends to stay at rest, while an object in motion tends to stay in motion unless acted upon by an outside force. The eggs stay at rest until acted upon by an outside force. Gravity is the force that that pulls the eggs down once there is no longer another outside force (the piece of cardboard) to hold them up. 2013 Museum of Science and Industry, Chicago - All rights reserved. To honor the fact that he was entering his eightieth year, one of the greatest tributes to a living writer was held. The celebrations began on the 25th when Hugo was presented with a S vres vase, the traditional gift for sovereigns. On the 27th one of the largest parades in French history was held. Marchers stretched from Avenue d'Eylau, down the Champs- lys es, and all the way to the center of Paris. The paraders marched for six hours to pass Hugo as he sat in the window at his house. This little collection of Legends and Fairy Tales is intended merely as an introduction to general French reading. The stories have been told as simply as possible, with infinite repetition of the same words and idioms to enable the pupil to obtain a good vocabulary almost unconsciously. They have also been narrated as graphically as practicable to arouse an interest in the plot, to stimulate curiosity, and thereby induce the pupil to read to the end. Name: j middle school Date: Around 1993 How do space ships get off the ground? Gravity pulls on any object near the earth, including you, and a spaceship. To get off the ground, you have to push yourself up harder than the earth is pulling you down. When you jump in the air, you are pushing against the ground. When a bird flies, it is pushing against the air with its wings. A spaceship (or rocket) pushes against its exhaust - the stuff that comes out the back. Just as if you were sitting on a swing and threw a baseball forward, you would move backwards. The harder you throw the baseball, the more you will move. In the same way a rocket throws hot gasses out the back and pushes itself forward. If it pushes hard enough, it "blasts off". If it keeps pushing hard enough, it keeps going higher. daniel n koury jr Click here to return to the Engineering Archives Update: June 2012 But to Kurneval this was home, and he hoped that the pleasant air and old surroundings would benefit his master. This can be useful if you want to curate a collection of videos that complement your own content or promote collaboration with other YouTubers. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to add a featured channel on YouTube: 1. Log in to your YouTube account. Before you can add a featured channel, you need to log in to your YouTube account using your email address and password. Make sure you are logged in as the owner of the channel where you want to add the featured channel. 2. Go to your channel homepage. Once you have successfully logged in, navigate to your channel homepage by clicking on your profile picture at the top right corner of the screen and selecting "My channel" from the dropdown menu. 3. Click on the "Customize channel" button. On your channel homepage, look for the "Customize channel" button located below your banner image and click on it. This will take you to the customization settings for your channel. 4. Select the "Channels" tab. After clicking on the "Customize channel" button, select the "Channels" tab located on the left side of the screen. The Channels tab is where you can manage all the channels that you want to feature on your own channel. 5. Click on the "+ Add channels" button. To add a new featured channel, click on the "+ Add channels" button located near the top of the screen. A popup window will appear asking you to enter the name or URL of the channel you want to feature. 6. Enter the name or URL of the channel you want to feature. Type in the name or URL of the channel you want to feature in the search bar provided in the popup window. As you type, YouTube will suggest channels that match your query. Select the correct channel from the list of suggestions. 7. Choose the layout for your featured channels. After adding the channel you want to feature, you can choose how you want to display them on your channel page. There are two options available - grid view or list view. Grid view displays the featured channels as square icons while list view shows them as text links. 8. Set the order of your featured channels. If you are featuring multiple channels, you can set the order in which they appear on your channel page. Simply drag and drop the channels into the desired order. 9. Save your changes. Once you have added and arranged your featured channels, make sure to save your changes by clicking on the "Save" button located at the bottom of the screen. Your featured channels should now be visible on your channel page. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Only channels that you are subscribed to can be featured on your channel. So before you can add a featured channel, make sure you are subscribed to it first. * When choosing which channels to feature, consider their relevance to your own content. Featured channels should ideally complement or enhance your own content rather than distract from it. * Be mindful of copyright issues when featuring channels that create similar content to yours. Avoid featuring channels that may infringe on your intellectual property rights. * Keep your featured channels up-to-date by regularly reviewing and updating them as needed. Remove any channels that no longer align with your brand or content strategy. By following these steps and guidelines, you can effectively add a featured channel on YouTube and enhance your viewers' experience by providing them with relevant and engaging content beyond what you produce yourself. These statements are short sentences describing a small step in a logical argument. The small statements make up the argument, the same way atoms make up molecules. Choosing Between a Digital or Acoustic Piano: A Comprehensive Guide 1. Determine your level of experience and skill. Before deciding between a digital or acoustic piano, consider your current skill level and musical goals. If you are a beginner, a digital piano may be a more cost-effective option as they often come with built-in learning tools and features. However, if you are an experienced player looking for a more authentic playing experience, an acoustic piano might be a better choice. 2. Set a budget. Decide how much money you are willing to spend on a piano. This will help narrow down your options and make the decision-making process easier. Keep in mind that while acoustic pianos tend to be more expensive upfront, digital pianos may have additional costs such as regular updates and maintenance. 3. Consider the space available. Think about where you plan to place the piano in your home. Acoustic pianos are generally larger and heavier than digital pianos, so make sure you have enough room and proper support for an acoustic model. On the other hand, digital pianos are typically more compact and portable. 4. Assess your need for versatility. Digital pianos offer various sound options and recording capabilities, making them ideal for composing and producing music. They also allow for headphone usage, which can be beneficial for practicing quietly. Acoustic pianos, however, provide a unique tonal quality that is difficult to replicate digitally. 5. Research brands and models. Once you've determined your needs and preferences, research different brands and models within your budget. Read reviews from professional musicians and hobbyists alike to get a well-rounded understanding of the instrument's performance and value. 6. Try before you buy. If possible, visit a local music store and try out both digital and acoustic pianos. Pay attention to factors like touch sensitivity, action weight, and overall sound quality. This hands-on approach will give you a better feel for what type of piano suits your individual needs. 7. Evaluate maintenance requirements. Acoustic pianos require regular tuning and adjustments, which can add to their overall cost. Digital pianos, on the other hand, may occasionally need software updates but generally require less ongoing maintenance. 8. Seek expert advice. Consult with a piano technician or musician to gain further insight into the pros and cons of each type of piano. Their expertise can help guide you towards a decision that aligns with your personal goals and circumstances. 9. Make a final decision based on your priorities. After considering all factors, choose the piano that best meets your needs in terms of affordability, functionality, and playability. Remember that there is no one-size-fits-all answer; ultimately, the right piano for you depends on your individual situation and preferences. Key Tips and Guidelines: * When choosing a digital piano, look for models with weighted keys to mimic the feel of an acoustic piano. * For beginners, consider investing in a keyboard with built-in lessons and learning tools. * High-quality speakers and headphones are essential for achieving optimal sound when using a digital piano. * Don't forget to factor in delivery and installation fees, especially for acoustic pianos. * Always test the action and tone of any piano before purchasing to ensure it meets your expectations. During the Second World War, Singapore was taken over by Japan in 1942, but returned to British control after Japan surrendered in 1945. Singapore started to govern itself in 1959, and in 1963 became part of the new federation of Malaysia, together with Malaya, North Borneo, and Sarawak. Political and social differences led to Singapore being removed from the federation two years later, becoming an independent country on its own. [picks up the container and looks inside] In Aristotle's book "The Physics", Aristotle divided " " (everything) into three roughly analogous elements: "matter" (the stuff of which the Universe is made), "form" (the arrangement of that matter in space) and "change" (how matter is created, destroyed or altered in its properties, and similarly, how form is altered). Physical laws were the rules governing the properties of matter, form and their changes. Later philosophers such as Lucretius, Averroes, Avicenna and Baruch Spinoza altered or refined these divisions. For example, Averroes and Spinoza have "active" principles governing the Universe which act on "passive" elements. One kind of air plant is a flower called an orchid. There are thousands of different kinds of orchids in the rainforest. Some air plants store water in pools in and around their roots. These pools can become homes for frogs and salamanders. Frogs usually need to lay their eggs in ponds, but some rainforest tree frogs lay their eggs in the pools in air plants. That way, the frogs never have to go down to the ground.Some plants are made for medicine for humans. In railway timetables 24:00 means the "end" of the day. For example, a train due to arrive at a station during the last minute of a day arrives at 24:00; but trains which depart during the first minute of the day go at 00:00. Title: How to Download Music for Free from iTunes Introduction: iTunes is one of the most popular platforms for purchasing and downloading music. However, many people are unaware that you can also acquire free music through this service. This tutorial will guide you through the process of legally downloading free music from iTunes, providing detailed explanations for each step along with helpful tips and guidelines. Step 1: Open iTunes Firstly, open your iTunes application on your computer or laptop. If you do not have iTunes installed, you can download it for free from Apple's official website (<https://www.apple.com/itunes/>). Make sure you have the latest version of iTunes to ensure all features work correctly. Step 2: Navigate to the Free On iTunes Section Once iTunes is open, look at the top menu bar. Click on 'Music'. From the dropdown list, hover over 'Free On iTunes', then select 'See All'. This action takes you directly to the page where you can find all the songs currently available for free download. Key Tip: The selection of free songs changes regularly, so check back often to discover new tunes! Step 3: Browse Available Tracks Now that you're in the right place, take some time to browse through the available tracks. They should be sorted by genres, making it easier for you to navigate. You might want to create a playlist beforehand to organize these newly acquired songs. Guideline: Always preview a song before deciding to download it. Just click on the track name or album cover to start playing the preview. Step 4: Initiate the Download Process When you decide which song(s) you wish to download, click on the price listed next to the track. Since they're free, instead of charging your account, iTunes will change the button text to 'Get'. Simply click 'Get' to add the song to your cart. Step 5: Access Your Cart & Begin Download To review your selections or begin the download, click on the shopping cart icon located near the search box in the upper-right corner of the window. Here, you'll see an overview of what you've chosen. To proceed with the download, hit the 'Confirm Purchase' button. Step 6: Locate Downloaded Songs After confirming your purchase, iTunes automatically begins downloading your selected songs into your library. Once completed, go to the 'Library' tab found at the top left corner of iTunes interface. Underneath, choose either 'Songs' or 'Playlists' depending on where you saved your new acquisitions. Congratulations! You now know how to download free music from iTunes legally and ethically. Remember to share your experiences and recommendations with friends, helping them discover great new artists too. Happy listening! Title: Create a Note Holding Heart Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through creating a cute and creative note holding heart that can be used as a decoration or gift. This project requires minimal materials and is easy to complete, making it perfect for crafters of all skill levels. By following this step-by-step guide, you'll create a beautiful and functional piece that will brighten up any space. Materials Required: 1. Cardstock paper (preferably red) 2. Ruler 3. Pencil 4. Scissors 5. Glue stick 6. Brad fastener or paperclip 7. Blank notecard or small piece of paper 8. Markers or colored pencils (optional) Step 1: Prepare the Heart Shape Choose a cardstock paper in your preferred color; we recommend using red for a classic look. Using a ruler, draw a large heart shape on the paper with a pencil. The size depends on your preference, but make sure it is big enough to hold a standard-sized notecard. Cut out the heart carefully with scissors. Key Tip: Use a stencil or template if you are unsure about drawing a perfect heart freehand. Step 2: Add Fold Lines On the backside of the heart, draw two horizontal lines parallel to one another across the middle of the heart. These lines should be approximately half an inch apart and extend from edge to edge. Draw two vertical lines connecting these horizontal ones at both ends, forming a rectangular grid on the backside of the heart. Erase any visible pencil marks after folding. Key Guideline: Keep the folds light so they don't show through the front side of the heart. Step 3: Make Creases Carefully crease along the drawn lines using your fingers or the blunt end of a pair of scissors. Be gentle while doing this to avoid damaging the paper. Once creased, flip over the heart to see the design take form. Step 4: Attach Fastener Place the brad fastener or paperclip where the top corners of the rectangle meet, aligning them perfectly. Securely attach it by pushing the prongs through the front and back layers of the heart. Spread open the prongs on the backside to secure the connection. Step 5: Preparing the Notecard Fold a blank notecard or a small piece of paper in half widthwise to create a smaller card that fits within the heart. If desired, personalize the card using markers or colored pencils before proceeding. Step 6: Insert the Notecard Slide the folded notecard into the heart compartment created by the folds. Adjust its position until it sits flat against the bottom part of the heart structure. When closed, the note should remain inside without falling out. Conclusion: By completing these steps, you have successfully crafted a unique and charming note holding heart. Display it proudly as home d cor, give it as a thoughtful present, or use it during special occasions like Valentine's Day, anniversaries, or birthdays. With practice, you may even want to try different colors and sizes to match various themes! Happy crafting! Look I'll see if we can get this to fall XXX. The basic movements are counted either 1-2 or 1-a-2, and are danced with a slight bouncing action. This action is created through the bending and straightening of the knees, with bending occurring on the beats of 1 and 2, and the straightening occurring on the "a". Samba is notable for its constantly changing rhythms however, with cross-rhythms being a common feature.
