EX1 and EX2 on living room couch And has spent most of the time chewing on the food. DHCP stands for Dynamic Host Control Protocol. It does one thing for you. It assigns an ip address, subnet, and gateway to each computer on your local area network. If you don't know the terms I just refered to you can visit our help page. I'll try to briefly go over what an ip address is here, because you need to understand that term to understand dhcp. You don't really have to understand what a subnet or gateway is to understand dhcp. It is enough to know that the subnet and gateway can be handed out via dhcp. An IP Address is a unique number given to every computer on a network. Once again I say unique, because two computers on the same network can not have the same IP address. The ip address generally takes for form of four 0 through 255 numbers which are seperated by periods. A common ip address would be 192.168.1.2. Everytime a computer is turned on it contacts the dhcp server to request an ip address, subnet and gateway. The dhcp server generally provides the next availble ip address. This is more important than it sounds. The order in which the computers are turned on effect which computer gets which ip address. One day you turn computer A on first and it gets the 192.168.1.2 ip address. The next day you turn computer A is turned on second and it gets a 192.168.1.3 ip address. Port Forwarding forwards a port to a certain ip address. If that IP address changed, then that port forwarding configuration would not be setup properly. One day your port forwarding configuration would work. The day it might not. In order to avoid this problem, we need to setup a static ip address on the computer that the ports are going to be forwarded to. A static ip address is an ip address that is not assigned by DHCP. A static ip address is an ip address that has been manually setup on that computer. DHCP actually knows nothing about an ip address that has been assigned staticly. It doesn't even know that ip address has been assigned. So it can very easily hand out an ip address via dhcp that is the same as the one that has been assigned statically. At the start of this explaination, we said that an ip address needs to be unique. When two computers have the same ip address there is an ip address conflict and neither of them can connect to the internet. Let's go back to computer A and computer B. Computer A has the static ip address 192.168.1.2. Computer B boots onto the network and DHCP then hands it the first available ip address. Which it considers to be 192.168.1.2. There are a couple really easy ways arround this. The first one is to just assign an ip address that is far away from the dhcp assigned ip address. If dhcp assigns 192.168.1.2 as it's first assignable ip address, you should assign a static ip address that is something like 192.168.1.50 or more. It's pretty unlikely that you will have 50 computers on your network, so there will never be an ip address conflict. If the first ip address handed out by dhcp was something like 192.168.1.253, you should enter should assign an ip address that is 192.168.1.150 or less. The only thing that matters is that there are no ip address conflicts. Just make sure you do not setup an static ip address that is one of the ip address that dhcp normally hands out. The second and safest way to avoid ip address conflicts it to modify the range of ip addresses that dhcp hands out. We have written guides to do this for each particular router. Well we wrote guides for all the routers we had screenshots of. This method is really simple. We tell the DHCP server to only hand out certain ip addresses. Then we assign a static ip address that is not being handed out by dhcp. Here is an example. We tell the dhcp server to hand out the ip addresses from 192.168.1.2 to 192.168.1.10. Then we assign a static ip address to computer A that is 192.168.1.11. Since the DHCP server will never hand out 192.168.1.11, there will never be an ip address conflict. Any ip address that is higher than 192.168.1.10 would work as a static ip address. Title: How to Care for Your Inflatable Water Slide Introduction: Inflatable water slides can provide countless hours of fun and enjoyment during hot summer days. However, proper care is essential to ensure their longevity and safety. This comprehensive guide will walk you through the necessary steps to maintain your inflatable water slide in excellent condition. Step 1: Reading the Manual Before setting up or using your inflatable water slide, thoroughly read its user manual. The manufacturer's instructions are crucial as they contain specific information about your model, including recommended maintenance procedures, weight limits, and setup guidelines. Familiarizing yourself with this information ensures safe usage and prevents potential damage. Step 2: Cleaning Before Use Always clean your inflatable water slide before initial assembly and after every subsequent use. Dirt, sand, and other debris can cause tears or punctures if left unattended. To clean, use mild dish soap mixed with warm water. Avoid harsh chemicals that may degrade the material. Rinelyour inflatable thoroughly, ensuring all cleaning solution has been removed, then allow it to dry completely before storing or inflating. Key Tip: Regularly inspect your water slide for signs of wear or tear such as small cuts, discoloration, or fading. Prompt attention to these issues can prevent further deterioration. Step 3: Proper Setup When setting up your inflatable water slide, choose a flat, stable surface free from sharp objects or rough terrain. Using a tarp underneath provides additional protection against potential damages caused by rocks or sticks. Always secure the slide according to manufacturer recommendations; usually involving anchors, stakes, or sandbags. Never leave an unsecured inflatable outdoors due to wind hazards. Step 4: Maintenance During Usage Regularly check the inflation level while the slide is in use. Over-inflation can lead to excessive pressure buildup, potentially causing rupture. Under-inflation might result in instability, increasing risks of injury. Adjust the blower accordingly based on the manufacturer's advice regarding PSI (pounds per square inch) levels. Step 5: Post-Use Drainage and Dry-Off After deflating the water slide, turn off any connected pumps immediately. Allow standing water to drain naturally by tilting the slide slightly. Once drained, wipe down excess moisture using soft cloths or towels. Ensuring complete dryness reduces mold growth chances which could harm both users and the equipment itself. Step 6: Storage Instructions Store your inflatable water slide indoors whenever possible to protect it from UV rays, extreme temperatures, and inclement weather conditions. Roll - don't fold - the slide gently when packing away, starting at one end and rolling towards the opposite side. Keep it loosely wrapped to avoid creases and stress points forming along folded areas. Guideline: Store your slide in a cool, dark location where humidity levels remain low. Consider investing in a storage bag designed specifically for large inflatables. Conclusion: Proper care and regular maintenance significantly extend the lifespan of your inflatable water slide, keeping it safe and enjoyable for years to come. By following these simple yet effective steps, you guarantee maximum utility and satisfaction from your investment. Remember, prevention is always better than cure! Title: How to Change the Temperature Units on an iPhone Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through changing the temperature units displayed on your iPhone from Fahrenheit to Celsius or vice versa. This can be particularly useful when traveling abroad or if you're more comfortable with one unit over the other. The process is straightforward and only takes a few steps. Key Tips & Guidelines: 1. Make sure your iPhone is updated to the latest iOS version. Some features may not be available on older versions. 2. Be aware that this change affects all applications that display temperatures, including Weather, Health, and any third-party apps. 3. Remember that after making these changes, they are system-wide and immediate; no need to restart your device. 4. Note that this guide assumes you have basic knowledge of using an iPhone and navigating its settings menu. Step 1: Access Settings App The first step is to locate and open the 'Settings' app on your iPhone. It looks like a grey gear icon and should be located among the other apps on your home screen(s) or in the app library. If you still cannot find it, swipe down anywhere on the home screen to bring up Spotlight Search, then type "Settings" into the search bar at the top of the screen. Tap on the 'Settings' result that appears. Step 2: Navigate to Display & Brightness Section Once inside the Setting app, scroll down until you see 'Display & Brightness'. Tap on it to proceed. Here, you can adjust various aspects related to your phone's display such as text size, night mode, and true tone. However, we won't make any alterations here since our goal is to modify the temperature units. Step 3: Select Weather Option Inside the Display & Brightness section, look towards the bottom where you'll find 'Weather', which might initially appear under 'Other Appearance Options.' By tapping on 'Weather,' you gain access to customize weather-related preferences. Step 4: Modify Temperature Unit Upon entering the Weather settings page, you'll notice several options regarding location services, background updates, etc., but what we're interested in is right near the top - 'Temperature Unit.' Two choices will be presented: 'Fahrenheit' and 'Celsius.' Depending upon current selection, choose either 'Fahrenheit' ( F) to switch to Celsius ( C), or select 'Celsius' to shift to Fahrenheit. A tick mark will indicate your chosen option. Conclusion: After completing these four simple steps, you have successfully changed the temperature units on your iPhone! Now, whether checking the forecast via Siri, browsing social media, or utilizing fitness trackers, you'll enjoy consistent temperature readings according to your preference. Enjoy exploring this new feature while staying informed about global climate conditions! Mr. Ashby seemed anxious to meet his friend before he should come to the dock and learn the news from others. Give me some steak and I'll put it on here. ### Understanding Families of Sets Hello young mathematicians! Today we are going to learn about something called "families of sets." Don't worry if it sounds complicated - it's really not! First, let's talk about what a "set" is. A set is just a collection of things. These things can be anything - numbers, letters, even other sets! For example, you could have a set of all the colors of the rainbow, or a set of your favorite animals. Now, imagine that you have a big set of things, like all the toys in your toy box. Some of those toys might belong together in smaller groups - maybe you have a group of dolls, a group of cars, and a group of blocks. Each of these groups is a "subset" of the bigger set (your toy box). A "family of sets" is just a special way of talking about a bunch of different subsets. It's a set that contains other sets as its elements. Let me show you what I mean. Imagine you have a set of four numbers: X = {1, 2, 3, 4}. Now, let's say we want to make a family of sets that includes all the possible subsets of X. One subset might be {1, 2} and another might be {3, 4}. We could also include the empty set (which doesn't have any numbers in it) in our family of sets. So one possible family of sets for X might look like this: F = {{{ }, {1, 2}}, {{3, 4}}} Here, F is a set that has two subsets as its elements: { } and {1, 2}. The first subset, { }, is the empty set, which means it doesn't contain any numbers. The second subset, {1, 2}, contains the numbers 1 and 2. The third subset, {3, 4}, contains the numbers 3 and 4. Another type of family of sets is when each set in the family is completely separate from the others. This means that none of the sets share any elements in common. For example, let's say we have three sets: A = {1, 2, 3}, B = {4, 5, 6}, and C = {7, 8, 9}. Then we could form a new family of sets that looks like this: F = {{1, 2, 3}, {4, 5, 6}, {7, 8, 9}} Here, F is a set that contains three subsets: {1, 2, 3}, {4, 5, 6}, and {7, 8, 9}. None of these subsets share any elements in common, so they are completely separate from each other. That's all there is to families of sets! Remember, a family of sets is just a fancy way of talking about a bunch of different subsets. You can think of it like a family tree, where each branch represents a different subset of the larger set. Have fun exploring the world of families of sets! Also, the cancer treatments that do exist are not all standardized (there is not one agreed-upon way of giving every treatment which is used each time the treatment is given). Cancer treatments are also not available everywhere in the world. People need to keep studying cancer epidemiology and forming international partnerships (where different countries work together) to find cures and make cancer treatments available everywhere. PREFACE. THE great number of Bible story books that have been published show how many attempts have been made to supply the want which has long been felt of a work containing a carefully written story of the Scripture narrative for young people. In this great work which is written in simple language, the captivating story of the Bible is told in a style that will cause it to be read over and over again, and the thrilling truths and beautiful lessons it contains will never be forgotten. It takes the reader through the Bible in fifty-two lessons, each lesson comprising three readings for each Sabbath of the year, while a series of questions following each reading, help to impress upon the young mind the truths of the Holy Scripture. Title: How to Add a New Language on Android Android devices come with multiple language options that allow users from different regions to interact with their phones in their native languages. This feature is beneficial when you want to learn a new language or communicate with people who speak another language. Here's an easy-to-follow guide on adding a new language on your Android device. **Step 1: Go to Settings** The first thing you need to do is access the settings menu on your Android phone or tablet. You can find this by swiping down twice on your home screen (or once if you have two-button navigation enabled) and tapping on the gear icon usually located at the top right corner. Alternatively, locate and tap on the app drawer and scroll through until you find the "Settings" app. **Step 2: Scroll Down and Tap System** Once inside the settings menu, scroll down until you see the option labeled 'System.' It should be near the bottom part of the list. On some devices like Samsung, this might appear as "General Management" instead. **Step 3: Select Languages & Input** After clicking on 'System,' look for 'Languages & input' under the Advanced section. If you are using a Xiaomi or Poco device, you will find it directly under 'Language & Region'. Click on this option to proceed. **Step 4: Tap on Add a Language** On the next page, there would typically be a heading saying 'Preferred languages.' Below that, you'll see the current system language set up on your device. To add a new language, click on 'Add a language.' Note that depending on your device model and manufacturer, these labels may vary slightly but they all serve the same purpose which is to let you manage the active languages on your device. **Step 5: Choose Your Desired Language** Now you get to choose the language you wish to add. A wide range of languages organized alphabetically by country will appear here. Simply keep scrolling till you reach your preferred choice. When selected, Android automatically downloads necessary resources such as font packs and dictionaries related to that specific language. **Step 6: Set The New Language As Default** To ensure that applications start displaying text in the newly added language, drag and drop it to the top of the list so it becomes the primary language. All apps supporting multilingual functionality will now switch to this setting where possible. **Key Tips:** * Not every application supports multi-language modes. Therefore, only those compatible ones will change based on your selection. * Some preinstalled Google services may still show content in English even after changing the default language due to regional restrictions. * Depending on the size of the language pack, data charges could apply while downloading the additional language files over cellular networks. Using Wi-Fi is recommended. By following these steps accurately, you should successfully add a new language on your Android device. Enjoy exploring your gadget in a whole new way! = = = children/CHIDES_NA/NewmanRatner/Interviews/11/5949DL.cha = = = Is that what it's about right now Ellie? Does it belong in the yellow room? " Title: Understanding Numbers with Modular Arithmetic Hello young mathematicians! Today we're going to learn about some cool properties of numbers using something called "modular arithmetic." This is a fancy term that means doing math with remainders. You already know about remainders from dividing numbers - it's the leftover part when one number doesn't divide evenly into another. Let's dive in! Imagine you have 13 friends coming over but your house has room for only 9 people at a time. When your first friend arrives, you can seat them comfortably. As each additional friend comes, instead of counting from 1 to 13, you could count how many more seats you need before reaching a full group of 9. After seating the first friend, you still need 8 more seats; after the second friend, you need 7 more seats, and so on. Once you reach needing 0 extra seats, you'll know your house is full again. In mathematical language, you would say that 13 "modulo" 9 is equal to 4 because 13 divided by 9 leaves a remainder of 4. We write it like this: 13 4 (mod 9). Now let's talk about a special property of numbers called primality. Prime numbers, like 2, 3, 5, and 7, are numbers greater than 1 that can only be divided evenly by themselves and 1. Non-prime numbers, also known as composite numbers, can be written as products of smaller numbers, like 4 = 2 2 or 10 = 2 5. There's a really neat connection between prime numbers and our new concept of modular arithmetic. It turns out that if you raise non-zero numbers to a certain power, depending on the prime factors involved, you always get back 1. Mathematicians call these powers "orders," denoted by the Greek letter lambda ( ). To find the order of a number, you compute the least common multiple (lcm) of phi values associated with its unique prime factorization. Phi ( ) represents Euler's totient function, which counts the positive integers less than a given number that don't share any factors other than 1 with it. Let me give you a fun fact related to orders: Some composite numbers called Carmichael numbers, like 561, have peculiar properties involving their order. These numbers can be broken down into distinct prime factors without repetition, and they must include at least three different ones. Additionally, their order will divide the difference between the Carmichael number itself and 1. That makes them quite rare and exciting finds among numbers! I hope you enjoyed learning about modular arithmetic and its connections to prime numbers and interesting phenomena like Carmichael numbers. Keep exploring, asking questions, and having fun with mathematics! [MOT still laughing while Chi is talking] I don't want any off your hands. Dogs are sometimes referred to as "man's best friend" because they are kept as domestic pets and are usually loyal and like being around humans. Dogs like to be petted, but only when they can first see the petter's hand before petting; one should never pet a dog from behind. Alliterative names can also help you stand out in the crowd and can make you more memorable. For example, both fictional characters and real people may stand out in your head as a result of the alliterative effect of their name. Think of: yeah, she helps make you know daddy's lunch every morning an'... Sure! Let me first clarify some concepts and then create an educational piece related to the snippet above for grade-school students. A graph is a set of nodes connected by links called edges. In a diagram, we represent nodes as dots and edges as lines connecting those dots. A subgraph is a part of a graph with its own set of nodes and edges - meaning all the edges within the selected nodes are included in the subgraph. Now let's understand spanning and full subgraphs using real-world scenarios: Imagine a school with classrooms (nodes) and hallways (edges). We want to analyze different parts of our school building. Two types of subgraphs could be useful here: 1. Spanning Subgraph: This includes all the classrooms (same nodes as original graph), but possibly fewer hallway connections than the entire school building. It still covers every classroom while having fewer or same number of total connections compared to the whole school. 2. Full Subgraph: Imagine selecting specific groups of classrooms and including all their internal connections; these would form separate clusters. Each cluster has complete connectivity among themselves and may share common external points like entrances, just like interconnected neighborhood blocks. Let's explore the given statement: "If $G'$ is a subgraph of a graph $G$, then either $G'$ is a spanning subgraph of $G$, or $G'$ is a full subgraph of $G$." This statement implies that any subgraph must fall into one of two categories based on node count and edge selection relative to the parent graph. However, there exists another type of subgraph which does not fit neatly into either category, hence disproving the initial claim: 1. Induced Subgraph: When we select certain nodes from the main graph along with all the edges between them, forming a new graph, we call it an induced subgraph. So, when a subgraph doesn't include all nodes ($G'$ is NOT a spanning subgraph) nor keeps ONLY internal connections ($G'$ is NOT a full subgraph), yet retains all existing edges between chosen nodes, it becomes an induced subgraph. Grade-School Educational Piece: Imagine playing Connect-the-Dots where each dot represents your friends and lines depict friendships. You decide to study smaller friendship circles within your larger group: * **Spanning Circle**: Includes EVERYONE in your big circle, maybe adding or keeping the same amount of friendships as before. * **Full Circle**: Focuses on smaller groups who are best buddies. These close-knit circles keep ALL friendships inside but ignore relationships outside their clique. However, sometimes you see unique patterns: * **Special Circle**: Chooses specific pals AND maintains current bonds amongst them, even though not everyone is included and no extra friendships are added. Think of siblings being friends with other kids in the family and hanging out together - they don't bring others into their sibling bonding time, but they also never exclude anyone else from joining separately. Title: Convince Your Parents to Let You Watch Wrestling: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: Wrestling is an exciting and dynamic sport that has captivated audiences worldwide. However, if you're a young wrestling enthusiast, you might face some resistance from your parents due to concerns about violence or age-appropriateness. This guide will provide you with a step-by-step approach to effectively communicate your interest in wrestling and address any potential concerns your parents may have. Remember, patience and understanding are crucial throughout this process. Step 1: Research and Understand Your Parents' Concerns Before initiating a conversation with your parents, take time to understand their perspective. Are they worried about violent content? Or do they believe wrestling isn't suitable for kids? By identifying their specific concerns, you can tailor your argument accordingly. * Key Tip: Make sure to listen carefully during this stage; showing respect for their viewpoint will make them more receptive to what you have to say later on. Step 2: Gather Information About Wrestling Arm yourself with knowledge about wrestling as a sport. Explain its history, rules, and benefits such as discipline, physical fitness, and sportsmanship. Highlighting these aspects can help alleviate fears related to excessive violence. * Guideline: Use reliable sources like official wrestling websites, documentaries, or books when gathering information. Credibility matters! Step 3: Choose the Right Time and Place Timing is everything. Pick a calm moment when both you and your parents are relaxed and free from distractions. This increases the chances of having a productive discussion. Step 4: Present Your Case Now that you've done your homework, present your case clearly and confidently. Begin by acknowledging their concerns, then counter them using the facts you've learned. Emphasize the positive aspects of wrestling and express why it appeals to you. * Key Tip: Stay cool and composed even if they initially reject your proposal. It might take several conversations before they agree. Step 5: Suggest Watching Together Propose watching a match together first. This allows them to see exactly what wrestling entails while providing quality bonding time. If possible, choose a well-known event that follows strict safety protocols. * Guideline: Be prepared for questions during or after the match. Being able to answer them demonstrates maturity and seriousness about your interest. Step 6: Negotiate Viewing Limits If your parents remain hesitant, propose setting viewing limits. For example, agree on certain days or hours when you can watch wrestling. Showing willingness to compromise shows responsibility and respect for their boundaries. Step 7: Reassure Them Regularly Reiterate your commitment to maintaining good grades, participating in other activities, and upholding family values. Reassuring them that wrestling won't interfere with your responsibilities can ease their worries. Conclusion: Patience and persistence are vital in convincing your parents to let you watch wrestling. Always remember to show appreciation for their concern and demonstrate your understanding of their point of view. With thoughtful preparation and open communication, you'll increase the likelihood of reaching an agreement satisfactory to all parties involved. Lesson Plan - Grades: PreK-K, 1-2 - Honey...Honey...Lion! by Jan Brett Set Up and Prepare Be sure to have a place in mind where students will be able to form a circle and do some running. Tell students that they will be playing a game of "Honey, Honey, Lion." This is basically a game of Duck, Duck, Goose with a few changes. Students will form a circle and sit on their bottoms. One student will start off by walking around the circle and tapping classmates' shoulders saying "honey" with each tap. They should pick one student that they will say "lion" to, when they tap them. The tapped student will then chase the "tapper" clockwise around the circle and try to tag him before he can sit down in his spot. Whether or not the tapper is tagged, they should sit in the tapped student's spot and the tapped student should start over with the whole process of tapping. (In this game nobody sits in the middle of the circle. Periodically remind students to raise a hand to show if they have had a turn as the tapper. The current tapper should tap someone who has not yet had a turn. Supporting All Learners It's good to model what a gentle tap looks and feels like. It's also important to remind students not to crouch, but to sit on their bottoms. In 1912, Greece took part in the Balkan Wars, where it gained many of the territories that make up the country now, such as Greek Macedonia and the islands of the Aegean Sea. Greece fought in both World War I and World War II in the side of allies. During World War I, Greece was divided into two countries, the State of Thessaloniki in the north and the State of Athens in the south. Both countries claimed to be the legitimate government of Greece, but the State of Thessaloniki received support from the Allies. The country was reunited in 1917, when the King abdicated. In 1920 Greece expanded again, and briefly reached its maximum size. The territories that the country had gained in Turkey were given back to Turkey in the 1923 Treaty of Lausanne, but Greece kept Western Thrace. The king returned in 1935, and Greece was under a fascist dictatorship from 1936 until 1941, friendly to the Allies, when it was invaded by Nazi Germany. To become fluent readers, children need to learn how to read faster, smoother, and more expressively. Students will be able to work on their reading fluency through repeated and dyad reading. By rereading text, students will learn to read more words per minute. By working with partners, students may learn new decoding skills and will get more practice reading. The more students read, the more their reading skills will improve. stopwatch (one per two people), one race track chart per child (The chart will have a race track drawn on it. The goal is to win the race, which means to make it around the track. There will be three different levels: start, middle of the track, and finish), class set of a children's book about racing Eat My Dust! Henry's Ford First Race, by Monica Kulling, worksheet with three or four simple sentences to read aloud to practice speed (ex. She has made a mess.) 1. Introduce the lesson by explaining the concept of cross-checking. Give an example of a sentence read the wrong way such as; I put my hat on my foot." Did that sentence make any sense? No, it should be I put my shoe on my foot. One thing to remember when you read is to make sure that the sentence makes sense when you read it aloud. Tell them that reading fluently results in reading that is more enjoyable and that today we are going to work on becoming fluent readers. 2. Today we are going to work on reading words as fast as we can. The point of the activity is not to skip any words or read them incorrectly. We want to read correctly as fast as we can. Model reading a sentence slowly decoding every word. Then read the same sentence faster to show the difference and the goal for the lesson. 3. Now, I want each of you to get a partner. I am going to hand out a worksheet with some sentences on them. I want you to practice reading the sentences out loud to your partner. Start slow to make sure you read all of the words correctly. Then try to say the sentences faster and smoother. Take turns and make sure each of you gets practice. 4. Read the racecar book aloud using the shared reading concept. Make sure the students follow along in their copy of the book. 5. Explain to the students how fast fluent readers read and how they should all practice reading at a quicker pace. Now that we have read the book, I want each of you to pick out two pages that are your favorite. I am going to pass out another worksheet with a race track on it along with a stopwatch. The goal of this activity is to see how fast you can read the pages you have chosen in order to make it around the track on the chart. Now boys and girls, you will each take turns reading to your partner. While one person reads, the other will keep the time on the watch. Then the next time you read, if your time has improved, you can move the car to the start position and so on. The car only moves if you increase your speed. I want you to do this activity until you win the race or make it around the track. Once this is done they should then prepare to read the book for the third time. This time they should read the book to a peer. The peer should use a checklist like the one attached to this page to help assess their reading. They should take turns reading to one another. After this is done they should discuss the book. Tell what they liked and what they did not like. They should write a few things they talked about down and turn it in along with the checklists. 6. Once you've won the race on the chart, I want each of you to read the book silently to yourself until everyone is finished. Observe each group of students by walking around to be able to hear the fluency develop with the repeated readings. Look at the charts to see if they are improving their times. Allow students more time to practice reading silently. At the end of the day just before they are about to go home I will allow the children to check out a book from the classroom with me and carry it home with them to read by themselves or with a parent. Hopefully this will be a way to get the parents involved with their child's learning as well as motivate the child to learn. This book does not have to be on the child's level. It can be any book that they would like to read. These books will be different than the books we use in our speed lessons. They will come from our classroom library. Lloyd. Here's an educational piece related to the snippet above for grade-school students: **Drag Racing and Friction:** Have you ever watched a drag race before? It's when two cars line up next to each other and take off as fast as they can down a short track. The first car to reach the finish line wins! When the race starts, both cars are sitting still. To start moving, a car needs something called "force." Force is like a push or pull that makes things move. In a drag race, there are several different forces acting on the car. One important force is called "friction," which pushes against the tires and tries to keep them from spinning. Imagine you're trying to run really fast, but someone is holding onto your shirt and pulling you back. That's kind of like what happens with friction - it tries to hold the car back and make it go slower. So how do racers make their cars go faster, even with all that friction slowing them down? They use special tires with lots of grip, so that the frictional force between the tires and the road is stronger. This helps the car build up more speed before the friction gets too strong and slows it down. Now, here's where things get interesting. Have you ever noticed that sometimes bigger kids don't always win races against smaller kids? Even though the bigger kid has more weight, the smaller kid can often catch up because he's lighter and easier to move around. It's the same way with cars. A heavier car will have more force pushing it backwards because of friction, but it also takes more force to get it moving in the first place. So just like with people, having more weight doesn't necessarily mean going faster. Instead, it's all about finding the right balance between weight and strength. Next time you watch a drag race, think about all these forces working together to make the cars go fast...or not so fast! |On this page teachers and parents will find background information and activities that correspond to the subject on the 'What's New' page. If you have any questions or would like help with other activities please contact us.| Trees are plants and thats where the generalizations stop. Trees share many similar characteristics, but they dont all fall into neat categories. Trees are essentially large plants, but there are exceptions there also. Trees are different sizes and shapes. There are over 20,000 different species of trees in the world with about 800 types in North America. Some botanists use size to differentiate trees from other types of plants. They say trees have a main stem (trunk) that is at least three inches thick and grow to at least fifteen feet tall. The exceptions to this generalization are trees that grow in harsh climates such as deserts or extremely cold and windy environments. Trees can be deciduous or evergreen. Evergreen trees, such as pines, gradually lose their leaves (needles) throughout the year. The leaves are always being replaced so the tree is never bare. Deciduous trees lose all of their leaves every year, generally in the fall. The leaves "change" color before dropping or "falling" off. Lets take a look at the parts of a tree by starting with the trunk and then working towards the center. The trunk acts as a support rod for the tree. It gives it its shape and strength. The trunk also holds the plumbing system for the tree. It supports the network of tubes that carry food from the leaves down to the roots and water and minerals up to the leaves. The outermost layer of the trunk is the outer bark or bark. It can be a variety of textures such as smooth, rough, scaly, or more. No matter how it feels, bark serves to protect the tree from injury and disease. Next in is the inner bark or phloem. This thin layer acts as the food supply for the tree. Water containing dissolved sugars and nutrients called sap, travel down the phloem in the leaves, branches, and trunk to nourish the tree. Stored sugars in the roots also travel up the phloem to the rest of the tree. The cambium is a very thin layer (only one or two cells thick) next to the phloem. This is the growing layer of the tree. Its what makes the trunk, branches, and roots grow thicker. The next layer is the sapwood. It is made up of the youngest layers of wood. This is the layer that forms the pipeline that carries water and minerals up the tree from the roots to the leaves. The last layer makes up most of the trunk. That layer is called the heartwood or just wood. The heartwood is dead wood and is old xylem that no longer functions and is now filled with a resin-like material. The heartwood is often darker than the sapwood. A tree has branches underground. These are called roots. They spread out to help anchor the tree and absorb water and minerals from the soil. Some trees have large taproots, which grow straight down to "tap" into water below. Other trees have lateral roots, which spread along just under the surface of the soil. Heres a quick review of the phenomenon that is so striking and regular that it gives its name to the entire fall season. In autumn, the leaves of the aspen become sealed off the rest of the tree. Deprived of nutrients and moisture, the leaves cannot form new chlorophyll. The old chlorophyll breaks down, and the green color disappears. The yellow pigment, present all along but masked by the green chlorophyll, is revealed as it begins to dominate. The main reason for this annual shedding of leaves is to conserve moisture. Broad leaves, with their large surface area, lose approximately 99% of the water the roots absorb from the soil through the leaves in a process called transpiration. The freezing of the soil in winter cuts off the supply of moisture to the roots, and water conservation becomes very important. If broad-leaved trees retained their leaves, they might become fatally dried out. Conifers have a much smaller surface area with their needle-like leaves and can survive without dropping their leaves. They actually survive by keeping their leaves because they use their leaves in photosynthesis on mild winter days. The seasonal shedding of leaves is directed by a combination of shortening hours of daylight and cooling temperatures that reach a critical point. A deciduous tree responds by creating a barrier of special corky cells where the leaf stem joins the twig. The leaf slowly dies as it is sealed from the trees circulatory system. Its attachment weakens and it drops from the tree. Another layer of specialized cells seals the tiny wound left when the leaf drops. This new layer protects the tree against loss of moisture and the entry of fungi. bark: The outermost layer of the tree trunk. cambium: The thin layer of cells next to the phloem that makes the trunk, branches, and roots of a tree grow thicker. chlorophyll: The green plant pigment that absorbs the sunlight needed for photosynthesis. conifers: Those trees whose seeds develop inside cones. Conifers are evergreen trees. deciduous trees: Those trees that lose all of their leaves each year, such as aspen trees. evergreen trees: Those trees that do not lose all of their leaves each year, such as pine trees. heartwood: Sapwood that has become filled with resin-like material and no longer transports water and minerals. It may also be called old xylem. phloem: The pipeline of cells that transports sugar and nutrients to all parts of a tree. It may also be called the inner bark. photosynthesis: The process which plants use the suns energy to convert carbon dioxide and water into sugar (food). sapwood: The most newly formed layer of wood. It is made of thick-walled cells that transport water and minerals. It may also be called new xylem. Roots are underground branches that soak up water and dissolved minerals that the tree needs for nourishment. stomata: The small pores in a trees leaves and stems that open to absorb carbon dioxide and release oxygen. transpiration: The process by which a tree loses water through stomata on its leaves and stems. trunk: The main stem of the tree that provides support and contains the pipeline for the transport of water, food, and minerals. xylem: The conducting cells in a tree that carry water and minerals to the trunk, branches, and leaves. 1. Have students select a tree on the school grounds that they can observe throughout the year. Visit the adopted tree periodically to observe seasonal changes and record those changes in a journal. Use drawings and words to describe what is seen. 2. Bring in a selection of leaves and have the students describe the similarities and differences observed in the leaves. Have them sort, count, and graph what they observe. 3. Using construction paper or felt, cut out pieces of tree parts and have the students name each piece, name its purpose, and assemble a tree. 4. Assign each student to be a component of a tree and have them collectively create a tree using their bodies. 5. Have a "Treats from Trees" party with such treats as nuts, fruits, spices (cinnamon, cloves, and nutmeg), and candy (maple sugar candy and chocolate). 6. Make leaf rubbings using crayons. 7. Make leaf prints: Place a leaf on a sheet of paper; paint the leaf; place a clean sheet of paper over it; use the side of a bottle to roll over the paper; and carefully lift off the top sheet. 8. Make leaf splatter prints: Pin a leaf to a clean piece of paper; dip a hairbrush or other stiff, broad brush in paint and using a Popsicle stick, scrape the paint from the brush so that it splatters covering the paper; and carefully remove the leaf. 9. Review the terms evergreen (coniferous) tree and deciduous tree. Which ones are more common in your area. Make a display from both kinds from fallen leaves and branches. 10. Using photographs or fallen leaves, identify native trees in your area. 11. Visit a demonstration garden or nursery or review catalogs from nurseries or garden book. Decide which trees would be best to plant at your school yard. Consider the needs of the trees. Raise money to purchase a tree to plant at the school yard and make it a class project to care for it by monitoring its water needs and growth rate. 12. Measure the temperature in the shade of the tree and in direct sunlight. How do they differ. Do this at different times of the day for a school week. Chart or graph your results. Do this again in a different season and compare your results. There is now a floating pile of plastic in the Pacific Ocean hundreds of miles wide. Title: How to Gracefully Excuse Yourself from an Annoying Brother's Company Introduction: Spending time with family is important, but there are moments when you need some personal space, especially if your brother is being particularly annoying. This guide will help you navigate such situations while maintaining harmony within the family. It's crucial to remember that everyone deserves their own space, and learning how to excuse yourself without causing conflict can be beneficial in the long run. Step 1: Identify the Situation Determine whether your brother's behavior genuinely bothers you or if it's just a minor annoyance. If his actions cross boundaries, make you uncomfortable, or negatively affect your mood, then it might be best to remove yourself from the situation. Key tip: Remember, it's okay to prioritize your mental well-being. Step 2: Stay Calm Approach this process with a calm demeanor. Raising your voice or showing frustration may escalate the situation, making it more challenging to extract yourself gracefully. Guideline: Take deep breaths before engaging in conversation with your brother, helping maintain composure and preventing unnecessary arguments. Step 3: Communicate Openly Express your feelings respectfully by saying something like, "I appreciate our time together, but I feel overwhelmed right now and could benefit from spending some time alone." By doing so, you validate your brother's importance as well as express your needs assertively. Step 4: Suggest Alternatives Offer alternatives to ensure your brother doesn't feel rejected or ignored. For instance, propose watching a movie together later or inviting him along when you engage in another activity shortly after taking a break. This way, he understands that your desire for solitude isn't permanent. Step 5: Set Boundaries Make sure your brother knows what behaviors bother you so he can avoid them moving forward. Use "I" statements rather than blaming language, e.g., "When you tease me about my interests, I feel disrespected," instead of accusing, "You always mock my hobbies!" Clearly defining these limits fosters mutual understanding and strengthens your relationship over time. Step 6: Be Honest (But Tactful) If your brother asks why you want to leave, respond truthfully yet tactfully. Explain that certain interactions drain your energy or cause stress, emphasizing your love and care for him despite needing distance at times. Being honest builds trust between siblings and encourages healthy communication patterns. Step 7: Give Space Once you've expressed your intentions, allow enough physical and emotional space for both parties. Engage in activities that recharge your batteries - read a book, listen to music, meditate, etc. Giving yourself time to relax ensures you're better equipped to handle future encounters with your brother. Step 8: Reconnect Afterward After regaining your equilibrium, consider reconciling with your brother. You don't necessarily have to discuss the incident unless you think it would help improve your bond. Instead, focus on enjoying each other's company again, ideally participating in an enjoyable shared interest or activity. Conclusion: Learning how to politely extricate oneself from an annoying sibling's presence requires patience, empathy, and self-awareness. However, mastering this skill leads to stronger relationships and improved mental health. Keep practicing open communication, setting clear boundaries, and giving space as needed; soon, navigating difficult familial situations becomes second nature. In 1741 Duke Ernest Augustus I of Saxe-Weimar inheritated the Duchy of Saxe-Eisenach. Ernest Augustus II, who succeeded in 1748, died in 1758, and his young widow, Anna Amalia, became regent for her infant son, Charles Augustus. The regency of Anna Amalia and the reign of Charles Augustus were important in the history of Saxe-Weimar. Both intelligent patrons of literature and art, Anna Amalia and Charles Augustus attracted to their court the leading scholars in Germany, including Goethe, Schiller and Herder, and made Weimar an important cultural centre. Popocat petl is the Aztec word for "Smoking Mountain". Popocat petl was an Aztec warrior who loved Iztacc huatl. Iztacc huatl's father sent Popocatepetl to war in Oaxaca. He promised him his daughter as his wife if he returned (which Iztacc huatl's father thought he would not). Iztacc huatl's father told her that her lover had died in battle. She died of sadness. Title: How to Cancel Your Weight Watchers Membership Weight Watchers is a popular weight loss program that offers various plans and resources to help individuals reach their goals. However, there may come a time when you decide to cancel your membership. This tutorial will guide you through the process of cancelling your Weight Watchers subscription with clear, concise steps and helpful tips along the way. ### Part 1: Understanding Your Membership Options #### Step 1: Identify your membership type Before initiating cancellation, determine whether you have a digital-only or studio + digital membership. Studio + digital members attend weekly meetings at local studios while also accessing online tools and resources; digital-only members only utilize online features such as the app and website. Knowing your membership type will ensure that you follow the correct cancellation procedure. #### Key Tips & Guidelines * Keep your login information handy throughout the cancellation process. * Familiarize yourself with any contract terms, including billing cycles and notice periods (usually 5 days before the renewal date). ### Part 2: Initiating Cancellation - Digital-Only Members #### Step 1: Log into your account Visit the Weight Watchers website and log into your account using your email address and password. If you cannot remember your credentials, click 'Forgot Password' to recover them. #### Step 2: Navigate to Account Settings Once logged in, locate and select 'Account Settings.' Here, you should see an option called 'Membership Details,' which displays essential details about your current plan. #### Step 3: Access Billing Information Under 'Membership Details', find the section related to your billing information. You might need to click another tab labelled 'Billing' or similar. Check if there are any pending payments due prior to cancellation. #### Step 4: Request Cancellation Look for a button or link titled 'Cancel Subscription' or 'End Membership'. Click this option, then confirm your request by following any subsequent prompts. Some users report needing to call customer service even after completing these steps, so be prepared for potential additional requirements. #### Key Tips & Guidelines * Note down the confirmation number provided upon successful cancellation. * Double-check all emails received from Weight Watchers regarding your cancelled status. ### Part 3: Initiating Cancellation - Studio + Digital Members #### Step 1: Contact Customer Service Unlike digital-only members, those who attend studio meetings must contact Weight Watchers directly to cancel their memberships. I don't think there's any more classes. JOHN JAMES AUDUBON (1785-1851) knew more about the birds of this country than any man had ever known before. He was born in the State of Louisiana. (Rita Bay note: Louisiana didn't become a state until 1812.) His father took him to France when he was a boy. He went to school in France. Little John James was fond of studying about wild animals. But most of all he wished to know about birds. Seeing that the boy liked such things, his father took pains to get birds and flowers for him. While he was yet a boy at school, he began to gather birds and other animals for himself. He learned to skin and stuff them. But his stuffed birds did not please him. Their feathers did not look bright, like those of live birds. He wanted living birds to study. His father told him that he could not keep so many birds alive. To please the boy he got him a book with pictures in it. Looking at these pictures made John James wish to draw. He thought that he could make pictures that would look like the live birds. But when he tried to paint a picture of a bird, it looked worse than his stuffed birds. The birds he drew were not much like real birds. He called them a "family of cripples." As often as his birthday came round, he made a bon-fire of his bad pictures. Then he would begin over again. All this time he was learning to draw birds. But he was not willing to make pictures that were not just like the real birds. So when he grew to be a man he went to a great French painter whose name was David. David taught him to draw and paint things as they are. Then he came back to this country, and lived awhile in Pennsylvania. Here his chief study was the wild creatures of the woods. He gathered many eggs of birds. He made pictures of these eggs. He did not take birds' eggs to break up the nests. He was not cruel. He took only what he needed to study. Golden Eagle by Audubon He would make two little holes in each egg. Then he would shake the egg, or stir it up with a little stick or straw, or a long pin. This would break up the inside of the egg. Then he would blow into one of the holes. That would blow the inside of the egg out through the other hole. These egg shells he strung together by running strings through the holes. He hung these strings of egg shells all over the walls of his room. On the mantelpiece he put the stuffed skins of squirrels, raccoons, opossums, and other small animals. On the shelves his friends could see frogs, snakes, and other animals. He married a young lady, and brought her to live in this museum with his dead snakes, frogs, and strings of birds' eggs. She liked what he did, and was sure that he would come to be a great man. He made up his mind to write a great book about American birds. He meant to tell all about the birds in one book. Then in another book he would print pictures of the birds, just as large as the birds themselves. He meant to have them look just like the birds. To do this he must travel many thousands of miles. He must live for years almost all of the time in the woods. He would have to find and shoot the birds, in order to make pictures of them. And he must see how the birds lived, and how they built their nests, so that he could tell all about them. It would take a great deal of work and trouble. But he was not afraid of trouble. That was many years ago. Much of our country was then covered with great trees. Audubon sometimes went in a boat down a lonesome river. Sometimes he rode on horseback. Often he had to travel on foot through woods where there were no roads. Many a time he had to sleep out of doors. He lost his money and became poor. Sometimes he had to paint portraits to get money to live on. Once he turned dancing master for a while. But he did not give up his great idea. He still studied birds, and worked to make his books about American birds. His wife went to teaching to help make a living. After years of hard work, he made paintings of nearly a thousand birds. That was almost enough for his books. But, while he was traveling, two large rats got into the box in which he kept his pictures. They cut up all his paintings with their teeth, and made a nest of the pieces. This almost broke his heart for a while. For many nights he could not sleep, because he had lost all his work. But he did not give up. After some days he took his gun, and went into the woods. He said to himself, " I will begin over again. I can make better paintings than those that the rats spoiled." But it took him four long years and a half to find the birds, and make the pictures again. He was so careful to have his drawings just like the birds, that he would measure them in every way. Thus he made his pictures just the size of the birds themselves. At last the great books were printed. In this country, in France, and in England, people praised the wonderful books. They knew that Audubon was indeed a great man. SOURCE: Stories of Great Americans for Little Americans By Samuel Eggleston American Book Co 1893 Digitized by Google Available for free download from Google Books Tomorrow, Audubon in the Woods Rita Bay Hello there! Today we are going to learn about something called a "confidence interval." Have you ever tried to guess how many jelly beans are in a jar? It can be really hard to know exactly how many there are just by looking, so sometimes we make our best guess. But even then, we might not be exactly right. That's where a confidence interval comes in handy! Let's say you guessed that there were 50 jelly beans in the jar, but you weren't sure if your guess was too high or too low. A confidence interval would give you a range of numbers that you could be pretty sure includes the actual number of jelly beans in the jar. For example, the confidence interval might tell you that there are between 45 and 55 jelly beans in the jar. This means that you can be fairly confident that the real answer is somewhere within that range. To create a confidence interval, you need to do some math calculations using the data that you have collected. In this case, let's say you counted the number of red, green, and blue jelly beans in the jar, and calculated the variance (which tells you how spread out the numbers are). To find the confidence interval, you would use a special formula that involves dividing one variance by another and multiplying it by a certain value based on the level of confidence you want to have. The result will give you the lower and upper bounds of your confidence interval. For example, if you wanted to have a 95% level of confidence, you would use the following formula: Confidence Interval = (Variance of Red Jelly Beans - Variance of Blue Jelly Beans) t sqrt((Variance of Red Jelly Beans / Number of Red Jelly Beans) + (Variance of Blue Jelly Beans / Number of Blue Jelly Beans)). Here, t is a value based on the desired level of confidence and the degrees of freedom associated with the sample sizes. Don't worry too much about the details of this formula yet; just remember that it helps us find a range of values that likely contains the true population variance. So why should you care about confidence intervals? Well, they can be very useful when you want to estimate unknown quantities based on limited information. By giving you a range of possible values instead of just one point estimate, confidence intervals provide more context and allow you to make better decisions. Just like knowing that the number of jelly beans in the jar is probably somewhere between 45 and 55, having a range of possibilities can help you feel more comfortable taking action based on your data. I hope this explanation has helped you understand confidence intervals a little better! Remember, they are a great tool for estimating unknown values and making informed decisions. For example, if you receive a data package which contains "AAAAABBBB", you could compress that into "5A4B", which has the same meaning but takes up less space. This type of compression is called "run-length encoding", because you define how long the "run" of a character is. In the above example, there are two runs: a run of 5 A's, and another of 4 B's. One symbol that represents this European country well is its national flag. The Italian tricolor consists of three equal vertical bands of green, white, and red (from left to right). This tutorial will guide you through the process of drawing the flag of Italy, step by step. **Things You'll Need:** 1. Paper 2. Pencil 3. Eraser 4. Ruler 5. Using a ruler will help ensure straight lines and even proportions throughout the drawing process. ## Step 2: Draw an Outline Rectangle Using your ruler, draw a rectangle on your paper with dimensions roughly 8 units high and 16 units wide. Title: How to Get Into a Down Stance Down stance is a fundamental position in many martial arts such as karate, taekwondo, and kung fu. It involves lowering your body by bending your knees while maintaining proper posture and balance. This stance provides stability, power, and quickness during movements or attacks. Here's an easy-to-follow guide on getting into a down stance with clear explanations for each step. **Step 1: Stand Upright** Start by standing upright with your feet shoulder-width apart. Your toes should be pointing forward, and your weight evenly distributed between both legs. This starting position allows you to maintain balance and control throughout the transition into the down stance. **Step 2: Pivot One Foot** Choose one foot (either left or right) to pivot first. For this example, let's assume you're going to pivot your left foot. Turn your left foot outwards at about 45 degrees while keeping your heel planted on the ground. The ball of your foot will now support most of your weight. Make sure that your knee doesn't go beyond your toe when turning; doing so could lead to injury over time. **Key Tip**: Keep your heels connected to the floor during the entire process. Lifting them can disrupt your center of gravity and compromise your balance. **Step 3: Bend Both Knees** Once you have turned your foot, bend both knees simultaneously. Lower your hips as if sitting back onto a chair, ensuring that your knees move straight down and do not collapse inward. A common mistake here is leaning too far forward or backward instead of dropping directly down. Remember, we want to sink down rather than lean. **Guideline**: As a general rule, aim to get your thighs parallel to the floor. However, depending on flexibility and strength, beginners might find themselves higher up initially. With practice, you'll gradually improve your form. **Step 4: Adjust Posture & Weight Distribution** While sinking into the down stance, make sure your upper body remains upright. Your spine should be straight, chest open, and shoulders relaxed but held high. Tuck your chin slightly towards your chest and gaze horizontally. Also, distribute your weight equally between both legs. You can check this by shifting your focus from one leg to another - there shouldn't be any noticeable difference in pressure. **Key Tips**: * Engage your core muscles to help keep your torso stable and aligned. * Avoid rounding your back or hunching over, which may strain your lower back. **Step 5: Extend Arms** Extend your arms sideways from your shoulders, palms facing forward. They should be roughly level with your hips. This action widens your base, enhancing stability and enabling smoother transitions between stances or techniques. **Step 6: Practice Regularly** Like any skill, mastering the down stance requires consistent practice. Start slow, focusing on correct alignment and balanced weight distribution. Gradually increase speed as you become more comfortable. Over time, incorporating the down stance into your regular training routine will significantly enhance your performance in various martial art disciplines. By following these steps, you'll develop a solid foundation for executing powerful moves and defending against opponents effectively. Happy practicing! Are you going to make it with me? LOWEST COMMON MULTIPLE HOW TO COMPARE FRACTIONS In this Lesson, we will answer the following: WE WILL SEE that to add fractions or to compare fractions that have different denominators, we must construct a common denominator. What denominator should we choose? We should choose the lowest common multiple of the original denominators. The student therefore must be clear as to what that means. 6, 12, 18, 24, 30. And here are the first few multiples of 8: 8, 16, 24, 32, 40. 24 is a common multiple of 6 and 8. It is their lowest common multiple, which we abbreviate as the LCM. The LCM is the first time that the multiples of 6 meet the multiples of 8. Example 1. Find the LCM of 9 and 12. Solution. Go through the multiples of 12 until you come to a multiple of 9. 12, 24, 36. 36 is the first multiple of 12 that is also a multiple of 9. 36 is their LCM. Example 2. Find the LCM of 2 and 8. 8 itself is their LCM. When the larger number is itself a multiple of the smaller number, then the larger number itself is their LCM. Example 3. Find the LCM of 5 and 20. Solution. 20 is their LCM. Now the product of two numbers will always be a common multiple. The product of 6 and 4, for example, is 24, and 24 is a common multiple -- but it is not their lowest common multiple. Their lowest common multiple is 12. Compare Lesson 22, Question 4. Example 4. What is the LCM of 10 and 27? Answer. 10 and 27 have no common divisors except 1. Therefore their LCM is 10 27 = 270. (1 is a common divisor of every pair of numbers, but some pairs have 1 as their only common divisor. 10 and 27 are such a pair.) Example 5. What is the LCM of 8 and 12? Answer. 24. Their LCM is not 8 12, because 8 and 12 have common divisors besides 1; for example, 4. (To find the LCM from prime factors, see Lesson 33.) Answer. Make a common denominator. Choose the LCM of 2 and 8 -- which is 8 itself. Example 2, above. Answer. Again, we will make the denominators the same, and then compare the numerators. As a common denominator, we will choose the LCM of 4 and 32, which is 32 itself. terms by 8, Answer. As a common denominator, choose the LCM of 6 and 9. Answer. Choose 18. both terms by 2. We choose a common multiple of the denominators, because we change denominators by multiplying them Adding fractions (as we will see in Lesson 25) involves the same technique as comparing them, because the denominators -- the units -- must be the same. For example, In the next Section, Question 4, we will see how to compare fractions by cross-multiplying. At this point, please "turn" the page and do some Problems. Continue on to the next Section. Please make a donation to keep TheMathPage online. Copyright 2013 Lawrence Spector Questions or comments? Blocks are a great manipulative for young children. They can be used to teach many things, but one of their best uses is to encourage children to problem solve. Have your children sort their blocks by color, shape or size. PATTERNING Using the sorted piles from the activity above, make a pattern with the blocks. Example: you had your child sort the blocks by shape. Make a line of blocks, such as; two square blocks, one rectangle block, two square blocks, etc. Have your child try to match your line with an identical one below it. Find a large rectangular block and have your child find two blocks that equal the same size as the one larger block. Look for other blocks that are double or other multiples of smaller blocks. Set out a balance scale and have children put a large block on one side and then experiment finding a group of smaller blocks that will equal the size of the larger block. Tell your children that you need to move the blocks in your room (or house) to a new location. Have your child or children try to think of ways you can move the blocks. Here are some ideas for things children could experiment with using blocks. Set out some squares of flooring. Have your children decide which surface is the easiest to build on. (Wood, carpet, rubber, artificial turf, cork board, etc.) Set out a cardboard box. Have your children see how many blocks they can fit into the box. Weight blocks to see which block is the heaviest. Have children see how high they can build a tower. Give your children a project to build. Examples: a hill, a road, a house, a bridge, a shape, a letter, etc. Perhaps break them into two groups and see how different children solved the same problem in different ways. Title: How to Change Your Social Security Direct Deposit Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through changing your Social Security direct deposit. The process can be completed online or by contacting the Social Security Administration (SSA) directly. This change is important if you want to ensure that your benefits are being deposited into the correct account. By following these steps, you'll learn how to make this update quickly and efficiently. **Note**: If you need help with other aspects related to your Social Security benefits, visit SSA's official website at <https://www.ssa.gov/>. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** - Have all necessary information ready before starting the process. - Keep track of confirmation numbers or letters after completing the changes. - Make sure your bank has updated their records accordingly. Step 1: Gather Necessary Information Before beginning the process, gather the required documents and details needed for verification purposes. You will need: - A valid email address - Your current Social Security Number (SSN) - Bank Account number and routing number - Name as it appears on your SSN card - Date of birth Step 2: Access the Online Application Visit the MySocialSecurity portal on SSA's website at <https://www.ssa.gov/myaccount/>. Click 'Sign In / Up' at the top right corner of the page. Follow prompts to create an account or sign in using existing credentials. Step 3: Navigate to Direct Deposit Options Once logged in, look for the section labeled 'Direct Deposit'. It should be under the 'Personal Details' heading. Click on 'Update' next to it. Step 4: Enter New Bank Information You'll now see fields where you can enter your new banking info including financial institution name, type of account (checking or savings), account number, and routing number. Double check these entries for accuracy before proceeding. Step 5: Review Changes Review all entered data one last time for accuracy. Pay special attention to spelling, capitalization, and numerical values since errors could cause delays or rejections. Step 6: Submit Request After confirming everything is correct, click 'Submit.' Upon successful submission, you will receive a confirmation message along with a reference number or printable letter verifying the request. Save or print this document for future reference. Alternative Method - Contacting SSA Over Phone: If you prefer speaking with someone from the SSA, call them toll-free at 1-800-772-1213 (TTY 1-800-325-0778). Representatives are available Monday through Friday, between 7 am and 7 pm Eastern Time. They will assist you in updating your direct deposit information over the phone. Please note that wait times may vary depending on the volume of calls received. Additional Considerations: - Allow up to three business days for processing. Check with your bank regarding when funds become accessible post-deposit. - Notify banks about upcoming changes so they can prepare their systems accordingly. - Remember to also update any automatic payments linked to your old account. Conclusion: Changing your Social Security direct deposit involves several steps but remains relatively straightforward once broken down. Whether done via the MySocialSecurity portal or through calling the SSA, maintaining accurate payment destinations ensures timely receipt of your hard-earned benefits. oh, you think it's funny to sit on Daddy's heel. [reaches in the container and brings out more objects] Disciplining your child can be a challenging but necessary part of parenting. The goal is not to punish your child, but rather to teach them appropriate behavior and help them develop self-control. Here's a detailed guide on how to discipline your child effectively: **Step 1: Establish Clear Rules and Expectations** The first step in disciplining your child is to establish clear rules and expectations. Make sure your child knows what behaviors are acceptable and unacceptable. Be specific and concise when explaining the rules. For example, instead of saying "be good," you might say "keep your hands to yourself" or "use walking feet inside." It's important that your child understands the rules and the consequences for breaking them. Key tip: Keep the number of rules to a minimum so they are easier for your child to remember. Consistency is also crucial - make sure everyone who cares for your child enforces the same rules. **Step 2: Use Natural Consequences** Whenever possible, let natural consequences occur. This means if your child doesn't wear a coat outside, they get cold. If they don't eat their dinner, they go to bed hungry. Letting children experience the results of their actions teaches them responsibility and accountability. Guideline: Only use natural consequences when they are safe and logical. Never put your child in danger just to prove a point. **Step 3: Implement Logical Consequences** If there aren't any natural consequences, create logical ones. These should directly relate to the misbehavior. For instance, if your child writes on the walls with crayons, have them help clean the walls. Remember, the purpose is not to punish, but to educate. Tip: Explain why the consequence is happening so your child learns from the experience. **Step 4: Avoid Physical Punishment** Physical punishment like spanking may stop misbehavior temporarily, but it isn't an effective long-term solution. It can lead to increased aggression, lower self-esteem, and even physical harm. Instead, focus on teaching your child better ways to behave. **Step 5: Practice Time-Outs Correctly** Time-outs can be useful tools for discipline when used correctly. They give both you and your child a chance to cool down. However, time-outs shouldn't last longer than one minute per year of age. Also, they should be spent quietly reflecting, not playing or watching TV. Key guideline: Don't send your child to time-out as a threat; do it calmly after giving a warning. And never use time-out as a primary form of discipline. **Step 6: Praise Good Behavior** Positive reinforcement is more powerful than negative. Catch your child being good and praise them for it. This encourages repeat performances of desirable behaviors. Tips: Be sincere with your praises and specify exactly what behavior you're commending. **Step 7: Model Appropriate Behavior** Children often mimic adults. By showing respectful, kind, and controlled behavior, you set a positive example for your child to follow. Remember, discipline takes patience, consistency, and love. Stay calm, maintain boundaries, and always show affection towards your child. With these steps, you'll foster a nurturing environment where your child feels secure, loved, and ready to learn. You have anything to go with it just the hamburger? [holding the car and the number up for ROM to see] Where do freshwater eels come from? Aristotle claimed they sprang straight from river mud, and some people thought they grew from bits of skin, or even horsehair. In 1896 an Italian discovered that a tiny 'saltwater fish' was in fact the larva of the freshwater eel. We are gradually learning more about their remarkable life journey, which begins and ends in the ocean. Full story by Paddy Ryan Main image: Shortfin eels The Short Story A quick, easy summaryRead the Full Story Eels look like snakes, but they are fish, with tiny scales on their slimy skin. There are 15 freshwater species worldwide. New Zealand has three species. They are top predators: no other species prey on them. These eels are named for their back fin, which is longer than on the shortfin eel. Dark brown to black, they live in rivers and streams throughout the country. These are probably the world's biggest freshwater eels, and are New Zealand's largest native fish. They can grow up to 1.75 metres long, and the largest one ever caught weighed 24 kilograms. One study found that males swam out to sea to breed at an average age of 23 years, and females at an average age of 34. They die after reproducing. At Lake Rotoiti near Nelson, some are over 100 years old. This smaller native eel is olive green and lives closer to the coast. It can grow to 1 metre and weigh up to 3.5 kilograms. Males reproduce and die at about 14 years, females at about 22. This is a recent arrival, and may have come from Australia. It was first noted in New Zealand in 1997. Its back is blotchy, and it lives only in northern rivers. Eels mainly search for food at night, using their sense of smell, and sensors in their skin. They eat worms, insects, grubs, snails and fish. Researchers found that one huge eel had eaten a whole duck. The eel mystery For centuries, people did not understand the eel's life cycle, because larval (baby) eels look nothing like adult eels. We now know that eels have different forms during their life. But it is still unclear where New Zealand eels breed - it is probably thousands of kilometres away in the tropical sea. Life cycle of New Zealand eels - Eels spawn (lay eggs) in warm seas. The female lays millions of eggs, and the male fertilises them. The adults then die. - Leaf-shaped larvae hatch and drift to New Zealand's coast. - The larvae change shape and become see-through eels (glass eels). Between July (winter) and December (summer), but mostly in spring, they migrate in huge numbers into river mouths. - Glass eels then turn grey-brown and become elvers. They swim up rivers, mostly at night. Sometimes they slither across grass, and they can climb up waterfalls. - Elvers get bigger and become adult eels, living in rivers, lakes and streams. They live there for many years before they breed. In autumn (February-April) they swim downriver and out to sea to breed. He could not leave without saying something, and he felt himself confounded by the archdeacon's eloquence. There was a heavy, unfeeling, unanswerable truth in what he had said; there was so much practical, but odious common sense in it, that he neither knew how to assent or to differ.
